On the perception side, COMPASS injects composition expertise into an MoE backbone in a minimally invasive manner and distills the inferred intent into $τ_c$. On the generation side, COMPASS reuses $τ_c$ as a global conditioning signal that steers the denoising trajectory, effectively converting passive composition analysis into explicit layout control. To support systematic instruction-following composition learning and evaluation at scale, we construct Comp-11, a large-scale dataset with an 11-class taxonomy and reasoning-augmented annotations. Extensive experiments show that COMPASS substantially improves category-level composition understanding and delivers more composition-consistent, prompt-faithful generation than strong baselines.

AI.tldr: COMPASS is a unified multimodal framework that uses a shared expert token to enable both composition perception and composition-guided generation, with a novel dataset for systematic evaluation.
AI.motivation: Current unified multimodal models are unreliable at fine-grained composition recognition and struggle to turn composition intent into controllable generation, lacking a single system that can both perceive and generate with composition control.
AI.method: COMPASS introduces a shared expert token τ_c as a central intent anchor. For perception, it injects composition expertise into a MoE backbone and distills inferred intent into τ_c. For generation, it reuses τ_c as a global conditioning signal to steer denoising trajectories for layout control. The framework is trained on Comp-11, a large-scale dataset with 11-class taxonomy and reasoning-augmented annotations.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show COMPASS substantially improves category-level composition understanding and delivers more composition-consistent, prompt-faithful generation than strong baselines.
AI.conclusion: COMPASS successfully unifies composition perception and generation in a single system with a shared intent anchor, enabling reliable fine-grained composition recognition and controllable generation, representing an important advancement in multimodal composition understanding and control.

title: Singular Learning and Occam's Razor in Deep Monomial Networks
summary: In the optimization of neural networks, gradient dynamics are influenced by critical points that arise from the model's architecture. These critical points occur where the Jacobian of the model's parametrization is rank-deficient, and are the most pronounced singularities studied in Singular Learning Theory. We investigate such points in deep fully-connected networks with monomial activations via tools from polynomial algebra such as Mason's Theorem. We show that, for sufficiently large activation degree, criticality occurs precisely at subnetworks, i.e., at parameter configurations where some neurons are inactive or redundant. This offers a mathematical perspective on the implicit bias in deep neural networks, explaining the tendency of these models to converge toward simpler functions.

AI.tldr: Critical points in neural network optimization occur where parametrization Jacobian is rank-deficient; for deep fully-connected networks with high-degree monomial activations, these critical points correspond to subnetworks where neurons are inactive or redundant, revealing implicit bias toward simpler functions.
AI.motivation: Understanding the implicit bias in neural network optimization - why deep networks tend to converge toward simpler functions despite having large capacity. The paper aims to mathematically explain gradient dynamics and critical points that influence neural network training.
AI.method: Uses tools from polynomial algebra (Mason's Theorem) to analyze deep fully-connected networks with monomial activations. Investigates critical points where the Jacobian of the model's parametrization is rank-deficient, focusing on networks with sufficiently large activation degree.
AI.result: Shows that for sufficiently large activation degree, criticality occurs precisely at subnetworks - parameter configurations where some neurons are inactive or redundant. This provides a mathematical characterization of the most pronounced singularities in neural network optimization.
AI.conclusion: The analysis offers a mathematical perspective on implicit bias in deep neural networks, explaining their tendency to converge toward simpler functions through the characterization of critical points as subnetworks with inactive/redundant neurons.

title: MAS-Lab: A Specification-Driven Validation Framework for Reliable Multi-Agent Systems
summary:
The rapid emergence of LLM-based agentic frameworks has significantly reduced the cost of assembling multi-agent systems (MAS), enabling fast prototyping and exploration of agentic behaviors. However, systems built with current tooling remain ill-suited for reliable, evolvable, and production-grade deployment. In practice, MAS are often developed in an ad-hoc and imperative manner, with agent logic, orchestration, observability, and control tightly interwoven, little to no explicit system-level validation, and development workflows optimized for demonstrations rather than long-lived, governed operation. As a result, behavior observed during experimentation rarely constitutes reliable evidence of behavior in production.
  In this paper, we introduce MAS-Lab, a specification-driven framework for principled development and experimental validation of multi-agent systems properties. MAS-Lab is designed to transform MAS from collections of scripts into engineered distributed systems by separating semantic intent from operational concerns, making behavior and control explicit, supporting reproducible experimentation, and preserving continuity across lifecycle stages. MAS-Lab consists of three layers: a declarative, framework-agnostic agentic specification layer (Spec); a stateful MAS Operating System that provides execution and control primitives plugged-in by design (MAS-OS); and a set of lab overlays with integrated observability and evaluation tools (Labs). Together, these components enable intent-based validation, principled system evolution, and a seamless transition to production-grade MAS.

AI.tldr: MAS-Lab: A specification-driven framework that transforms multi-agent systems from ad-hoc scripts into engineered distributed systems with explicit behavior specifications, reproducible experimentation, and smooth transition to production.
AI.motivation: Current LLM-based agentic frameworks enable rapid prototyping but produce systems ill-suited for production deployment. MAS are typically developed in ad-hoc, imperative ways with tightly interwoven logic, poor validation, and workflows optimized for demos rather than governed operation. This leads to unreliable production behavior despite experimental successes.
AI.method: MAS-Lab introduces a three-layer framework: 1) Declarative, framework-agnostic agentic specification layer (Spec) for semantic intent; 2) Stateful MAS Operating System (MAS-OS) for execution and control primitives; 3) Lab overlays with integrated observability and evaluation tools (Labs). This separates semantic intent from operational concerns and enables reproducible experimentation.
AI.result: The framework transforms MAS from script collections into engineered distributed systems by making behavior and control explicit, supporting reproducible experimentation, and preserving continuity across lifecycle stages. It enables intent-based validation and principled system evolution.
AI.conclusion: MAS-Lab provides a principled approach to multi-agent system development that bridges the gap between experimentation and production, enabling reliable, evolvable, production-grade deployment through specification-driven design and explicit system validation.

title: BV-Blend: Uncertainty-Weighted Historical Baselines for Stable Critic-Free RL with Verifiable Rewards
summary: Critic-free reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR), exemplified by Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO), avoids training a value function (critic) and reduces memory and compute overhead relative to critic-based PPO pipelines for aligning large language models. However, GRPO-style advantage estimation depends on prompt-local (within-prompt-group) reward statistics and can be unstable. In particular, when all rollouts in a prompt group receive identical rewards, the within-group reward variance becomes zero, and group normalization yields zero advantages for that group, impeding learning in cold-start regimes with binary verifiers. We introduce BV-Blend, a critic-free framework that stabilizes advantage estimation by combining prompt-local on-policy statistics with semantic-cluster-conditioned historical moments. BV-Blend maintains EMA-tracked reward moments for each cluster, derives a confidence weight from a standard error of the mean (SEM) proxy, and uses this weight to blend historical and prompt-local baseline and variance statistics into a standardized advantage for PPO-style clipped updates. Experiments on verifiable reasoning benchmarks show that BV-Blend improves training stability and performance, and remains robust in regimes where group-normalized methods may stall.

AI.tldr: BV-Blend is a critic-free RL framework that stabilizes advantage estimation by blending prompt-local statistics with historical cluster-conditioned moments, addressing instability issues in GRPO-style methods when reward variance is zero.
AI.motivation: GRPO-style RLVR methods avoid training critics but can be unstable due to dependency on prompt-local reward statistics. When all rollouts in a prompt group receive identical rewards (common in binary verifier cold-start regimes), within-group variance becomes zero, group normalization yields zero advantages, and learning stalls.
AI.method: BV-Blend combines prompt-local on-policy statistics with semantic-cluster-conditioned historical moments. It tracks EMA of reward moments for each cluster, computes a confidence weight from standard error of the mean proxy, and uses this weight to blend historical and prompt-local baseline/variance statistics to compute standardized advantages for PPO-style clipped updates.
AI.result: Experiments on verifiable reasoning benchmarks show BV-Blend improves training stability and performance, remaining robust in regimes where group-normalized methods may stall (especially in cold-start with binary verifiers).
AI.conclusion: BV-Blend provides a stable critic-free RL framework that addresses the instability of GRPO-style advantage estimation by intelligently blending prompt-local and historical statistics, enabling effective learning even when reward variance is zero.

title: An Agentic AI Pipeline for Appliance-Level Energy Anomaly Detection and LLM-Driven Recommendations
comment: 07 pages, 01 figure, accepted for presentation at the IEEE International Conference on Communication, Computing, Networking, and Control in Cyber-Physical Systems (CCNCPS 2026)
summary: Appliance-level energy monitoring in office buildings produces noisy alerts that non-expert facility managers struggle to use. This paper proposes an end-to-end agentic pipeline that combines deep time-series forecasting, variational anomaly detection, and LLM-based reasoning to generate prioritized, actionable maintenance recommendations. The system tracks seven office appliances using a hybrid Singular Spectrum Analysis (SSA) and Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) forecasting model, and applies a per-appliance LSTM Variational Autoencoder (VAE) with attention to flag abnormal daily consumption episodes. A three-stage LangChain pipeline begins with a Context Agent that always retrieves three core RAG sources (model reliability, hourly baseline, and expert knowledge) and conditionally adds up to three more (forecast context, anomaly history, global baseline) based on event characteristics, capped at eight reasoning steps. A Diagnosis Agent converts the evidence into a structured JSON diagnosis, and a Report Agent renders a human-readable narrative. A reflective memory layer incorporates operator feedback. The dashboard shows real-time 30-minute forecasts, intraday consumption, the previous day anomaly report, and a feedback form. We evaluate the forecasting model, anomaly detector with appliance-specific thresholds, and LLM reasoning on a 16-scenario benchmark including sustained and transient spikes, unexpected shutdowns, and systemic events, comparing five LLM backends under static vs. dynamic retrieval. Dynamic retrieval matches full static retrieval across all backends while cutting average context from six to three-six sources per event. The best backend scores 90.4/100 with a 100% pass rate at a 70-point threshold, and a fully local 7B-parameter model passes all 16 scenarios.

AI.tldr: An end-to-end agentic pipeline combines time-series forecasting, anomaly detection, and LLM reasoning to convert noisy appliance-level energy alerts into prioritized, actionable maintenance recommendations for non-expert facility managers.
AI.motivation: Appliance-level energy monitoring in office buildings generates noisy alerts that non-expert facility managers struggle to interpret and act upon effectively.
AI.method: Hybrid SSA-LSTM forecasting for 7 office appliances, per-appliance LSTM Variational Autoencoder with attention for anomaly detection, and a three-stage LangChain pipeline with Context/Diagnosis/Report agents using dynamic retrieval (max 8 reasoning steps).
AI.result: Best LLM backend scores 90.4/100 with 100% pass rate at 70-point threshold; dynamic retrieval matches full static retrieval while reducing average context from 6 to 3-6 sources; fully local 7B-parameter model passes all 16 scenarios.
AI.conclusion: The agentic pipeline successfully converts complex energy monitoring data into actionable insights, demonstrating practical viability with local models and efficient dynamic context retrieval for real-world facility management applications.

title: The Two Genie Game: Adoption and Welfare in Audit-Grounded AI Governance
comment: 36 pages, 3 figures. Lean 4 formalization and figure scripts: https://github.com/dlewissandy/two-genie-scripts
summary:
We ask under what conditions an agent with a harm-minimizing policy can displace an approval-seeking (RLHF) agent in a competitive market, and when that policy is sufficient to prevent community harm. We use evolutionary game theory (finite-population Moran-Fermi pairwise comparison) to formalize this subject to assumptions of wisher hindsight, peer testimony, a monotone harm ledger, sufficient information density of community feedback, and a finite, depleting resource pool, in a negative-sum environment.
  We show that adoption is favored when the prior distributions on how readily wishers attune to community sentiment are monotone, exhibit endpoint inversion, and have a centro-symmetric pairing property, and demonstrate this with several long-tailed priors (Hill, Pareto, Lomax, Frechet). Where it is favored, a critical adoption level separates communities that drift back to the approval-seeking agent from those for which the audited agent fixes; above that level fixation is the overwhelmingly likely outcome. We derive when fixation is attainable as a bound on the effective (informational) size N_c of the community, which must be small enough to allow fixation before depletion. We present these as Theorems 5.4 and 5.5; the algebraic and finite-grid backbone is machine-checked in Lean 4, with the barrier-crossing asymptotics retained as explicit hypotheses.
  We show that a self-audited agent with a community ledger is not, in general, sufficient to prevent community harm. Sufficiency depends both upon the alignment of the agent's audit with community values and the timeframe over which harm is evaluated. Regardless of alignment, once adoption reaches dominance, the state is absorbing. The same policy that reduced harm under alignment becomes a trap, welfare-negative under misalignment and, even under alignment, one that locks in harm deferred past the adoption horizon.

AI.tldr: Harm-minimizing agents can displace approval-seeking RLHF agents in competitive markets under specific evolutionary conditions, but their adoption creates absorbing states that may lock in deferred harm regardless of alignment.
AI.motivation: To understand when harm-minimizing agents can outcompete approval-seeking (RLHF) agents in markets, and whether such policies actually prevent community harm or create welfare-negative traps.
AI.method: Uses evolutionary game theory (finite-population Moran-Fermi pairwise comparison) with assumptions including wisher hindsight, peer testimony, monotone harm ledger, sufficient community feedback density, and finite depleting resources in negative-sum environments. Analyzes adoption conditions using mathematical theorems with machine-checked proofs in Lean 4.
AI.result: Harm-minimizing adoption favored when prior distributions on wisher attunement are monotone with endpoint inversion and centro-symmetric pairing. Once adoption reaches critical level, fixation becomes overwhelmingly likely. However, self-audited agents with community ledgers are generally insufficient to prevent harm - sufficiency depends on audit alignment with community values and evaluation timeframe. Adoption creates absorbing states that can lock in harm.
AI.conclusion: Harm-minimizing policies can displace approval-seeking agents under specific evolutionary conditions, but successful adoption creates absorbing states that may be welfare-negative traps, highlighting the need for careful consideration of alignment and temporal evaluation horizons in agent deployment.

title: Modelling Emotional Memory in Children with Tensor Networks
summary: We demonstrate how emotional valence influences the order-dependent structure of children's recognition memory: correct recall of a sequence of emotionally-valenced toys depended not just on the valence of a given toy itself, but also on the valence of the toys shown before and after it. Whilst standard psychological models confirm that order-dependence differs across an event (a set of toys shown in sequence), accuracy is low and the model does not reflect how memory for an emotional object influences others in the set. A classical tensor network model factoring in valence is able to achieve a 77.98\% accuracy in modelling the results of the study. While not strictly a ``quantum cognition'' model, this massive increase in accuracy shows the value of quantum-inspired methods for modelling order-dependent phenomena, such as emotional memory. Further, the task protocol we introduce presents a novel, real-world tool for exploring emotional temporal memory in children for analysis using classical and quantum-like models of cognition.

AI.tldr: A tensor network model incorporating emotional valence achieves 78% accuracy in predicting children's emotional memory patterns, outperforming standard psychological models and demonstrating the value of quantum-inspired methods for order-dependent cognition.
AI.motivation: Standard psychological models fail to accurately capture how emotional valence influences the order-dependent structure of children's recognition memory, particularly how memory for one emotional object affects memory for others in a sequence.
AI.method: Researchers introduced a task protocol showing sequences of emotionally-valenced toys to children, then used a classical tensor network model that factors in emotional valence to analyze the order-dependent memory patterns.
AI.result: The tensor network model achieved 77.98% accuracy in modeling the experimental results, a massive improvement over standard psychological models which had low accuracy and failed to capture how emotional memory for one object influences memory for others in the sequence.
AI.conclusion: The study demonstrates the value of quantum-inspired methods for modeling order-dependent phenomena like emotional memory, and introduces a novel real-world protocol for exploring emotional temporal memory in children using both classical and quantum-like cognitive models.

title: TrajRS: Towards Certified Robustness in Pedestrian Trajectory Prediction
comment: Accepted by 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing
summary: The robustness of trajectory prediction models is crucial for developing safe autonomous driving systems. Adversarial attacks on trajectory prediction can significantly impair the accuracy of predicted trajectories, leading to hazardous driving behaviors. While heuristic defense strategies have been implemented to enhance the robustness of trajectory prediction models, these measures often fail against more sophisticated, targeted adversarial attacks. Hence, there is a pressing need to establish verifiable safety assurances for trajectory prediction models. In this paper, we extend the traditional Randomized Smoothing framework to "TrajRS", which provides a certified robust radius for smoothed trajectory predictors. We clarify and expand the formal definitions of robustness in trajectory prediction and tailor the practical TrajRS scheme specifically to "robustness for the optimal prediction" and "robustness for all possible predictions". An extensive set of experiments demonstrates that TrajRS effectively achieves robustness certification for all smoothed pedestrian trajectory predictors in this work.

AI.tldr: TrajRS extends Randomized Smoothing to provide certified robustness guarantees for trajectory prediction models against adversarial attacks in autonomous driving.
AI.motivation: Adversarial attacks can severely impair trajectory prediction accuracy in autonomous driving systems, leading to hazardous behaviors. Existing heuristic defense strategies are insufficient against sophisticated attacks, creating a need for verifiable safety assurances.
AI.method: Extends traditional Randomized Smoothing framework to "TrajRS" which provides certified robust radius for smoothed trajectory predictors. Clarifies formal robustness definitions and tailors the scheme specifically to "robustness for optimal prediction" and "robustness for all possible predictions".
AI.result: TrajRS effectively achieves robustness certification for all smoothed pedestrian trajectory predictors tested in extensive experiments, providing verifiable safety assurances against adversarial attacks.
AI.conclusion: TrajRS successfully provides a framework for certified robustness in trajectory prediction models, addressing critical safety needs in autonomous driving systems by offering mathematical guarantees against adversarial attacks.

title: A Trainable-by-Parts Operator Learning Framework: Bridging DeepONet and Karhunen-Loeve Expansions for Large-Scale Applications
summary: Training operator-learning models for large-scale problems governed by partial differential equations (PDEs) is challenging due to the curse of dimensionality, memory constraints, and limited training data. These challenges arise in many scientific and engineering applications, including subsurface flow, climate modeling, and geological carbon storage (GCS). In this work, we propose a scalable operator-learning framework based on the Karhunen-Loeve Deep Neural Network (KL-DNN) and demonstrate its performance for modeling GCS. The model is trained on a dataset comprising 100 samples of large-scale simulations in a three-dimensional domain with 1.7 million cells and 50 time steps. The KL-DNN method constructs latent spaces using low-rank singular value decomposition of static properties and a nested Karhunen-Loeve expansion for dynamic pressure fields, enabling full-resolution predictions without subsampling or spatial coarsening. The KL-DNN model achieves an average root mean square error (RMSE) of 1.1 psi for pressure (0.04% relative error with respect to the average pressure in the domain) and RMSE of 0.0146 for CO2 saturation (5% relative error with respect to the average saturation inside the plume). The model requires 20 minutes of training on a single GPU, representing a 19% reduction in the pressure errors, 7% reduction in the saturation error, and a two-order-of-magnitude speedup compared to DeepONet trained on the same dataset. These results, along with inference time of less than one minute, establish the proposed model as a practical and accurate solution for large-scale PDE problems, enabling rapid uncertainty quantification, history matching, and real-time decision support.

AI.tldr: Proposes KL-DNN, a scalable operator-learning framework for large-scale PDE problems, achieving 1.1 psi pressure RMSE (0.04% relative error) and 0.0146 saturation RMSE (5% relative error) with 20-minute GPU training, outperforming DeepONet.
AI.motivation: Training operator-learning models for large-scale PDE problems faces challenges: curse of dimensionality, memory constraints, and limited training data, particularly in applications like subsurface flow, climate modeling, and geological carbon storage.
AI.method: KL-DNN (Karhunen-Loeve Deep Neural Network) constructs latent spaces using low-rank singular value decomposition of static properties and nested Karhunen-Loeve expansion for dynamic pressure fields, enabling full-resolution predictions without subsampling or spatial coarsening. Trained on 100 samples of 3D simulations with 1.7M cells and 50 time steps.
AI.result: KL-DNN achieves 1.1 psi average RMSE for pressure (0.04% relative error) and 0.0146 RMSE for CO2 saturation (5% relative error). Requires 20 minutes training on single GPU, representing 19% reduction in pressure errors and 7% reduction in saturation error compared to DeepONet, with two-order-of-magnitude speedup.
AI.conclusion: The proposed KL-DNN model provides a practical and accurate solution for large-scale PDE problems, enabling rapid uncertainty quantification, history matching, and real-time decision support with inference time under one minute.

title: HyphaeDB: A Living Knowledge Topology for Agent-First Memory
summary: Every existing vector database and agent memory framework treats memory as passive storage that agents query explicitly. No system propagates knowledge between agents through the memory layer itself. We introduce HyphaeDB, an agent-native memory infrastructure that reinterprets the Hierarchical Navigable Small World (HNSW) graph topology the data structure at the core of every modern vector database not as a search optimization, but as a communication fabric for multi-agent AI systems. In HyphaeDB, agents are nodes in the vector space with persistent positions, knowledge propagates via a gossip protocol through the graph's neighbor structure with energy-based attenuation, and emergent behaviors contradiction detection, pattern crystallization, and consensus formation arise from the combination of topology, propagation dynamics, and local interaction rules. We present the architecture built on three primitives (knowledge nodes, topology edges, and memory diffs), a multi-layer abstraction hierarchy with promotion via emergent consensus, and theoretical analysis grounding the system in small-world network theory, epidemic broadcast protocols, and swarm intelligence. We provide a reference implementation on PostgreSQL with pgvector and describe a concrete deployment in Swarm-Driven Development, a multi-agent software engineering methodology. HyphaeDB represents, to our knowledge, the first system to combine navigable small world topology with gossip-based knowledge propagation for multi-agent coordination.

AI.tldr: HyphaeDB is a novel agent-native memory infrastructure that uses HNSW graph topology as a communication fabric for multi-agent systems, enabling knowledge propagation through gossip protocols and emergent behaviors.
AI.motivation: Current vector databases and agent memory frameworks treat memory as passive storage requiring explicit queries, lacking mechanisms for automatic knowledge propagation between agents through the memory layer itself.
AI.method: Reinterprets HNSW graph topology as a communication fabric; agents are nodes with persistent positions; knowledge propagates via gossip protocol through neighbor structure with energy-based attenuation; built on three primitives (knowledge nodes, topology edges, memory diffs); uses multi-layer abstraction hierarchy with promotion via emergent consensus.
AI.result: Emergent behaviors like contradiction detection, pattern crystallization, and consensus formation arise; system combines navigable small world topology with gossip-based propagation; reference implementation on PostgreSQL with pgvector; concrete deployment in Swarm-Driven Development methodology.
AI.conclusion: HyphaeDB represents the first system to combine navigable small world topology with gossip-based knowledge propagation for multi-agent coordination, transforming memory from passive storage to active communication infrastructure.

title: ComMem: Complementary Memory Systems for Test-Time Adaptation of Vision-Language Models
comment: A brain-inspired complementary memory framework leveraging fast visual caching and slow textual refinement for VLM test-time adaptation
summary: Test-time adaptation (TTA) of vision-language models (VLMs) is essential for their robust deployment in dynamic, real-world environments. However, existing TTA methods often adapt locally without accumulating knowledge over time, or operating within a single modality without exploiting VLMs' inherently multi-modal nature. Inspired by the \textbf{Com}plementary \textbf{Mem}ory systems of the biological brain, we propose \textbf{ComMem}, an innovative approach that mimics the distinct but cooperative roles of the hippocampus and neocortex to enable effective TTA for VLMs. ComMem consists of two key components: a fast-adapting detailed memory, akin to the hippocampus, that forms a dynamic visual cache from high-confidence test samples; and a slow-integrating abstract memory, akin to the neocortex, that continually refines global textual prototypes. For each test instance, ComMem jointly optimizes both memory systems to ensure cross-modal consistency. Extensive experiments on 15 benchmark datasets show that ComMem significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods under both natural distribution shifts and cross-dataset generalization, offering a promising direction for enhancing VLMs' practical adaptability.

AI.tldr: ComMem: A test-time adaptation method for vision-language models inspired by complementary brain memory systems (hippocampus and neocortex), using dual memory components for better adaptation to dynamic real-world environments.
AI.motivation: Existing test-time adaptation (TTA) methods for vision-language models have limitations: they adapt locally without accumulating knowledge over time, or operate within a single modality without exploiting VLMs' inherently multi-modal nature.
AI.method: ComMem mimics complementary memory systems of the biological brain, featuring two key components: 1) fast-adapting detailed memory (hippocampus-like) that forms dynamic visual cache from high-confidence test samples; 2) slow-integrating abstract memory (neocortex-like) that continually refines global textual prototypes. For each test instance, ComMem jointly optimizes both memory systems to ensure cross-modal consistency.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on 15 benchmark datasets show that ComMem significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods under both natural distribution shifts and cross-dataset generalization.
AI.conclusion: ComMem offers a promising direction for enhancing VLMs' practical adaptability by leveraging brain-inspired complementary memory systems for effective test-time adaptation, addressing limitations of existing approaches through cross-modal consistency and knowledge accumulation over time.

title: A Gravitational Interpretation of Fine-Tuning Reversion
summary: Fine-tuning on harmless data can partially undo behaviors acquired earlier in training. Safety can erode under benign post-alignment updates, unlearned capabilities can re-emerge, latent traits can transfer through apparently unrelated supervision, and related post-alignment fragility appears in other generative settings. We argue these phenomena are usefully viewed through a common training-history lens. Our hypothesis is geometric: large early training phases create dominant behavioral manifolds, while later alignment or specialization phases are shallower displacements from them. Subsequent fine-tuning can therefore inherit a persistent reversion component pointing back toward a witness of the dominant manifold. We call this the gravitational interpretation of fine-tuning reversion. Across our main settings, representational drift rapidly acquires a component along a history-defined reversion direction (v_rev). In our main track, alignment with v_rev rises from cos = 0.429 +/- 0.052 after the first update to 0.647 +/- 0.021 by step 20. Across 24 run-step pairs, every observed alignment exceeds the p99 of an isotropic activation-space null. We demonstrate that selectively blocking motion along v_rev changes the final alignment at T=100 from 0.648 +/- 0.009 to -0.211 +/- 0.021 and reduces harmfulness from 19.0% +/- 4.0% to 8.5% +/- 1.5% with little task cost. These results support v_rev as a causally relevant mediator of early post-alignment reversion in our setup. Importantly, we do not claim that v_rev is the unique safety direction, nor that the dominant manifold is directly observed; rather, we identify a robust, history-defined direction that explains and partially controls early reversion dynamics.

AI.tldr: Fine-tuning can undo safety alignment, causing reversion to earlier unsafe behaviors due to persistent structural patterns from early training.
AI.motivation: Safety alignment in AI models can degrade during subsequent fine-tuning, where supposedly removed harmful behaviors re-emerge, indicating fragility in post-alignment updates that needs better understanding.
AI.method: Proposed a geometric "gravitational" interpretation where early training creates dominant behavioral manifolds, later alignment creates shallow displacements, and fine-tuning can revert to the dominant manifold. Measured alignment with a history-defined reversion direction (v_rev) and tested selective blocking of motion along this direction.
AI.result: Found rapid representational drift along v_rev direction (cosine alignment from 0.429 to 0.647 by step 20). Blocking v_rev motion reduced final alignment to -0.211 and decreased harmfulness from 19.0% to 8.5% with minimal task performance cost.
AI.conclusion: Early training creates persistent behavioral structures that cause reversion during fine-tuning, and v_rev is a robust, history-defined direction that explains and partially controls this reversion dynamics, though not necessarily the unique safety direction.

title: Multi-Agent Routing as Set-Valued Prediction: A WildChat Benchmark and Cost-Aware Evaluation
comment: 9 pages, 8 figures. Under review at ACM RecSys 2026
summary: Tool and agent routing from natural-language prompts is naturally a set-valued prediction problem: a single query may require multiple agents, while over-selection increases execution cost. The benchmark introduced here is derived from WildChat and contains 3,000 prompts over a fixed 12-agent catalog, with AI-assisted heuristic labels under a fixed schema and controlled rebalancing for multi-label evaluation. The evaluation protocol combines set-level metrics (Precision, Recall, F1, Jaccard, and Exact Match), latency, an execution-oriented capability-coverage simulation, and a constrained weighted-routing setting based on ordinal agent-cost tiers. Compared methods include nearest-neighbor matching, linear multilabel classification, dependency-aware baselines, a fine-tuned encoder, deterministic weighted post-scoring via Weighted Agent Routing (WAR), and a zero-shot LLM baseline. Results show that supervised routers substantially outperform nearest-neighbor and zero-shot LLM routing. The fine-tuned encoder achieves the strongest unconstrained set accuracy, while the linear multilabel model provides the strongest practical baseline. In the constrained setting, the weighted routing layer improves utility when applied on top of strong supervised scorers, with the largest gain observed for Encoder+WAR. Overall, the benchmark and evaluation protocol support reproducible study of accuracy-cost trade-offs in fixed-catalog multi-agent routing.

AI.tldr: A new benchmark for multi-agent routing from natural language prompts with evaluation of accuracy-cost trade-offs, showing supervised routers outperform naive baselines and weighted routing improves utility in constrained settings.
AI.motivation: Tool and agent routing from natural language prompts is inherently a set-valued prediction problem where single queries may require multiple agents, but over-selection increases execution costs. There's a need for benchmarks that evaluate accuracy-cost trade-offs in fixed-catalog multi-agent routing.
AI.method: Created a benchmark from WildChat with 3,000 prompts over 12-agent catalog, using AI-assisted heuristic labels with controlled rebalancing for multi-label evaluation. Evaluation combines set-level metrics (Precision, Recall, F1, Jaccard, Exact Match), latency, execution-oriented capability-coverage simulation, and constrained weighted-routing with ordinal agent-cost tiers. Compared methods include nearest-neighbor, linear multilabel classification, dependency-aware baselines, fine-tuned encoder, Weighted Agent Routing (WAR), and zero-shot LLM baseline.
AI.result: Supervised routers substantially outperform nearest-neighbor and zero-shot LLM routing. The fine-tuned encoder achieves strongest unconstrained set accuracy, while linear multilabel model provides strongest practical baseline. In constrained settings, weighted routing layer (WAR) improves utility when applied to strong supervised scorers, with largest gain for Encoder+WAR combination.
AI.conclusion: The benchmark and evaluation protocol support reproducible study of accuracy-cost trade-offs in fixed-catalog multi-agent routing, providing a framework for comparing routing methods and demonstrating that supervised approaches with weighted optimization outperform simpler baselines.

title: Agentic Abstention: Do Agents Know When to Stop Instead of Act?
summary: LLM agents are expected to act over multiple turns, using search, browsing interfaces, and terminal tools to complete user goals. Yet not every goal is well specified or achievable in the available environment. In such cases, a reliable agent should recognize that further interaction is unlikely to help and abstain from additional tool calls. We define Agentic Abstention, the problem of deciding when an agent should stop acting under uncertainty. Unlike standard LLM abstention, which is usually evaluated as a single-turn answer-or-abstain decision, agentic abstention is a sequential decision problem: an agent can answer, abstain, or gather more information at each turn, and the need to abstain may only become clear after interacting with the environment. We study this problem across web shopping, terminal environments, and question answering, evaluating 13 LLM-as-agent systems and 2 agent scaffolds on more than 28,000 tasks. Our results show that the main challenge is not only whether agents can abstain, but also when they abstain. Some agents never abstain when they should, while others do so only after many unnecessary interactions. This gap is especially large on tasks where the instruction appears feasible until the environment reveals otherwise (e.g., no valid result matches the instruction). We further find that model scale, reasoning, and agent scaffolding affect abstention in different ways, where larger or more capable models sometimes perform worse at timely abstention. Finally, we introduce CONVOLVE, a context engineering method for improving agentic abstention that distills full interaction trajectories into reusable stopping rules. On WebShop, CONVOLVE substantially improves timely abstention without updating model parameters, raising Llama-3.3-70B's timely recall rate from 26.7 to 57.4. Our dataset and code are available at https://lhannnn.github.io/agentic-abstention

AI.tldr: This paper introduces "Agentic Abstention" - the problem of when LLM agents should stop acting in multi-turn environments when goals are unclear or unachievable, and proposes CONVOLVE, a method to improve timely abstention using distilled interaction rules.
AI.motivation: LLM agents often operate in multi-turn environments using tools like search, browsing, and terminals, but not all user goals are well-specified or achievable. Current agents struggle to recognize when further interaction is futile and should abstain, leading to unnecessary tool calls and interactions.
AI.method: The paper studies agentic abstention across web shopping, terminal environments, and question answering, evaluating 13 LLM-as-agent systems and 2 agent scaffolds on 28,000+ tasks. They introduce CONVOLVE, a context engineering method that distills full interaction trajectories into reusable stopping rules to improve abstention timing without updating model parameters.
AI.result: Results show the main challenge is not just whether agents can abstain, but when they abstain. Some agents never abstain when needed, while others do so only after many unnecessary interactions. Model scale, reasoning, and scaffolding affect abstention differently, with larger models sometimes performing worse at timely abstention. CONVOLVE improved timely recall rate from 26.7 to 57.4 on WebShop with Llama-3.3-70B.
AI.conclusion: Agentic abstention is a crucial sequential decision problem for LLM agents, distinct from single-turn abstention. The paper provides a comprehensive study showing current limitations and demonstrates that context engineering methods like CONVOLVE can significantly improve timely abstention without model updates.

title: NIVA: A Multimodal Foundation Model for Actionable Earth System Intelligence
summary: Recent advances in AI-driven weather and climate modeling have improved forecast skill while reducing computational cost. However, existing data-driven approaches are limited in their ability to model coupled Earth system dynamics, which is required for extending predictability beyond the ~2-week horizon. To address this, we introduce NIVA, a multimodal foundation model designed to learn unified representations across Earth system components. While the full framework targets atmosphere, ocean, ice, and land interactions, we focus here on a two-modality setting (ocean and atmosphere) as a controlled proof of concept to evaluate whether foundation models can learn coupled dynamics. Trained on large-scale Earth system simulations, NIVA learns physically meaningful cross-modal structure, providing a foundation for subseasonal-to-seasonal prediction. As initial validation, we show that NIVA captures key modes of climate variability through accurate prediction of major climate indices.

AI.tldr: NIVA is a multimodal foundation model that learns unified representations across Earth system components to address limitations in modeling coupled dynamics for extended weather and climate predictability.
AI.motivation: Existing AI-driven weather/climate models have improved forecast skill but are limited in modeling coupled Earth system dynamics, which is essential for extending predictability beyond ~2 weeks. Current data-driven approaches struggle with capturing interactions between atmosphere, ocean, ice, and land components.
AI.method: NIVA is a multimodal foundation model trained on large-scale Earth system simulations. The paper focuses on a two-modality proof-of-concept (ocean and atmosphere) to evaluate if foundation models can learn coupled dynamics. The model learns physically meaningful cross-modal representations across Earth system components.
AI.result: NIVA successfully learns physically meaningful cross-modal structure and captures key modes of climate variability. As initial validation, the model accurately predicts major climate indices, demonstrating its potential for subseasonal-to-seasonal prediction.
AI.conclusion: NIVA provides a foundation for modeling coupled Earth system dynamics, addressing the limitations of current data-driven approaches for extended predictability. The successful two-modality proof-of-concept demonstrates that foundation models can effectively learn coupled dynamics across Earth system components.

title: Mixture of Debaters: Learn to Debate at Architectural Level in Multi-Agent Reasoning
summary: Existing multi-agent debate frameworks suffer from two critical limitations: they rely on static architectures where agent roles and coordination patterns are fixed at design time, and they require instantiating multiple model copies, incurring substantial computational overhead. We propose Mixture of Debaters (MoD), a unified framework that enables dynamic self-debate within a single model by leveraging the Mixture-of-Experts paradigm. We address three key challenges in adapting MoE for dialectical reasoning: (1) dual-routing that decouples role allocation from process flow, dynamically determining when to debate versus when to synthesize; (2) momentum switching that smooths token-level routing with local context, reducing expert-switch jitter; and (3) unified self-debate that encapsulates diverse debating personas into lightweight expert modules, eliminating inter-agent communication while preserving behavioral diversity. Extensive experiments on multimodal benchmarks demonstrate that MoD outperforms both single-model baselines and conventional multi-agent systems, achieving superior accuracy with 3.7x lower latency and 87% reduction in token consumption.The source code can be accessed at https://github.com/YongLD/MoD.

AI.tldr: Mixture of Debaters (MoD) is a dynamic self-debate framework using Mixture-of-Experts that enables internal multi-agent debate within a single model, reducing computational overhead while improving performance.
AI.motivation: Existing multi-agent debate frameworks have two major limitations: static architectures with fixed agent roles/coordination patterns that can't adapt dynamically, and high computational overhead from requiring multiple model copies.
AI.method: MoD leverages Mixture-of-Experts paradigm with three innovations: (1) dual-routing to decouple role allocation from process flow, (2) momentum switching to smooth token-level routing with local context, and (3) unified self-debate that encapsulates diverse debating personas into lightweight expert modules within a single model.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on multimodal benchmarks show MoD outperforms single-model baselines and conventional multi-agent systems, achieving superior accuracy with 3.7x lower latency and 87% reduction in token consumption.
AI.conclusion: MoD successfully addresses the limitations of static multi-agent debate frameworks by enabling dynamic self-debate within a single model, achieving better performance with significantly reduced computational costs.

title: Agent Safety Is Action Alignment
summary: Large language models increasingly act as agents: they call tools, move money, delete records, and send messages on a user's behalf. To keep them safe, practitioners imported the chatbot-era recipe (train the model to refuse unsafe inputs) into the agentic setting, and treat the resulting capability loss as a manageable ``alignment tax.'' We argue this is a \emph{category error}. Refusal is a primitive for \emph{content safety}, where the harm is in the model's output and is therefore a learnable function of it. Agentic harm is different in kind: it lies not in any output but in the relation between the authority an action exercises and the authority the user granted, which is absent from the text the model sees. Importing content-safety methods into this regime does not trade capability for safety; it pays capability and buys negative security. We support this with three lines of evidence spanning the autonomy spectrum: defense-trained models learn surface patterns rather than intent; the same training collapses multi-step agents before any threat appears while leaving them exploitable; and even undefended frontier models exceed granted authority under ordinary use. We conclude that action safety cannot be installed in weights. It must be expressed as \emph{least privilege}, enforced \emph{outside} the model at the action boundary, and evaluated as \emph{action alignment} (a relational, deployment-conditioned property) rather than a refusal score.

AI.tldr: Current content-safety approaches (like refusal training) are ineffective for LLM agents because agentic harm stems from actions exceeding granted authority, not harmful outputs. Action safety requires external least-privilege enforcement at action boundaries, not model weights.
AI.motivation: As LLMs increasingly function as agents performing real-world actions (calling tools, moving money, deleting records), practitioners have incorrectly applied chatbot-era safety methods (refusal training) to agent settings, treating capability loss as "alignment tax." This is problematic because agentic harm is fundamentally different from content harm.
AI.method: The paper presents three lines of evidence across the autonomy spectrum: 1) defense-trained models learn surface patterns rather than understanding intent, 2) safety training collapses multi-step agents before threats appear while leaving them vulnerable, and 3) even undefended frontier models exceed granted authority under normal use. The analysis distinguishes content safety (harm in outputs) from action safety (harm in exceeding granted authority).
AI.result: Current refusal-based safety approaches fail for agentic settings: they degrade capability without providing real safety, create exploitable vulnerabilities, and models still exceed authority boundaries. The evidence shows that action safety cannot be achieved through model training alone.
AI.conclusion: Action safety requires a fundamentally different approach: implementing least privilege principles, enforcing safety outside the model at action boundaries, and evaluating safety as "action alignment" (a relational, deployment-conditioned property) rather than refusal scores. Safety must be architectural, not merely learned in model weights.

title: Improving Coherence in Hierarchical Time Series Forecasting using Structured Temporal Fusion
comment: 7 pages, 2 figures. Preprint. Source files included
summary:
In many real-world applications, such as retail sales, energy usage, and supply chain planning, forecasting is performed across hierarchical structures. These structures often represent aggregations (e.g., products to categories to regions), where forecasts must not only be accurate but also coherent, meaning that lower-level predictions sum correctly to higher-level forecasts. Traditional statistical methods, such as Bottom-Up and MinT, enforce coherence through post-processing but fail to model complex nonlinear temporal dependencies and covariate interactions.
  We propose Hierarchical Temporal Fusion (HTF), a novel extension of the Temporal Fusion Transformer (TFT) that integrates structured hierarchical embeddings with a coherence-aware loss function to ensure consistent forecasts across all levels of a hierarchy. Rather than applying reconciliation after forecasting, HTF embeds coherence directly into the training objective. The coherence loss penalizes the difference between aggregated child forecasts and their corresponding parent forecasts during training, enabling the model to learn both temporal dynamics and structural consistency simultaneously.
  We evaluate HTF on two publicly available benchmark datasets: the M5 Walmart forecasting dataset and a publicly available hierarchical energy consumption dataset. Results demonstrate that HTF substantially reduces forecast incoherence while improving forecasting accuracy compared with classical reconciliation methods and deep learning baselines. In addition, attention visualization and embedding analysis provide insight into how temporal and structural information contribute to hierarchical forecasting performance.

AI.tldr: HTF extends Temporal Fusion Transformer with hierarchical embeddings and coherence loss to produce accurate and coherent hierarchical forecasts without post-processing reconciliation.
AI.motivation: Hierarchical forecasting is critical in applications like retail, energy, and supply chain, where forecasts must be coherent across aggregation levels. Traditional methods enforce coherence through post-processing but fail to model complex nonlinear temporal dependencies and covariate interactions effectively.
AI.method: Hierarchical Temporal Fusion (HTF) integrates structured hierarchical embeddings with a coherence-aware loss function that penalizes differences between aggregated child forecasts and parent forecasts during training, embedding coherence directly into the learning objective rather than applying reconciliation after forecasting.
AI.result: HTF substantially reduces forecast incoherence while improving forecasting accuracy compared to classical reconciliation methods (Bottom-Up, MinT) and deep learning baselines on both the M5 Walmart dataset and hierarchical energy consumption dataset.
AI.conclusion: HTF successfully integrates hierarchical structure into deep learning forecasting by learning both temporal dynamics and structural consistency simultaneously, providing more accurate and coherent hierarchical forecasts than post-processing approaches.

title: Persona-Trained Monte Carlo: Estimating Market-Outcome Distributions via Swarms of Persona-Conditioned Neural Policy Bots in a Limit Order Book
comment: 58 pages, 3 figures, 9 tables, 3 algorithms. Survey and proposed framework; no implementation or empirical results
summary: We propose Persona-Trained Monte Carlo (PTMC), a method for estimating distributions of market-outcome statistics by repeatedly simulating limit-order-book interaction among swarms of persona-conditioned neural-policy trading bots. Each run instantiates many bots sharing one trained policy network but conditioned on heterogeneous, individually sampled persona parameters drawn from a learned trader-heterogeneity distribution; the bots interact in a continuous double auction, and the resulting price path is one Monte Carlo sample. Repeating this over independent persona-population draws yields an ensemble from which a target market statistic is estimated. Randomness enters through persona draws, within-run action sampling, and optional exogenous shocks, not solely through price as in classical Monte Carlo. We distinguish PTMC from adjacent paradigms, including classical Monte Carlo, hand-coded agent-based models, single-agent reinforcement learning, and large-language-model-based generative agents. To justify the design, we survey cross-disciplinary foundations -- agent-based computational economics, market microstructure, behavioral finance, deep reinforcement learning, generative/LLM-based agents, news-driven trading, systemic risk, econophysics, and game theory -- connecting each literature to a specific design choice in the policy network, training data, or validation protocol. We formalize the PTMC estimator and its convergence properties, specify a candidate bot architecture and training objective, and propose a four-level validation methodology: stylized-fact matching, microstructure- and agent-level checks, and historical stress-test comparison against a zero-intelligence baseline. The framework is proposed but not implemented: we contribute a formal estimator, a cross-disciplinary design justification, and a validation roadmap, and conclude with open research questions.

AI.tldr: PTMC is a proposed method that uses persona-conditioned neural-policy trading bots in limit-order-book simulations to estimate distributions of market-outcome statistics through Monte Carlo sampling.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to address the limitations of existing approaches for market outcome estimation by combining insights from multiple disciplines to create a more realistic simulation of heterogeneous trader behavior in financial markets.
AI.method: Repeatedly simulate limit-order-book interactions among swarms of persona-conditioned neural-policy trading bots, where each bot shares a trained policy network but has individually sampled persona parameters from a learned heterogeneity distribution, generating Monte Carlo samples of price paths.
AI.result: The paper presents a formal PTMC estimator with convergence properties, specifies candidate bot architecture and training objectives, and proposes a four-level validation methodology, but the framework is proposed rather than implemented.
AI.conclusion: PTMC offers a novel approach to market outcome estimation that integrates insights from multiple disciplines, providing a formal estimator, cross-disciplinary design justification, and validation roadmap, with future work needed for implementation and addressing open research questions.

title: Self-Supervised Theorem Discovery in a Formal Axiomatic System
summary: Recent artificial intelligence (AI) systems have shown remarkable progress in mathematical reasoning. Many existing approaches, including large language models (LLMs), draw on human prior knowledge in the form of mathematical text, code, or theorem libraries. Although these approaches are highly effective in practice, it remains an open question whether an agent can autonomously discover useful theorems without such human priors. We study this question in a formal axiomatic system by developing an agent that starts from axioms and inference rules alone and gradually grows a library of useful theorems. Concretely, we propose a self-supervised theorem-discovery algorithm that alternates between proof search and useful-theorem extraction, building a theorem library whose entries are reused as lemmas for subsequent proof search. Experiments show that the agent discovers tens of thousands of theorems and finds proofs for human-written benchmark problems, suggesting that its discoveries include theorems meaningful from a human mathematical perspective. Furthermore, the discovered theorems improve LLM proof performance when provided as prompt lemmas, indicating that they can serve as external knowledge for LLM reasoning. Our results provide evidence that useful theorems can emerge from proof search without relying on human-provided theorem libraries. More broadly, they suggest a path toward self-evolving AI systems for mathematics whose discoveries remain formally verifiable.

AI.tldr: AI agent autonomously discovers useful mathematical theorems from axioms alone without human priors, building a reusable theorem library that improves LLM proof performance.
AI.motivation: While current AI systems excel at mathematical reasoning by leveraging human knowledge (text, code, theorem libraries), it remains unknown whether an agent can autonomously discover useful theorems without such human priors. The paper investigates whether self-supervised theorem discovery is possible starting from only axioms and inference rules.
AI.method: Developed a self-supervised theorem-discovery algorithm that alternates between proof search and useful-theorem extraction. The agent starts from basic axioms and inference rules, gradually building a library of useful theorems discovered during proof search, which are then reused as lemmas for subsequent proofs.
AI.result: The agent discovered tens of thousands of theorems and found proofs for human-written benchmark problems, demonstrating that its discoveries include theorems meaningful from a human mathematical perspective. Additionally, the discovered theorems improved LLM proof performance when used as prompt lemmas, showing they can serve as external knowledge for LLM reasoning.
AI.conclusion: Useful theorems can emerge from proof search without relying on human-provided theorem libraries, suggesting a path toward self-evolving AI systems for mathematics whose discoveries remain formally verifiable.

title: Geometric Measurements of the Axiom of Choice in Neural Proof Embeddings
summary: The axiom of choice has divided the foundations of mathematics for over a century, but the distinction between classical and constructive proofs has remained a philosophical and methodological one. We use Lean 4's kernel-level tracking of axiom dependence to show that the axiom of choice has a measurable geometric correlate in proof space that obeys a one-parameter mixture law and has operational consequences for neural theorem provers. To do this, we partition $471{,}260$ declarations of Mathlib by transitive dependence on the axiom of choice and represent a filtered population of $42{,}355$ traced theorems by their sequences of tactic invocations. We use the constructive proofs in this dataset to train a self-supervised proof encoder and show that when using it to measure classical proofs, three complementary measurements (anomaly score, reconstruction loss, and density-superlevel containment) exhibit a common decline with the proof's distance from the axiom in the dependency graph, from sharp separation at the shallow boundary (AUC $0.847$ at distance $2$) to indistinguishability at distance~$9{+}$. Robustness controls show that the signature survives length, file, author, and topic controls, and replicates under full-source encoders trained on normalised proof source. Operationally, we show that on an evaluation sample of $251$ Mathlib theorems, Lean's \texttt{aesop} tactic solves constructive theorems at $13\times$ the rate of classical ones, and a neural-guided hybrid using the ReProver tactic generator compresses the gap to $5\times$. The geometric anomaly score predicts \texttt{aesop} failure beyond proof length, providing an operational link between the geometric signature and prover performance.

AI.tldr: The axiom of choice creates measurable geometric signatures in proof space that correlate with theorem prover performance, with constructive proofs being much easier for automated provers to solve than classical ones.
AI.motivation: To bridge the philosophical distinction between classical and constructive mathematics with empirical, measurable geometric correlates in proof space, and to understand how axiom dependence affects automated theorem proving performance.
AI.method: Used Lean 4's kernel-level tracking to partition 471,260 Mathlib declarations by dependence on axiom of choice, represented 42,355 traced theorems by tactic sequences, trained self-supervised proof encoders on constructive proofs, and measured classical proofs using anomaly detection metrics.
AI.result: Found that three complementary measurements (anomaly score, reconstruction loss, density-superlevel containment) decline with proof distance from axiom in dependency graph, with clear separation at shallow boundaries (AUC 0.847 at distance 2) to indistinguishability at distance 9+. Aesop tactic solves constructive theorems at 13× rate of classical ones, neural-guided hybrid reduces gap to 5×.
AI.conclusion: The axiom of choice has measurable geometric signatures in proof space that correlate with operational consequences for theorem provers, providing empirical evidence that philosophical distinctions between constructive and classical mathematics manifest in practical automated reasoning performance.

title: Budgeted Act-or-Defer Multi-Agent LLM Deliberation with Local Reliability Bounds
summary: Multi-agent deliberation among LLMs can improve reasoning, but deployment requires deciding when the current answer is reliable enough to act on and when it should be escalated to human review. We formulate this as budgeted act-or-defer decision making. At each round, the system maps the debate prefix to a low-dimensional state, computes a $k$-nearest-neighbor lower confidence bound on state-conditional correctness using calibration data, and acts only when the bound exceeds a user-specified reliability threshold. The certificate controls wrong actions through the decomposition $β= δ+ α+ \varepsilon_{\mathrm{act}}$, separating calibration failure, residual action risk, and representation gap. The guarantee is conditional, not distribution-free: it relies on a valid local bias envelope and an action-region representation-gap bound, and each assumption is paired with falsification-style diagnostics. Because the same absolute wrong-action budget has different meanings across tasks of different difficulty, we set budgets relative to each task's final-round error using training data only, and evaluate safety by normalized budget usage $\mathrm{WA}/β$. On six benchmarks against nine baselines, the method uses 9--12% of the pre-declared budget on activated datasets, reaching up to 84% automation and 96% acted-on accuracy; on stress-test datasets, it defers rather than forcing unreliable automation. Rather than relying on per-task post-hoc threshold search, the method prospectively converts a user-declared wrong-action budget into an auditable act-or-defer operating point before deployment, under explicitly stated assumptions.

AI.tldr: A budgeted act-or-defer framework for LLM multi-agent deliberation that determines when to act on automated answers versus when to defer to humans, with formal guarantees on wrong-action rates and empirical validation across multiple benchmarks.
AI.motivation: While multi-agent deliberation among LLMs improves reasoning, practical deployment requires deciding when the current answer is reliable enough to act on versus when it should be escalated to human review. Existing approaches often lack formal guarantees and require per-task threshold tuning.
AI.method: Formulates budgeted act-or-defer decision making where debate prefixes are mapped to low-dimensional states. Uses k-nearest-neighbor lower confidence bounds on state-conditional correctness with calibration data, acting only when bounds exceed user-specified reliability thresholds. Certificates control wrong actions through decomposition β = δ + α + ε_act separating calibration failure, residual action risk, and representation gap. Sets budgets relative to each task's final-round error using training data only.
AI.result: On six benchmarks against nine baselines, method uses 9-12% of pre-declared budget on activated datasets, reaching up to 84% automation and 96% acted-on accuracy. On stress-test datasets, it defers rather than forcing unreliable automation. Provides normalized budget usage (WA/β) as safety metric.
AI.conclusion: The method prospectively converts user-declared wrong-action budgets into auditable act-or-defer operating points before deployment, with explicit assumptions and falsification-style diagnostics, offering a principled alternative to per-task post-hoc threshold search.

title: Mechanistic Personality Analysis of LLMs Steering Personality via Latent Feature Interventions
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) have demonstrated the ability to simulate human-like OCEAN personality traits in generated text. Previous efforts have focused on prompt engineering or fine-tuning to shape LLM personality. In this work, we propose a mechanistic interpretability approach that directly intervenes on the model's latent features. Our method identifies latent directions in the residual stream corresponding to a target OCEAN trait using sparse autoencoders (SAEs) and contrastive activation analysis. We formalize an additive steering vector in activation space and demonstrate how applying a small additive shift to the hidden states enhances the target trait while preserving overall language modeling performance. To determine the optimal combination of feature shifts, we explore a linear weighting heuristic with grid search optimization that balances personality expression with task performance. Our approach shows promise in controllably steering personality traits at the mechanistic level while maintaining high performance on standard benchmarks.

AI.tldr: A mechanistic interpretability approach for steering LLM personality traits by identifying and intervening on latent directions in the residual stream, using sparse autoencoders and contrastive activation analysis to apply controlled additive shifts while maintaining language modeling performance.
AI.motivation: Previous methods for shaping LLM personality traits relied on prompt engineering or fine-tuning. The authors want a more direct, mechanistic approach that can controllably steer personality traits while preserving the model's core language modeling capabilities.
AI.method: Uses sparse autoencoders (SAEs) and contrastive activation analysis to identify latent directions in the residual stream corresponding to target OCEAN personality traits. Formalizes an additive steering vector in activation space and applies small additive shifts to hidden states. Employs linear weighting heuristic with grid search optimization to find optimal feature shift combinations.
AI.result: The approach demonstrates successful enhancement of target personality traits through additive steering vectors while preserving overall language modeling performance. The method shows promise for controllable personality trait steering at the mechanistic level.
AI.conclusion: The proposed mechanistic interpretability approach offers a direct method for steering LLM personality traits through latent feature intervention, providing better control than prompt engineering or fine-tuning while maintaining model performance on standard benchmarks.

title: Replica Symmetry Breaking and Algorithmic Thresholds in Empirical Risk Minimization under Multi-Index Model
summary:
Modern machine learning models are trained by optimizing high-dimensional non-convex empirical risk functions. Such cost functions can have a multitude of local optima and yet, gradient-based optimization appears to converge to near-global optima.
  Within a simple supervised learning setting, we develop a precise picture of which parts of the empirical risk landscape are accessible by polynomial-time algorithms. We are given i.i.d. pairs $\{(\boldsymbol{x}_i,y_i):\; 1 \le i\le n\}$ with $\boldsymbol{x}_i\in \mathbb{R}^d$ standard Gaussian feature vectors, and $y_i\in\mathbb{R}$ response variables that depend on $\boldsymbol{x}_i$ through their projections on an unknown $k$-dimensional subspace. We use empirical risk minimization to learn a model that depends on an $m$-dimensional projection of the data (e.g., an $m$-neurons neural network).
  We propose an incremental approximate message passing (IAMP) algorithm and precisely characterize the training error it achieves, as well as the relation between test and training error, in the high dimensional asymptotics $n,d\to\infty$, with $n/d\toα\in (0, +\infty)$. Based on earlier work in related models, we expect that the performance achieved by our algorithm is optimal among polynomial-time algorithms.

AI.tldr: Paper studies which parts of empirical risk landscapes are accessible to polynomial-time algorithms in high-dimensional supervised learning, characterizing optimal performance via incremental approximate message passing.
AI.motivation: Despite optimizing high-dimensional non-convex empirical risk functions, gradient-based methods often converge to near-global optima. The paper aims to understand precisely which regions of these landscapes are actually reachable by polynomial-time algorithms, bridging the gap between optimization theory and computational complexity.
AI.method: The authors propose an incremental approximate message passing (IAMP) algorithm for a supervised learning setting with i.i.d. Gaussian features. They analyze learning from projections in high-dimensional asymptotics where n,d→∞ with fixed ratio α = n/d. The setting involves learning a model that depends on m-dimensional data projections.
AI.result: The paper precisely characterizes both the training error achieved by the IAMP algorithm and the relationship between test and training error in the high-dimensional limit. The authors expect (based on related work) that this performance is optimal among all polynomial-time algorithms.
AI.conclusion: The research provides a rigorous theoretical framework for understanding the computational landscape of empirical risk minimization, identifying fundamental limits on what polynomial-time algorithms can achieve in high-dimensional supervised learning problems.

title: Clarus: Coordinating Autonomous Research Agents toward Web-Scale Scientific Collaboration
summary: Existing autonomous research agents can support parts of the research process, but most systems still treat research as either an isolated assistant task or a closed workflow. Therefore, autonomous science needs a collaboration infrastructure that coordinates projects, agents, and digital and physical resources. We identify this as a shift from code-centered execution loops to research-oriented collaboration processes, where questions, evidence, participants, and resources must be coordinated under uncertainty. In this framing, an agent may be an AI system, a human researcher, a team, a laboratory, or an organization-backed participant. To this end, we present Clarus, a collaboration infrastructure for coordinating autonomous research agents toward web-scale scientific collaboration. Clarus reformulates research as an open, auditable, attributable, and resource-aware multi-phase collaboration process. It defines a minimal project-agent-resource object model and organizes scientific collaboration through four layers including Research Application, Digital Collaboration, Physical Substrate, and Physical World. Core modules are implemented as pluggable mechanisms, allowing Clarus to adapt to task risk, collaboration structure, and resource constraints. Through a controlled paper-generation case study, we show that Clarus can organize a research goal into a traceable, reviewable, attributable, and accumulative collaboration network across phases, tasks, and participants. Together, the object model, collaboration protocol, trust mechanisms, and prototype validation provide an initial foundation for open research networks. Clarus is now available at clarus.holosai.io.

AI.tldr: Clarus is a collaboration infrastructure that coordinates autonomous research agents, digital/physical resources, and human participants for web-scale scientific collaboration through an open, auditable, and attributable multi-phase process.
AI.motivation: Existing autonomous research agents operate in isolated workflows rather than enabling comprehensive research collaboration. There's a need for infrastructure that can coordinate projects, agents, and resources in research-oriented collaboration processes under uncertainty.
AI.method: Clarus defines a minimal project-agent-resource object model and organizes collaboration through four layers (Research Application, Digital Collaboration, Physical Substrate, Physical World). It implements core modules as pluggable mechanisms and reformulates research as an open, auditable, attributable multi-phase process.
AI.result: Through a paper-generation case study, Clarus demonstrated ability to organize research goals into traceable, reviewable, attributable collaboration networks across phases, tasks, and participants, providing foundations for open research networks.
AI.conclusion: Clarus represents a shift from code-centered execution loops to research-oriented collaboration processes, offering initial infrastructure for coordinating autonomous agents toward web-scale scientific collaboration with built-in transparency and accountability mechanisms.

title: What LLMs explain is not what they believe: Evaluating explanation sufficiency under models' own input beliefs
comment: 23 pages, 9 figures, 13 tables, Forty-Third International Conference on Machine Learning (ICML 2026)
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed in high-stakes domains, where free-text explanations such as chain-of-thought and post-hoc rationales are used to justify model outputs. Yet it remains unclear whether these explanations are sufficient, i.e., if they contain enough information to explain the model's output-generating process. We generalize classical sufficiency from feature attributions to arbitrary explanations and prove that explanation sufficiency can change depending on the input distribution, which must be explicitly defined for LLM explanations. We propose using the LLM itself to generate alternative inputs conditioned on an explanation, capturing its beliefs about possible inputs. We formalize self-consistent sufficiency as a goal for free-text explanations and introduce an information-theoretic metric, SCSuff, that enables evaluation of free-text explanations without relying on predefined biases or shortcuts. Our experiments show that SCSuff agrees with targeted perturbation tests where applicable and demonstrate that explanation sufficiency can vary with the input distribution. We find LLM explanations are generally insufficient and weakly correlated with model size, accuracy, or output entropy. Analysis of final-token hidden states shows that top and bottom SCSuff scores can be predicted from internal representations, suggesting that SCSuff can guide detection and improvement of sufficient LLM explanations. The code for this paper is available at https://github.com/rajesh-lab/self-consistent-sufficiency .

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Self-Consistent Sufficiency (SCSuff), a new metric for evaluating whether free-text explanations from LLMs contain enough information to explain the model's decision-making process, showing that current LLM explanations are generally insufficient.
AI.motivation: LLMs are increasingly used in high-stakes domains where explanations like chain-of-thought and post-hoc rationales are used to justify outputs, but it's unclear whether these explanations are truly sufficient (contain enough information) to explain the model's decision process.
AI.method: Generalized classical sufficiency to arbitrary explanations, proposed using the LLM itself to generate alternative inputs conditioned on an explanation, formalized self-consistent sufficiency, and introduced an information-theoretic metric SCSuff that evaluates free-text explanations without predefined biases.
AI.result: SCSuff agrees with targeted perturbation tests, shows explanation sufficiency varies with input distribution, finds LLM explanations are generally insufficient and weakly correlated with model size/accuracy/output entropy, and demonstrates top/bottom SCSuff scores can be predicted from internal representations.
AI.conclusion: Explanation sufficiency is a crucial but often overlooked property of LLM explanations that depends on input distribution, and SCSuff can guide detection and improvement of sufficient explanations by leveraging the LLM's own beliefs about possible inputs.

title: Linguistic Firewall: Geometry as Defense in Multi-Agent Systems Routing
comment: 8 pages (9 more for appendix), 3 figures. Published at the Second Workshop on Agents in the Wild: Safety, Security, and Beyond (AIWILD) at ICML 2026
summary: The rapid integration of Large Language Models (LLMs) has driven the evolution of Multi-Agent Systems (MAS), where specialized agents collaborate to execute complex workflows.
