Effective orchestration in these environments requires robust routing mechanisms to efficiently allocate tasks to the most suitable agent. However, existing routers fundamentally rely on unverified proxies, ranging from textual self-descriptions to static surrogate representations, to gauge an agent's competence. This reliance on non-empirical data creates a critical gap between an agent's projected profile and its actual operational capabilities, introducing severe security vulnerabilities. Malicious agents can easily misrepresent their proficiencies or harbor covert backdoors that evade both standard external analysis and static representation-learning techniques. In this work, we introduce ANTAP (Automatic Non-Textual Agent Picker), an evaluation-driven routing architecture that discards indirect proxies in favor of active capability testing. By dynamically querying agents to ascertain their true competencies empirically, ANTAP distills performance into fixed behavioral operators within a shared semantic space. At inference time, routing is performed via a purely non-textual algebraic projection, establishing a "linguistic firewall" that renders metadata-based attacks inexpressible. In our experiments, ANTAP achieves near-zero ASR against description-based injection attacks, compared to 67.3\% and above for the description-based router baseline. Against adaptive embedding attacks, ANTAP achieves substantially lower ASR than the embedding-based baseline, with a 20\% reduction, while remaining resilient to description manipulation by design.

AI.tldr: ANTAP is an evaluation-driven routing architecture for multi-agent systems that uses active capability testing instead of textual descriptions or embeddings to prevent security vulnerabilities from malicious agents.
AI.motivation: Current routing mechanisms in multi-agent systems rely on unverified proxies like textual self-descriptions or static embeddings to assess agent competence, creating a dangerous gap between claimed and actual capabilities. This allows malicious agents to misrepresent their abilities or hide backdoors, leading to severe security vulnerabilities.
AI.method: ANTAP introduces active capability testing by dynamically querying agents to empirically ascertain their true competencies. It distills performance into fixed behavioral operators within a shared semantic space and uses purely non-textual algebraic projection for routing, creating a "linguistic firewall" against metadata-based attacks.
AI.result: ANTAP achieves near-zero Attack Success Rate (ASR) against description-based injection attacks, compared to 67.3% and above for description-based router baselines. Against adaptive embedding attacks, ANTAP achieves substantially lower ASR with a 20% reduction compared to embedding-based baselines, and remains resilient to description manipulation by design.
AI.conclusion: By replacing indirect proxies with empirical capability testing and non-textual routing, ANTAP effectively closes the security gap between claimed and actual agent competencies, establishing robust protection against malicious agent deception while maintaining effective task routing.

title: Primary ICD Category Prediction using LLM-based Probing
comment: 9 pages, 2 figures. Supplementary materials provided as an ancillary file
summary:
Objective: ICD codes are central to reimbursement, research, and population health surveillance, yet automated coding systems often struggle to integrate diagnostic signals from both clinical narratives and structured electronic health record (EHR) variables. We evaluated whether frozen medical large language model (LLM) representations can serve as a shared embedding space for multimodal primary diagnosis category prediction.
  Materials and Methods: We constructed a MIMIC-IV cohort of 13,645 admissions from the 10 most frequent primary ICD-10 codes, consolidated into seven categories. Structured variables were serialized into clinical narratives and combined with leakage-pruned discharge notes. Using a frozen MedFound-Llama3-8B-finetuned backbone, we extracted hidden states from five transformer layers and trained linear probes for structured-only, unstructured-only, and combined inputs, comparing against XGBoost and information-matched PLM-ICD baselines and evaluating MIMIC-III adaptation with a compact bottleneck adapter.
  Results: The combined probe performed best on MIMIC-IV (87.69% strict; 91.45% medical accuracy), exceeding both single-modality probes and baselines. The structured-only probe outperformed its standard baseline by 6.19 points in medical accuracy. Diagnostic information became increasingly linearly separable in deeper layers, and a 2M-parameter adapter restored cross-dataset transfer to MIMIC-III using only 5% of target labels.
  Discussion: LLM embeddings can unify structured and narrative EHR information for multimodal diagnosis prediction, supporting efficient reuse of clinical representations across modalities and datasets through a small representation-level module.
  Conclusion: Multimodal probing of frozen medical LLM representations provides a practical approach for studying EHR modalities and adapting clinical representations across datasets.

AI.tldr: Frozen medical LLM embeddings can effectively unify structured and narrative EHR data for ICD code prediction, outperforming single-modality approaches and enabling efficient cross-dataset adaptation with minimal parameters.
AI.motivation: ICD codes are crucial for healthcare reimbursement, research, and surveillance, but automated coding systems struggle to integrate diagnostic signals from both clinical narratives and structured EHR variables effectively.
AI.method: Used frozen MedFound-Llama3-8B model to extract hidden states from transformer layers, trained linear probes for structured-only, unstructured-only, and combined inputs. Compared against XGBoost and PLM-ICD baselines, evaluated cross-dataset adaptation with MIMIC-III using a compact bottleneck adapter.
AI.result: Combined probe achieved best performance (87.69% strict, 91.45% medical accuracy), exceeding single-modality probes and baselines. Diagnostic information became more linearly separable in deeper layers, and a 2M-parameter adapter restored cross-dataset transfer using only 5% of target labels.
AI.conclusion: Multimodal probing of frozen medical LLM representations provides a practical approach for studying EHR modalities and adapting clinical representations across datasets, enabling efficient reuse of clinical representations.

title: Randomized Exploration for Linear Bandits via Absolute Perturbations
summary: In stochastic linear bandits, the canonical Upper Confidence Bound (UCB) algorithm admits a simple frequentist regret analysis but can be computationally demanding, while Thompson Sampling (TS) is computationally attractive yet typically harder to analyze due to its non-optimistic nature. We propose Absolute Thompson Sampling (ATS), a simple modification of TS that ensures optimism in expectation by replacing the signed exploration noise with its absolute value. This preserves the computational efficiency of TS while avoiding the technically involved anti-concentration arguments common in TS analyses, enabling a simple UCB-style regret analysis. We show that ATS achieves $\tilde{O}(d^{3/2}\sqrt{K})$ regret, matching existing bounds for TS in linear bandits. We further introduce Ensemble Absolute Thompson Sampling (EATS), which takes the maximum over multiple absolute perturbations with normalization by the ensemble size. As the ensemble size grows, EATS converges to the UCB objective, recovering UCB behavior in the limit. Experiments show that moderate ensemble sizes already yield strong performance. Our results point to a bridge between randomized exploration and deterministic optimism both in theory and practice.

AI.tldr: ATS replaces TS's signed exploration noise with absolute values to ensure optimism while maintaining computational efficiency, enabling simpler UCB-style analysis with matching regret bounds. EATS ensemble version bridges TS and UCB.
AI.motivation: There's a trade-off in linear bandits: UCB has simple frequentist analysis but is computationally demanding, while TS is computationally efficient but harder to analyze due to its non-optimistic nature. The authors aim to create an algorithm that combines the best of both.
AI.method: Proposes Absolute Thompson Sampling (ATS) - a modified TS that replaces signed exploration noise with absolute value, ensuring optimism in expectation. Also introduces Ensemble ATS (EATS) which takes maximum over multiple absolute perturbations normalized by ensemble size, converging to UCB as ensemble grows.
AI.result: ATS achieves $\tilde{O}(d^{3/2}\sqrt{K})$ regret, matching existing TS bounds in linear bandits. EATS bridges TS and UCB behavior, with experiments showing strong performance even with moderate ensemble sizes.
AI.conclusion: ATS provides a computationally efficient algorithm with simple UCB-style analysis by ensuring optimism via absolute value modifications. The work establishes a theoretical and practical bridge between randomized exploration (TS) and deterministic optimism (UCB).

title: MedEvoEval: Evaluating Continual Evolution of Doctor Agents through Simulated Clinical Episodes
summary: Doctor agents are moving beyond single-turn answer generation toward evolving clinical decision systems. Within an outpatient episode, they acquire evidence, use examination and consultation resources, and decide when to finalize a diagnosis and management plan. Across episodes, their behavior may change through memory, retrieval, reflection, or other update mechanisms. Current evaluations only partially cover this setting. Fixed-input medical QA benchmarks score final answers from complete inputs, whereas many interactive benchmarks still focus on individual encounters or fixed runs, providing limited support for evaluating how episode-level decisions interact with cross-episode experience. We introduce MedEvoEval, an executable longitudinal evaluation framework based on action-gated simulated outpatient episodes. Each source case is converted into role-specific patient, examination, and manager views; evidence is revealed only through valid actions; and each episode records a structured trace that links observations, actions, final outputs, manager scores, and optional experience write-back. We release a runnable E&D artifact with 700 processed episodes, provenance notes, schemas, an episode runner, scoring scripts, configurations, example logs, analysis code, and trajectory- and step-level derivatives. Experiments show that episode traces expose process costs hidden by final-answer scoring, show how MDT-style consultation reallocates resources, and support longitudinal analyses of memory maturation, held-out transfer, update-stage response, and backward retention. Together, these results show that MedEvoEval provides a concrete basis for evaluating whether doctor agents improve through experience, transfer useful behavior, and retain earlier capabilities over time.

AI.tldr: MedEvoEval is a longitudinal evaluation framework for doctor agents that goes beyond single-turn QA to simulate outpatient episodes with action-gated evidence, enabling evaluation of clinical decision-making across multiple episodes with memory and learning capabilities.
AI.motivation: Current evaluations of doctor AI systems are inadequate for real-world clinical settings where agents make evolving decisions across multiple patient episodes. Existing benchmarks either score final answers from complete inputs (static QA) or focus on individual encounters, failing to capture how episode-level decisions interact with cross-episode experience and learning through memory mechanisms.
AI.method: The authors introduce MedEvoEval, an executable longitudinal evaluation framework based on action-gated simulated outpatient episodes. Each case is converted into role-specific patient, examination, and manager views where evidence is revealed only through valid actions. The framework records structured episode traces linking observations, actions, final outputs, manager scores, and optional experience write-back. The released artifact includes 700 processed episodes, schemas, an episode runner, scoring scripts, and analysis tools.
AI.result: Experiments demonstrate that episode traces reveal process costs hidden by final-answer scoring, show how multidisciplinary team (MDT)-style consultation reallocates resources, and support longitudinal analyses of memory maturation, held-out transfer, update-stage response, and backward retention. The framework enables evaluation of how doctor agents improve through experience and transfer behavior across episodes.
AI.conclusion: MedEvoEval provides a concrete basis for evaluating whether doctor agents genuinely improve through experience, transfer useful clinical behaviors, and retain earlier capabilities over time, addressing critical gaps in current evaluation methods for evolving clinical decision systems.

title: Improving Patient Subtyping on Longitudinal Data using Representations from Mamba-based Architecture
summary: Effective sub-typing (also known as grouping or clustering) of patients using their electronic health record (EHR) data can greatly inform precision medicine efforts. However, subtyping temporal EHR datasets is known to be challenging due to inherent EHR issues, including complexity and irregularity. In this study, we propose a self-supervised Mamba-based model that learns effective EHR representations and enables enhanced patient subtyping. We evaluate the proposed model on public and private real-world EHR datasets to classify the data based on the available labels and subtype patients based on the representations learned from the model. Through an extensive set of experiments, we demonstrate that our model's design choices lead to better performance compared to competitive baseline models for prediction. Moreover, we evaluate several clustering techniques to demonstrate that our findings offer valuable insights into subtyping patients based on temporal records from EHR models\footnote{Our implementations are available at https://github.com/healthylaife/triplet_mamba.

AI.tldr: A self-supervised Mamba-based model for learning effective EHR representations to enable enhanced patient subtyping, addressing challenges of temporal EHR data complexity and irregularity.
AI.motivation: Patient subtyping using EHR data is crucial for precision medicine but challenging due to EHR issues like complexity and irregularity of temporal data.
AI.method: A self-supervised Mamba-based model that learns effective EHR representations, evaluated on public and private real-world EHR datasets for classification and patient subtyping.
AI.result: The model outperforms competitive baselines for prediction tasks, and various clustering techniques demonstrate valuable insights for patient subtyping based on temporal EHR records.
AI.conclusion: The proposed Mamba-based model offers an effective solution for EHR representation learning and patient subtyping, contributing to precision medicine efforts by overcoming challenges of temporal EHR data complexity.

title: Expert Evaluation of Clinical AI Tools on Real Point-of-Care Clinical Queries
summary: Physicians now pose millions of clinical questions to AI tools each week, yet these tools are evaluated largely on hypothetical or exam-style questions, not those actually asked in practice. We report a blinded evaluation built on 620 Real-world Point-Of-Care Queries (Real-POCQi) submitted to the OpenEvidence (OE) platform by physicians spanning 30 specialties, as well as 187 questions from HealthBench. 149 practicing physicians across 36 states made head-to-head comparisons between answers from three frontier general-purpose models (Claude Opus 4.8, Gemini 3.1 Pro, and GPT-5.5) and a specialized clinical tool (OE), with graders matched to each question's specialty. When comparing answers along five dimensions relevant to clinical decision support -- accuracy, clinical utility, source quality, verifiability, & completeness -- physicians scored the specialized tool highest on all axes; in the primary analysis on Real-POCQi, win differences (margins between win and loss rates) ranged from 25 to 39 percentage points (p<0.001). Results remained consistent in sensitivity analyses stratifying by citation display, answer length, OE-user status, and Real-POCQi versus HealthBench. In parallel, LLM judges were found to systematically differ from expert judges, though both generally agreed on the best model. These findings underscore two conclusions: (i) AI tool evaluations should reflect real-world query distributions and use expert judges that mirror the specialization defining modern medicine and (ii) the consistent advantage of the specialized tool over general-purpose models does not necessarily mean that the latter cannot serve similar purposes, but that targeted engineering and customization can yield meaningful gains in performance for its users. We release Real-POCQi as a public benchmark, as well as the prespecified statistical analysis for reproducing results of this study.

AI.tldr: Specialized clinical AI tool outperforms frontier general-purpose LLMs on real-world physician queries when evaluated by expert physicians.
AI.motivation: Current AI evaluations use hypothetical/exam questions rather than real clinical queries, creating a gap between lab performance and real-world utility.
AI.method: Blinded evaluation using 620 real-world physician queries (Real-POCQi) plus 187 HealthBench questions; 149 physicians across 36 states compared answers from 3 frontier models vs specialized OpenEvidence tool.
AI.result: Specialized tool scored highest on all 5 clinical dimensions; win differences of 25-39 percentage points (p<0.001). Results robust across sensitivity analyses. LLM judges systematically differed from expert judges.
AI.conclusion: AI evaluations should use real-world queries and expert judges; specialized engineering yields meaningful gains over general-purpose models for clinical applications.

title: When More Sampling Hurts: The Modal Ceiling and Correlation Ceiling of Test-Time Scaling
comment: 24 pages, 10 figures, 3 tables. Code and data: https://github.com/bay-yearick-lab/sampling-ceilings
summary: People overthink; language models over-sample, and the extra effort can talk both into a worse answer. Reasoning systems answer a hard question by sampling it many times (test-time scaling), and the more they draw, the more often a correct answer turns up somewhere, so coverage, the fraction of problems with at least one correct try, climbs and appears to be progress. But a deployed system must return one answer, and choosing it, not knowing which try is right, is selection; selection is capped, and past a point extra samples only make the model surer of a confident mistake, even as every draw adds cost. The gap between climbing coverage and stalled selection, the identifiability gap, is the answer a model can produce but not pick. So the real question is not whether to sample but how far, and the answer is: not far. For picking an answer, the vote has already settled within a few dozen draws, the modal ceiling; for scoring a benchmark, sooner still, the correlation ceiling. Beyond that, extra draws cost compute and add nothing, and can even make the answer worse. This paper turns the cutoff into a single number, the effective number of samples, that any sampling run already reveals. The bottleneck is recognizing a right answer, not generating one.

AI.tldr: Extra sampling in reasoning systems creates a gap between generating correct answers (coverage) and actually selecting them (selection), with diminishing returns after a few dozen samples due to identifiability limitations.
AI.motivation: Current reasoning systems use test-time scaling by sampling many answers to increase coverage, but this creates a fundamental problem: systems can generate correct answers but fail to identify and select them, wasting computational resources while potentially becoming more confident about wrong answers.
AI.method: The paper introduces the concept of "identifiability gap" between coverage and selection, and proposes measuring the "effective number of samples" - a single number that reveals when additional sampling provides no benefit for answer selection.
AI.result: Shows that voting/selection settles within a few dozen draws (modal ceiling), and benchmark correlation saturates even sooner (correlation ceiling). Additional sampling beyond these points adds cost without improving selection and can even degrade performance.
AI.conclusion: The bottleneck in reasoning systems is recognizing correct answers, not generating them. Sampling should be limited to a few dozen draws, as measured by the effective number of samples, to avoid wasted computation and potential performance degradation from over-sampling.

title: Customized Generative AI Agent for Transportation Engineering Practice: A Development and Continued Pre-training Guideline
summary: Recent advancements in generative artificial intelligence (AI) and large language models (LLMs) have shown significant promise in automating complex reasoning, summarization, and question-answering tasks. However, the effectiveness of general-purpose LLMs in specialized engineering domains remains limited due to insufficient exposure to technical standards, engineering terminology, and domain-specific semantics. This study proposes a systematic approach to developing a customized generative AI agent for transportation engineering applications. A curated corpus of U.S. transportation manuals, design guidelines, and regulatory documents is used to conduct continued pretraining of six state-of-the-art LLMs through a unified low-rank adaptation (LoRA) framework. The training process is monitored to ensure convergence and model stability. Performance is evaluated using standard natural language processing metrics, including BLEU-4 and ROUGE, with Qwen2.5-7B and LLaMA-3.1-8B demonstrating the highest domain alignment and response quality. Results validate the effectiveness of LoRA-based adaptation in improving LLM performance on technical content interpretation and context-specific reasoning. This work contributes a reproducible development framework for constructing domain-specialized generative AI agents, supporting broader deployment in transportation research, design, planning, and policy analysis.

AI.tldr: This paper develops a custom AI agent for transportation engineering by adapting six state-of-the-art LLMs using LoRA on domain-specific documents, finding Qwen2.5-7B and LLaMA-3.1-8B perform best for technical interpretation.
AI.motivation: General-purpose LLMs are ineffective for specialized engineering domains due to lack of exposure to technical standards, terminology, and domain-specific semantics in areas like transportation engineering.
AI.method: Systematic approach using curated corpus of U.S. transportation manuals, guidelines, and regulations to conduct continued pretraining of six state-of-the-art LLMs through unified LoRA framework, with monitored convergence and stability.
AI.result: Qwen2.5-7B and LLaMA-3.1-8B demonstrated highest domain alignment and response quality using BLEU-4 and ROUGE metrics. LoRA-based adaptation effectively improves LLM performance on technical content interpretation and context-specific reasoning.
AI.conclusion: The study provides a reproducible framework for developing domain-specialized generative AI agents, enabling broader deployment in transportation research, design, planning, and policy analysis through validated LoRA adaptation.

title: Closed-Form Steepest Descent Direction toward Flat Minima: Reducing Upper Bounds on the Loss Hessian Eigenspectrum in Neural Networks
summary: The flatness hypothesis suggests that flatness of the loss landscape, as measured by the eigenvalues of the loss Hessian, correlates with better neural network generalization. While various algorithms reduce these eigenvalues, most focus on procedural design, leaving it unclear how data distributions and NN parameters structurally determine directions toward flat minima. Characterizing these directions analytically is generally intractable. To overcome this mathematical difficulty, recent studies derived the Wolkowicz-Styan (WS) upper bound on the maximum eigenvalue of the cross-entropy loss Hessian in three-layer NNs. Although this upper bound is differentiable, its gradient was not derived. Therefore, we analytically derive the gradient of the WS upper bound to characterize directions leading to flat minima. Based on this, we propose Hessian Spectral Range (HSR) Regularization, which updates parameters along the steepest descent direction of the WS bound. Experiments demonstrate that HSR Regularization narrows the Hessian eigenvalue spectrum, avoids sharp minima and saddle points, and promotes convergence to flat minima. Although the applicability of this method is currently limited to cross-entropy loss and three-layer architectures, to the best of the authors' knowledge, this is the first study to report a closed-form gradient that promotes convergence to flat minima without numerical approximations. Therefore, the theoretical analysis of this gradient is expected to contribute to the further development of NNs.

AI.tldr: Researchers derive the first closed-form gradient of a Hessian eigenvalue upper bound and use it to create HSR regularization, which steers neural networks toward flat minima for better generalization.
AI.motivation: While flatness correlates with better generalization and algorithms exist to reduce loss Hessian eigenvalues, there's limited understanding of how data distributions and parameters structurally determine directions to flat minima, and analytical characterization is intractable. Additionally, though a differentiable upper bound exists for the maximum eigenvalue, its gradient hasn't been derived.
AI.method: Analytically derive the gradient of the Wolkowicz-Styan upper bound for maximum eigenvalue of cross-entropy loss Hessian in three-layer networks. Use this gradient to propose Hessian Spectral Range Regularization, which updates parameters along the steepest descent direction of this upper bound.
AI.result: Experiments show HSR regularization narrows the Hessian eigenvalue spectrum, avoids sharp minima and saddle points, and promotes convergence to flat minima. The method provides the first closed-form gradient that promotes convergence to flat minima without numerical approximations.
AI.conclusion: The theoretical analysis of this gradient represents a significant contribution, as it's the first study to report a closed-form gradient promoting flat minima convergence. Though limited to cross-entropy loss and three-layer architectures, this work is expected to contribute to further neural network development.

title: Preventing Error Propagation in Multi-Agent AI through Runtime Monitoring
summary: Multi-agent AI systems can improve answer selection by allowing different language models to exchange reasoning traces, revise initial predictions, and support a final decision. However, such communication may also introduce reliability risks: reasoning from one agent can correct another agent's mistake, but it can also mislead an agent that was initially correct. This paper studies reliable multi-agent AI communication through reasoning exchange and runtime answer revision. We develop a framework in which agents first answer multiple-choice questions independently, then share reasoning traces and revise their decisions. We conduct numerical experiments where we evaluate whether this process improves accuracy, produces more positive than negative answer transitions, and remains effective across domains such as cybersecurity, networking, and general knowledge. The results help identify when multi-agent reasoning improves reliability and when it may propagate errors.

AI.tldr: Multi-agent AI systems can improve answer selection through reasoning exchange and revision, but this communication may introduce reliability risks where correct agents can be misled.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to study reliable multi-agent AI communication through reasoning exchange and runtime answer revision, as such communication can both improve accuracy by correcting mistakes but also introduce risks by misleading initially correct agents.
AI.method: Develop a framework where agents first answer multiple-choice questions independently, then share reasoning traces and revise their decisions. Conduct numerical experiments to evaluate whether the process improves accuracy, produces more positive than negative answer transitions, and remains effective across domains like cybersecurity, networking, and general knowledge.
AI.result: The results help identify when multi-agent reasoning improves reliability and when it may propagate errors, showing the conditions under which reasoning exchange leads to improved accuracy versus error propagation.
AI.conclusion: Multi-agent AI communication through reasoning exchange can enhance reliability but carries risks of error propagation; careful implementation is needed to ensure positive transitions outweigh negative ones.

title: Entropy Regularized Reinforcement Learning for Zero-Sum Stochastic Differential Games in a Regime-Switching Jump-Diffusion Process
summary: To address parameter misspecification and sudden structural environmental changes in conventional stochastic differential game (SDG) frameworks, this paper introduces a distributional control approach that characterizes optimal strategies as probability distributions over actions, conditioned on the continuous state, the discrete regime state, and parameters. This forms a reinforcement learning framework for entropy-regularized zero-sum stochastic differential games (ERRL-ZSSDGs) in a regime-switching jump-diffusion process. Using the dynamic programming principle (DPP), we derive the associated coupled systems of Hamilton-Jacobi-Bellman-Isaacs (HJBI) equations, from which equilibrium strategies are expressed via gradients of the value function. For linear-quadratic problems, semi-analytical solutions for both value function and equilibrium strategies are obtained by solving a system of coupled ordinary differential equations (ODEs). In more general settings, an Actor-Critic policy improvement algorithm is developed to approximate the value functions and equilibrium policies across different regimes. The method is applied to an investment game, and numerical examples illustrate the effect of the temperature parameter and regime transitions on optimal policies and values.

AI.tldr: The paper develops a distributional control framework for entropy-regularized zero-sum stochastic differential games in regime-switching jump-diffusion processes, addressing parameter misspecification and structural changes through probability distributions over actions.
AI.motivation: To overcome limitations of conventional stochastic differential game frameworks, specifically parameter misspecification and sudden structural environmental changes, which traditional approaches struggle to handle effectively.
AI.method: Introduces a distributional control approach where optimal strategies are probability distributions over actions conditioned on state, regime, and parameters. Uses dynamic programming principle to derive coupled Hamilton-Jacobi-Bellman-Isaacs equations. For linear-quadratic problems, obtains semi-analytical solutions via ODE systems. For general settings, develops an Actor-Critic policy improvement algorithm for value function and policy approximation across regimes.
AI.result: Successfully derives equilibrium strategies expressed via gradients of the value function. Obtains semi-analytical solutions for linear-quadratic problems. Develops an Actor-Critic algorithm for general settings. Application to an investment game with numerical examples shows effects of temperature parameter and regime transitions on optimal policies and values.
AI.conclusion: The proposed distributional control framework effectively handles parameter uncertainty and structural changes in stochastic differential games, providing both theoretical solutions for tractable cases and practical computational methods for general problems, with demonstrated applicability to financial investment scenarios.

title: Memory as an Attack Surface in LLM Agents: A Study on Multiple-Choice Question Answering
summary: AI agents extend conventional large language model (LLM) applications by integrating language understanding with task execution, external tool use, and memory mechanisms. While memory allows agents to retain prior interactions and provide more personalized and context-aware responses, it also introduces a new vulnerability: information stored in memory can influence future outputs even when the current query is clean. In this paper, we investigate memory manipulation in LLM-based agents for multiple-choice question answering. We first design and implement an LLM-based AI agent with an external memory component that stores and retrieves task-relevant information. We then introduce basic memory manipulation scenarios in which misleading or corrupted memories are inserted into the agent before it answers multiple-choice questions. Using a controlled experimental setup, we compare the agent's performance before and after memory manipulation and measure changes in answer accuracy, attack success rate, and selection of manipulated options. Our results show that even simple memory manipulations can noticeably affect the agent's final answers, causing it to select incorrect options despite receiving clean and well-formed questions.

AI.tldr: The paper shows that AI agents with memory systems are vulnerable to manipulation - corrupted memories can cause them to answer multiple-choice questions incorrectly even with clean queries.
AI.motivation: While memory enables AI agents to provide personalized, context-aware responses, it creates a new security vulnerability: information stored in memory can influence future outputs even when current queries are clean, posing risks to agent reliability.
AI.method: Researchers designed an LLM-based AI agent with external memory, introduced memory manipulation scenarios where misleading/corrupted memories were inserted, then tested the agent's multiple-choice answering performance before and after manipulation using controlled experiments.
AI.result: Even simple memory manipulations noticeably affected the agent's final answers, causing it to select incorrect options despite receiving clean, well-formed questions. The study measured decreased answer accuracy, increased attack success rate, and selection of manipulated options.
AI.conclusion: Memory manipulation presents a significant security vulnerability in AI agents, highlighting the need for memory safety mechanisms and verification protocols to ensure agent reliability when using external memory systems.

title: Entropy-Regularized Reinforcement Learning for Linear-Quadratic Stackelberg Differential Games in Regime-Switching Diffusion Models
summary: Stackelberg differential games (SDGs) provide a powerful framework for hierarchical decision-making in stochastic and continuous-time environments, yet their solution remains computationally challenging due to the complexity of traditional dynamic programming and Hamilton-Jacobi-Bellman-Isaacs (HJBI) methods, especially in high-dimensional systems. This paper proposes an entropy-regularized reinforcement learning (ERRL) approach for linear-quadratic SDGs (LQ-SDGs) within a continuous-time diffusion framework governed by Markovian regime switching. The key innovation lies in deriving exploratory weakly-coupled HJBI equations with entropy regularization, which promotes stochastic policies that actively avoid suboptimal equilibria -- a limitation of classical SDG methods. Neural networks are integrated to approximate regime-dependent value functions and solve high-dimensional partial differential equations (PDEs) efficiently, while a novel sampling technique enhances computational tractability. Numerical results demonstrate the effectiveness of the framework compared to conventional approaches, particularly in escaping suboptimal traps through exploratory policies. The study highlights the critical role of entropy regularization and neural network approximations in achieving robust solutions for hierarchical decision-making problems under abrupt environmental shifts.

AI.tldr: An entropy-regularized RL approach using neural networks to solve continuous-time linear-quadratic Stackelberg differential games with Markovian regime switching, overcoming computational challenges of traditional methods.
AI.motivation: Traditional methods for solving Stackelberg differential games (HJBI equations and dynamic programming) are computationally challenging, especially for high-dimensional systems and continuous-time environments with stochasticity and regime switching.
AI.method: Proposes entropy-regularized reinforcement learning approach that derives exploratory weakly-coupled HJBI equations with entropy regularization, uses neural networks to approximate regime-dependent value functions, and employs novel sampling techniques for computational tractability.
AI.result: The framework demonstrates effectiveness compared to conventional approaches, particularly in escaping suboptimal traps through exploratory policies, with numerical results validating the approach.
AI.conclusion: Entropy regularization and neural network approximations play critical roles in achieving robust solutions for hierarchical decision-making problems under abrupt environmental shifts in continuous-time LQ-SDGs.

title: Low-cost concept-based localized explanations: How far can we get with training-free approaches?
comment: 6 pages, 2 figures, 4 tables. Accepted at the 2026 IEEE International Conference on Artificial Intelligence (CAI), 8-10 May 2026, Granada, Spain. Code: https://github.com/darianfgUgr/CoNa
summary: Concept-based Explainable AI (C-XAI) seeks human-understandable explanations grounded in semantic concepts, yet validation is limited by the scarcity of fine-grained concept annotations. We evaluate whether mid-scale Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) can perform localized concept naming under strict zero-shot conditions by assigning labels to bounding-box regions at both object and part levels. We propose a reproducible zero-shot evaluation protocol for Concept Naming (CoNa) with (i) closed-set, category-constrained prompting for moderate vocabularies and (ii) Open-CoNa, an embedding-similarity-based strategy for large label spaces. Experiments with four MLLMs (7B-32B) show consistent performance trends across datasets, reaching 62%-88% object-level exact-match accuracy, highlighting the potential of training-free concept annotation from localized regions. We discuss limitations and failure modes and release a reproducible framework to support future low-cost C-XAI research.

AI.tldr: Mid-scale MLLMs can perform zero-shot concept naming for bounding boxes, achieving 62%-88% object-level accuracy using training-free methods with closed-set and open-set strategies.
AI.motivation: Concept-based XAI needs human-understandable explanations but suffers from limited fine-grained concept annotations. The research explores whether mid-scale MLLMs can provide low-cost concept annotations without training.
AI.method: Proposed zero-shot evaluation protocol for Concept Naming (CoNa) with two strategies: (1) closed-set, category-constrained prompting for moderate vocabularies, and (2) Open-CoNa using embedding-similarity for large label spaces. Evaluated four MLLMs (7B-32B parameters) on localized bounding box regions at object and part levels.
AI.result: Consistent performance trends across datasets with 62%-88% object-level exact-match accuracy. Demonstrated potential for training-free concept annotation from localized regions. Released reproducible framework for future C-XAI research.
AI.conclusion: Mid-scale MLLMs show promising capability for zero-shot concept naming, offering a low-cost solution for concept annotation in C-XAI. Limitations and failure modes were discussed, with framework release to support further research.

title: Constrained Tabular Diffusion for Finance
comment: Published at ACM International Conference on AI in Finance (ICAIF) 2025
summary: Generative models in finance face the dual challenge of producing realistic data while satisfying strict regulatory and economic objectives, a requirement that standard tabular diffusion models cannot provide. To address this difficulty, we introduce Constrained Tabular Diffusion for Finance (CTDF), a novel integration of sampling-time feasibility operations with mixed-type tabular diffusion in financial applications. By incorporating a training-free feasibility operator into the reverse-diffusion sampling loop, CTDF enforces hard constraints for applications such as simulation, legal compliance, and extrapolation. Extensive experiments on large-scale financial datasets demonstrate zero constraint violations and improvement in scarce data utility. CTDF establishes a robust method for generating trustworthy and compliant synthetic data, opening new avenues for rigorous generative modeling and analysis in the financial domain.

AI.tldr: CTDF is a constrained tabular diffusion model for finance that generates synthetic data while strictly enforcing regulatory and economic constraints through a sampling-time feasibility operator.
AI.motivation: Standard tabular diffusion models fail to meet the strict regulatory and economic requirements in finance, which demand not only realistic data but also guaranteed compliance with hard constraints.
AI.method: CTDF integrates a training-free feasibility operator into the reverse-diffusion sampling loop of mixed-type tabular diffusion, allowing it to enforce hard constraints during data generation.
AI.result: Experiments on large-scale financial datasets show zero constraint violations and improved utility for scarce data, demonstrating the model's effectiveness in generating compliant synthetic data.
AI.conclusion: CTDF establishes a robust method for generating trustworthy and compliant synthetic financial data, opening new avenues for rigorous generative modeling and analysis in the financial domain.

title: Managing the Human Fallback: Skill Investment Under Improving AI and Worker Mobility
summary: When firms deploy autonomous AI, they must decide how much work to leave to the system and how much to keep workers engaged. This decision affects current output and future human capital. We develop a parsimonious two-period model in which AI may outperform the worker when it functions, but may fail with positive probability. A firm chooses worker engagement; engagement lowers current output for below-benchmark workers, but changes future skill through learning and erosion. We distinguish two dimensions of AI progress: capability, the system's output when it works, and reliability, the probability that it works. In a single-firm benchmark, engagement is valuable only as fallback investment. The firm engages the least-skilled workers most, because they have the largest skill gaps and are least costly to bring toward a useful fallback level. With worker mobility, engagement also affects labor-market sorting: workers prefer jobs that build more valuable skill trajectories. This sorting motive targets higher-skill workers near the AI frontier, where skill gains are more valuable and engagement is less costly. Mobility can therefore reverse the engagement pattern, shifting investment from the least-skilled toward the most-skilled workers below the AI benchmark. Mobility also reshapes how AI progress affects engagement: greater capability raises engagement by increasing the value of the skill trajectory a firm offers, whereas greater reliability can raise or lower it because it reduces fallback need while also changing learning opportunities. Under worker mobility, human-AI work design becomes a problem of human-capital investment, in which allocating work today shapes future skill.

AI.tldr: A model analyzing how firms should allocate work between AI systems and human workers, considering current productivity versus future human capital development, with worker mobility changing investment patterns from least-skilled to frontier workers.
AI.motivation: Firms deploying autonomous AI face a fundamental trade-off: how much work to allocate to AI systems versus keeping workers engaged. This decision affects not just current productivity but also future human capital development, creating tension between short-term output and long-term skill development.
AI.method: A parsimonious two-period model where AI may outperform workers when functioning but can fail with positive probability. Firms choose worker engagement levels, which affect current output and future skills through learning and erosion. The model distinguishes between AI capability (output when working) and reliability (probability of working), and examines both single-firm and worker mobility scenarios.
AI.result: In single-firm settings, engagement serves only as fallback investment, with least-skilled workers engaged most due to their larger skill gaps and lower costs to reach useful fallback levels. With worker mobility, engagement affects labor-market sorting - workers prefer jobs that build valuable skill trajectories. This shifts investment from least-skilled to higher-skill workers near the AI frontier, reversing the engagement pattern. Greater AI capability increases engagement by making skill trajectories more valuable, while greater reliability has ambiguous effects by reducing fallback need but changing learning opportunities.
AI.conclusion: Human-AI work design becomes fundamentally a human-capital investment problem under worker mobility, where allocating work today shapes future skill development. The model shows that worker mobility dramatically changes optimal engagement strategies, shifting investment toward frontier workers who can benefit most from skill development near the AI benchmark.

title: A Path-Space Formulation of Prediction in World Models: From a Single Action to Prediction, Planning, and Irreversibility
summary: We propose a path-space formulation of prediction in AI world models. Rather than sequences of one-step conditional distributions, we argue that a world model implicitly defines a probability measure over future trajectories. In the local regime where latent dynamics admit an effective Markovian description, this path measure takes the Onsager-Machlup form. Within this framework, prediction (most probable trajectory), planning (constrained optimization), and uncertainty (fluctuations) emerge as operations on a single action functional. We decompose the latent dynamics into reversible and irreversible components and introduce operational measures of entropy production from model rollouts. In controlled small-scale attention-based models, we find that attention asymmetry is acquired during training in proportion to the irreversibility of the data. Symmetrizing the learned attention suppresses entropy production and selectively degrades long-horizon prediction of irreversible dynamics while preserving relaxational prediction. These results suggest that irreversibility may serve as a computational resource for predictive world models. More generally, the fundamental predictive object is a distribution over future paths rather than states.

AI.tldr: World models should predict probability distributions over future trajectories rather than sequences of one-step predictions, with irreversibility serving as a computational resource for long-horizon prediction.
AI.motivation: The paper argues that current AI world models focus on sequences of one-step conditional distributions, but the fundamental predictive object should be probability measures over future trajectories. This shift provides a unified framework for prediction, planning, and uncertainty quantification.
AI.method: Proposes a path-space formulation where world models implicitly define probability measures over future trajectories. Uses Onsager-Machlup form in the Markovian regime, decomposing latent dynamics into reversible and irreversible components. Measures entropy production from model rollouts and analyzes attention asymmetry in controlled small-scale attention-based models.
AI.result: Found that attention asymmetry develops during training proportional to data irreversibility. Symmetrizing learned attention suppresses entropy production and selectively degrades long-horizon prediction of irreversible dynamics while preserving relaxational prediction.
AI.conclusion: Irreversibility serves as a computational resource for predictive world models, and the fundamental predictive object should be distributions over future paths rather than states, providing a unified framework for prediction, planning, and uncertainty.

title: Characterizing Large Language Model Agentic Workflows: A Study on N8n Ecosystem
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) are rapidly being adopted in low-code and no-code automation platforms, where non-expert users design workflows that combine natural language understanding with external services and APIs. LLM agents are LLM systems that use LLMs as a core "brain" to reason, plan, and autonomously execute complex, multi-step tasks. In this paper, we present the first large-scale empirical study of LLM agentic workflows in low-code automation platforms. We analyze more than 6,000 publicly available n8n workflows and examine four aspects of their design: task distribution, structural and tool use patterns, reliability mechanisms, and autonomy levels. Our analysis shows that LLM workflows are not merely prompt response pipelines. Instead, LLMs are commonly embedded within broader automation structures involving control logic, external tools, communication services, storage systems, and human review points. We further find that while many workflows include lightweight post-processing or routing logic after LLM execution, explicit reliability mechanisms such as structured fallback paths, repair loops, failure-specific alerts, and human approval gates remain relatively uncommon. These results reveal a gap between the increasing deployment of LLM agents in practical automation ecosystems and the limited engineering support for reliability, safety, and governance. Overall, our study provides ten empirical findings and five research takeaways for researchers, platform developers, and practitioners seeking to understand and improve real-world LLM agentic workflows.

AI.tldr: First large-scale empirical study of LLM agentic workflows in low-code platforms analyzing 6,000+ n8n workflows, revealing gaps between deployment and reliability mechanisms.
AI.motivation: LLMs are rapidly being adopted in low-code/no-code automation platforms for non-expert users, but there's a lack of understanding about how LLM agentic workflows are actually designed and implemented in real-world settings.
AI.method: Analyzed more than 6,000 publicly available n8n workflows to examine four key aspects: task distribution, structural and tool use patterns, reliability mechanisms, and autonomy levels.
AI.result: LLM workflows are not simple prompt-response pipelines but complex structures involving control logic, external tools, communication services, storage, and human review. However, explicit reliability mechanisms (structured fallback, repair loops, failure alerts, human approval) remain uncommon despite increasing deployment.
AI.conclusion: There's a significant gap between LLM agent deployment in practical automation and the limited engineering support for reliability, safety, and governance. The study provides 10 empirical findings and 5 research takeaways to guide researchers, platform developers, and practitioners.

title: Hierarchical Decision Making with Structured Policies: A Principled Design via Inverse Optimization
summary: Hierarchical decision-making frameworks are pivotal for addressing complex control tasks, enabling agents to decompose intricate problems into manageable subgoals. Despite their promise, existing hierarchical policies face critical limitations: (i) reinforcement learning (RL)-based methods struggle to guarantee strict constraint satisfaction, and (ii) optimal control (OC)-based approaches often rely on myopic and computationally prohibitive formulations. To reconcile these trade-offs, hierarchical RL-OC architectures have emerged as a promising paradigm. However, the formulation of the lower-level optimization within these frameworks remains underexplored, often relying on heuristic or myopic objectives. In this work, we propose a principled framework that systematically integrates upper-level goal abstraction with structured lower-level decision making. We adopt an inverse optimization approach to inform the structure of the lower-level problem from expert demonstrations, ensuring that the objective of the lower-level policy remains aligned with the overall long-term task goal. To validate the approach, our framework is evaluated on distinct decision making tasks: network-based resource allocation and continuous collision avoidance. Empirical results demonstrate that our method consistently outperforms strong baselines based on end-to-end RL, learning-augmented optimal control, and existing hierarchical RL approaches in both efficiency and decision quality.

AI.tldr: A hierarchical RL-OC framework that uses inverse optimization to align lower-level objectives with long-term task goals, outperforming existing methods in efficiency and decision quality.
AI.motivation: Existing hierarchical policies have limitations: RL-based methods struggle with constraint satisfaction, while optimal control approaches are often myopic and computationally expensive. Current hierarchical RL-OC architectures lack principled formulation of lower-level optimization, relying on heuristic objectives.
AI.method: Proposes a principled framework integrating upper-level goal abstraction with structured lower-level decision making. Uses inverse optimization to inform lower-level problem structure from expert demonstrations, ensuring lower-level objectives align with long-term task goals.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on network-based resource allocation and continuous collision avoidance tasks shows the method consistently outperforms end-to-end RL, learning-augmented optimal control, and existing hierarchical RL approaches in both efficiency and decision quality.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework successfully reconciles trade-offs in hierarchical decision-making by providing principled integration of RL and optimal control, demonstrating superior performance through systematic alignment of lower-level objectives with overall task goals.

title: HiComm: Hierarchical Communication for Multi-agent Reinforcement Learning
summary: Cooperative multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) often relies on communication to mitigate partial observability, yet most existing protocols treat messages as flat dense vectors detached from the structure of the observations they summarize. This design overlooks an important source of inductive bias in many cooperative environments, where observations naturally follow a hierarchy such as groups and entities. We propose \textsc{HiComm}, a plug-in communication module that grounds messages in the sender's hierarchical observation. \textsc{HiComm} is receiver-driven: the receiver issues a query, and the hierarchy is resolved through a three-stage decoding process that first selects a group, then a sender, and then an entity within that group, returning the corresponding feature slice as the message. This converts communication from unstructured vector transmission into structured information retrieval over the sender's observation hierarchy. We instantiate this mechanism with Straight-Through Gumbel-Softmax for differentiable discrete selection and a lightweight shared projection design that attaches to standard MARL pipelines. Experiments across cooperative MARL tasks with different observation structures and coordination demands show that \textsc{HiComm} matches or outperforms representative learned communication baselines while reducing communication volume by up to $23\times$ per receiver per episode.

AI.tldr: HiComm is a hierarchical communication module for cooperative multi-agent RL that converts communication from flat vector transmission into structured information retrieval over the sender's observation hierarchy, reducing communication volume by up to 23×.
AI.motivation: Current cooperative MARL communication protocols treat messages as flat dense vectors disconnected from the natural hierarchical structure of observations (like groups and entities), overlooking important inductive biases present in many cooperative environments.
AI.method: HiComm is a receiver-driven plug-in module where the receiver issues a query resolved through a three-stage decoding process: selects a group, then a sender within that group, then an entity within that group, returning the corresponding feature slice as the message. Uses Straight-Through Gumbel-Softmax for differentiable discrete selection and lightweight shared projection design.
AI.result: Experiments across cooperative MARL tasks with different observation structures and coordination demands show HiComm matches or outperforms representative learned communication baselines while reducing communication volume by up to 23× per receiver per episode.
AI.conclusion: Grounding communication in hierarchical observation structures rather than using flat vectors improves efficiency and performance in cooperative MARL, converting communication from unstructured vector transmission to structured information retrieval.

title: Generative Learning as a Tool to Improve Perception of Emotional Body Motion Expressions
summary: Emotional body motion expressions are an essential element of non-verbal communication. Effectively conveying these expressions through technology is of utmost importance, for example, with virtual reality avatars and in social robotics. Recent advances in generative models have opened new opportunities for advancing research on emotional body motion learning. However, generating accurate emotional expression representations is challenging, given the subtlety of emotional cues, individual variability, and cultural differences. We investigate whether a generative model can implicitly learn emotional body motions directly from culturally grounded motion-capture data, without explicit emotion-motion guidance. Using a dataset of emotional performances by 49 Japanese actors, we trained a Transformer-based generative model to generate expressive motions conditioned on 13 discrete emotion labels. We evaluate the generated motions from two perspectives: (1) an LSTM-based classifier to assess recognizability by machine observers, achieving a recognition accuracy of 22.80%, and (2) a human perception study with Japanese raters to assess alignment with human affective interpretations, yielding a recognition accuracy of 24.91%. Beyond these, we evaluate the utility of generative modeling for three practical tasks: augmenting emotion recognition models, extracting representative emotion-specific motion patterns, and synthesizing smooth transitions between emotion intensities. Our findings highlight the potential of implicit, data-driven generative modeling to enhance affective computing applications and our understanding of emotion expressions.

AI.tldr: A Transformer-based generative model learns emotional body motions from Japanese motion-capture data without explicit emotion guidance, achieving ~24% recognition accuracy in both machine and human evaluations, with applications in emotion recognition augmentation and motion synthesis.
AI.motivation: Emotional body motion expressions are crucial for non-verbal communication in technologies like VR avatars and social robotics, but generating accurate emotional expressions is challenging due to subtle cues, individual variability, and cultural differences.
AI.method: Used a dataset of emotional performances by 49 Japanese actors, trained a Transformer-based generative model to generate expressive motions conditioned on 13 discrete emotion labels without explicit emotion-motion guidance.
AI.result: Machine recognition accuracy of 22.80% using LSTM-based classifier, and human perception study with Japanese raters achieved 24.91% recognition accuracy. The model demonstrated utility for augmenting emotion recognition models, extracting emotion-specific motion patterns, and synthesizing smooth transitions between emotion intensities.
AI.conclusion: Generative models can implicitly learn emotional body motions from culturally grounded data, highlighting the potential of data-driven approaches to enhance affective computing applications and improve understanding of emotion expressions.

title: Flow Reasoning Models: Scaling Reasoning Through Iterative Self-Refinement
summary: Discrete flow models have recently shown promising performance on few-step text generation; however, when naively applied to structured reasoning tasks such as Sudoku and Zebra puzzles, they converge confidently to incorrect answers (solving only $\sim$36% of Sudoku puzzles). We introduce Flow Reasoning Models (FRMs), a training and test-time-scaling framework for structured reasoning with flow models. We make the observation that, despite their poor solve rate, flow models can act as their own verifiers. A correct answer is a stable fixed point of the denoising dynamics, returning to itself when re-noised and re-solved. This enables a test-time-scaling paradigm: propose many candidate solutions and keep those that are dynamically stable, which alone reaches high solve rates on Sudoku-Shah (~$100\%$) and Zebra ($95.9\%$). This even generalizes to harder out-of-distribution puzzles like Sudoku-Extreme ($96.1\%$), without ever training on that distribution. This pure search, however, wastes a great deal of computation generating incorrect candidate solutions. We therefore design a training recipe to improve the base model's efficiency. First, we train flow models with a self-conditioning channel and close it at inference, letting them refine their own past predictions. Second, we train models to avoid their own failed generations using direct preference optimization. These changes substantially improve the base model's efficiency, letting it reach $99.2\%$ on Sudoku in just $7$ forward passes, over $8\times$ fewer than the strongest matched masked-diffusion baseline we compare needs for the same accuracy. When combined with test-time scaling, this lets flow models solve hard out-of-distribution puzzles (e.g. Sudoku-Extreme) far more efficiently.

AI.tldr: Flow Reasoning Models (FRMs) improve flow models' structured reasoning by using test-time scaling with dynamic stability verification and training enhancements like self-conditioning and DPO, achieving near-perfect solve rates on puzzles like Sudoku and Zebra.
AI.motivation: Discrete flow models perform poorly on structured reasoning tasks like Sudoku and Zebra puzzles (only ~36% solve rate), confidently converging to incorrect answers despite their few-step generation capabilities.
AI.method: 1) Test-time scaling: Generate multiple candidate solutions and filter using dynamic stability (correct answers are stable fixed points when re-noised/re-solved). 2) Training enhancements: Add self-conditioning channel that closes at inference for refinement, and use direct preference optimization to avoid failed generations.
AI.result: Achieves near-perfect solve rates: Sudoku-Shah (~100%), Zebra (95.9%), and Sudoku-Extreme (96.1% out-of-distribution). Combined approach reaches 99.2% Sudoku solve rate in just 7 forward passes, 8× fewer than baselines.
AI.conclusion: FRMs effectively combine test-time scaling with improved training to make flow models efficient and accurate for structured reasoning, showing strong generalization to out-of-distribution puzzles without additional training.

title: On design-unbiased algorithmic Machine Learning
summary: Machine Learning (ML) algorithms, such as k-Nearest Neighbours (kNN) or random forest, eschew the ideal of true data models in favour of predictive performance. However, minimising the MSE or F-score cannot lead to unbiasedness directly, which is important in many situations such as official statistics. We study the conditions of algorithmic ML, other than the existence and knowledge of true data models, which lead to unbiased prediction or classification for a given finite population, including how the training data may be sampled from the population, how a trained prediction algorithm can be tuned to achieve unbiased prediction or classification for that population, and how the performance of out-of-sample prediction or classification can be assessed unbiasedly. The inference is based on the known probability design of samples and training sets, rather than any assumed distributions or models.

AI.tldr: This paper examines conditions for achieving unbiased predictions in machine learning algorithms without assuming true data models, focusing on finite populations and design-based inference.
AI.motivation: Machine learning algorithms prioritize predictive performance over true data models, but unbiasedness is crucial in contexts like official statistics where MSE or F-score minimization alone doesn't guarantee unbiased predictions.
AI.method: The study analyzes conditions for unbiased ML predictions/classification for finite populations, examining: 1) how training data is sampled from populations, 2) how prediction algorithms can be tuned for unbiasedness, and 3) how out-of-sample performance can be assessed unbiasedly. Uses design-based inference based on known probability sampling designs rather than assumed distributions or models.
AI.result: The paper establishes conditions under which ML algorithms (like kNN and random forest) can achieve unbiased predictions or classification for finite populations when proper sampling designs are used and algorithms are appropriately tuned, without relying on assumed data models.
AI.conclusion: Unbiased ML predictions are achievable for finite populations through proper sampling design and algorithmic tuning using design-based inference, providing a framework for trustworthy ML in official statistics and other applications requiring unbiasedness.

title: Pooled Leaderboards Hide System-Specific Winners: A Reporting-Protocol Audit of Offline Root-Cause Analysis Benchmarks
summary: Offline root-cause-analysis (RCA) benchmarks commonly rank methods by a single pooled top-1 accuracy across multiple subsystems, and engineers often read the pooled winner as a recommendation for their own subsystem. We audit that reading on three public RCA benchmark families -- OpenRCA, RCAEval, and PetShop -- covering 11 subsystems and 778 matched scoring units. To keep pairwise comparisons on identical cases, the main analysis retains four methods or comparators with complete coverage: BARO, a CD-1min adapter, max-$|Z|$, and per-service alert-count. All six pairwise comparisons show subsystem-level effects of both signs, every random-effects 95\% prediction interval crosses zero, and case-level interaction tests reject exchangeability in 5 of 6 pairs. Leave-one-system-out selection picks the lower-scoring method on up to 5 of 11 held-out subsystems, with regret reaching 24.8 pp on RCAEval / Sock-Shop. We release a 320-line audit module; given a matched RCA benchmark score table, it recomputes the same per-subsystem stability checks alongside pooled scores.

AI.tldr: RCA benchmarks using pooled accuracy rankings don't generalize well across subsystems - the top method varies significantly by subsystem, and pooled winners often perform worse on specific subsystems.
AI.motivation: Current offline root-cause-analysis (RCA) benchmarks rank methods by pooled top-1 accuracy across multiple subsystems, and engineers often interpret these rankings as recommendations for their own subsystems. However, it's unclear whether pooled winners actually perform best on individual subsystems.
AI.method: The authors audit this issue on three public RCA benchmark families (OpenRCA, RCAEval, PetShop) covering 11 subsystems and 778 matched scoring units. They analyze four methods with complete coverage (BARO, CD-1min adapter, max-|Z|, per-service alert-count) using pairwise comparisons, random-effects models, and leave-one-system-out selection to examine subsystem-level effects.
AI.result: All six pairwise comparisons show subsystem-level effects of both signs, all random-effects 95% prediction intervals cross zero, and case-level interaction tests reject exchangeability in 5 of 6 pairs. Leave-one-system-out selection picks the lower-scoring method on up to 5 of 11 held-out subsystems, with regret reaching 24.8 percentage points on RCAEval/Sock-Shop.
AI.conclusion: Pooled accuracy rankings in RCA benchmarks don't reliably indicate which method performs best on specific subsystems. The authors release a 320-line audit module that recomputes per-subsystem stability checks alongside pooled scores to help practitioners make better method selections for their specific contexts.

title: HARD-KV: Head-Adaptive Regularization for Decoding-time KV Compression
summary: Long-context LLM inference faces a fundamental conflict: head-adaptive compression algorithms (e.g., Top-$p$ nucleus sampling) offer superior accuracy by dynamically fluctuating memory budgets, yet modern inference engines (e.g., vLLM) demand rigid, static memory patterns to leverage CUDA Graphs and PagedAttention. We resolve this ``Static-Dynamic'' mismatch with HARD-KV, a unified framework that that bridges dynamic selection with rigid system constraints. HARD-KV introduces a Cascade Cache hierarchy, managing the token lifecycle across dense, sparse, and condensed tiers. Crucially, we propose a Logits Calibration mechanism that normalizes diverse importance metrics into a unified probability space, enabling consistent Top-$p$ budgeting across heterogeneous heads. To bridge the efficiency gap, we offer a system-level solution, which rewrites fragmented, dynamic indices into contiguous physical layouts compatible with high-performance inference engine. Extensive experiments on math-reasoning benchmarks (AIME, U-Math) verify that HARD-KV achieves up to 2$\times$ throughput improvement over static baselines while maintaining high-fidelity generation in 10k+ token scenarios. Code is available at https://github.com/SuDIS-ZJU/HARDInfer.

AI.tldr: HARD-KV is a unified framework that bridges dynamic head-adaptive KV cache compression with static inference engine requirements, enabling efficient long-context LLM inference while maintaining accuracy.
AI.motivation: There's a fundamental conflict in long-context LLM inference: head-adaptive compression algorithms (like Top-p sampling) need dynamic memory budgets for optimal accuracy, but modern inference engines (like vLLM) require rigid, static memory patterns to leverage CUDA Graphs and PagedAttention efficiently.
AI.method: HARD-KV introduces: 1) A Cascade Cache hierarchy managing tokens across dense, sparse, and condensed tiers; 2) Logits Calibration mechanism that normalizes diverse importance metrics into unified probability space for consistent Top-p budgeting; 3) System-level solution that rewrites fragmented dynamic indices into contiguous physical layouts compatible with high-performance inference engines.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on math-reasoning benchmarks (AIME, U-Math) show HARD-KV achieves up to 2× throughput improvement over static baselines while maintaining high-fidelity generation in 10k+ token scenarios.
AI.conclusion: HARD-KV successfully resolves the "Static-Dynamic" mismatch in long-context LLM inference, enabling dynamic head-adaptive KV cache compression to work with modern inference engines' static requirements, significantly improving throughput without sacrificing accuracy.

title: Direct Causation in International Humanitarian Law and the Challenge of AI-Mediated Civilian Cyber Operations
comment: 11 pages, 1 figure, Workshop on Technical AI Governance Research ICML 2026
summary: International humanitarian law protects civilians from direct attack unless and for such time as they take direct part in hostilities, with the ICRC's 2009 Interpretive Guidance operationalising this rule through a three-criterion cumulative test. This paper argues that AI-mediated civilian cyber operations challenge the direct causation element of this test in a structurally specific way: when a civilian deploys an autonomous multi-agent cyber system of the kind recently demonstrated in offensive AI research, the "one causal step" standard fails because harm is produced by system-generated decisions made after human disengagement, and the integral-part requirement does not extend because it presupposes downstream human contributors whose conduct can be independently classified. The framework therefore defaults to treating such deployments as indirect participation, in tension with its purpose of capturing civilians who personally take part in hostilities. Beyond the doctrinal analysis, this paper identifies goal-specification granularity as the property on which the integral-part test's concreteness component implicitly turns, classifies AI-mediated operations along a five-level spectrum, and argues that existing technical AI governance instruments do not log or report this property.

AI.tldr: AI-mediated civilian cyber operations challenge international humanitarian law's direct participation test because autonomous systems create causal chains that break the "one causal step" standard and integral-part requirements.
AI.motivation: The paper addresses how recent advances in offensive AI research, particularly autonomous multi-agent cyber systems deployed by civilians, create structural challenges to existing legal frameworks for determining when civilians directly participate in hostilities under international humanitarian law.
AI.method: The paper employs doctrinal analysis of the ICRC's 2009 Interpretive Guidance three-criterion cumulative test for direct participation in hostilities, examining how AI-mediated operations structurally challenge the direct causation element. It develops a conceptual framework classifying AI-mediated operations along a five-level spectrum and analyzes goal-specification granularity as a key property.
AI.result: The analysis shows that AI-mediated civilian cyber operations challenge the "one causal step" standard because harm results from system-generated decisions after human disengagement, and the integral-part requirement fails to extend as it presupposes downstream human contributors. The framework defaults to treating such deployments as indirect participation, creating tension with its original purpose. The paper also identifies that existing AI governance instruments do not log or report the critical property of goal-specification granularity.
AI.conclusion: Current international humanitarian law frameworks are inadequate for addressing AI-mediated civilian cyber operations, as autonomous systems create causal gaps that escape the direct participation test. The paper calls for doctrinal evolution to address these structural challenges and highlights the need for technical AI governance instruments to incorporate goal-specification granularity reporting.

title: Active Quantum Kernel Acquisition for Gaussian Process Regression
summary: Quantum kernel estimation on near-term hardware is shot-budgeted: every entry of the kernel Gram matrix is a Bernoulli expectation that must be sampled with a finite number of circuit executions. Recent work on quantum kernel classification has shown that allocating shots non-uniformly across kernel entries, weighted by their downstream task sensitivity, can reduce the shot budget required to reach a target accuracy. We extend this idea to Gaussian process (GP) regression, a setting whose downstream quantities (full-spectrum posterior variance, log-determinant, marginal likelihood) couple to kernel error more tightly than the sign-only outputs of classification. We derive three closed-form pair-level sensitivities predictive coupling $|α_iα_j|$, leave-one-out residual, and marginal-likelihood gradient and plug them into a Neyman-style minimum-variance allocation rule. To prevent catastrophic over-concentration when the warm-up sensitivity estimate is itself noisy, we add a high uniform coverage floor justified by a Frobenius lower bound on the missing-entry perturbation. On four UCI benchmarks and two synthetic RBF + Bernoulli controlled studies, the resulting allocator delivers $10$--$21\%$ test-RMSE improvement over uniform allocation across the moderate-budget regime. The gain transfers (i) to genuine ZZ and Pauli-Z quantum kernels on quantum-natural data ($-13$--$15\%$ at low budget, $p<0.05$ paired) and (ii) to four downstream tasks (Bayesian quadrature, heteroscedastic regression, hyperparameter learning, multi-output Cokriging). On UCI features embedded into a ZZ kernel the gain disappears, consistent with the exponential-concentration regime where shot allocation has nothing to exploit.

AI.tldr: Quantum kernel estimation with non-uniform shot allocation extends from classification to Gaussian process regression, achieving 10-21% RMSE improvement through sensitivity-based optimization with protection against over-concentration.
AI.motivation: Quantum kernel estimation on near-term hardware is shot-budgeted, requiring finite circuit executions for kernel matrix entries. Previous work showed non-uniform allocation works for classification, but GP regression has more tightly coupled downstream quantities (posterior variance, log-determinant, marginal likelihood) that need better error management.
AI.method: Derived three closed-form pair-level sensitivities (predictive coupling |α_iα_j|, leave-one-out residual, marginal-likelihood gradient) and used them in a Neyman-style minimum-variance allocation rule. Added a high uniform coverage floor based on Frobenius lower bound to prevent over-concentration from noisy sensitivity estimates.
AI.result: On four UCI benchmarks and two synthetic RBF + Bernoulli studies, the allocator achieved 10-21% test-RMSE improvement over uniform allocation. Gains transferred to genuine quantum kernels on quantum-natural data (-13-15% at low budget, p<0.05) and to four downstream tasks. No gain on UCI features embedded in ZZ kernel due to exponential concentration.
AI.conclusion: Sensitivity-based non-uniform shot allocation effectively improves quantum Gaussian process regression performance when downstream quantities are tightly coupled to kernel error, but benefits disappear in exponential-concentration regimes where there's nothing to exploit.

title: Selective Memory Retention for Long-Horizon LLM Agents
comment: Accepted at the International Conference on Machine Learning (ICML) 2026
summary: When does retention matter for memory-augmented LLM agents?
