Coupling the framework with experimentally calibrated damage laws demonstrates that fiber orientation, clustering, and porosity collectively govern local effective stiffness. The approach provides a physics-informed, data-efficient pathway to identify mechanically weak microstructural cells and accelerate digital-twin development for SFT components.

AI.tldr: A data-driven surrogate model using GNN-LSTM hybrid architecture accurately predicts mechanical behavior of short-fiber thermoplastic composites with 100x computational speedup and high accuracy (R²≈0.98).
AI.motivation: Short-fiber thermoplastic composites are widely used in aerospace/automotive but their mechanical behavior is complex due to mesoscale interactions (fiber orientation, clustering, porosity). Traditional mesoscale finite element models are computationally intractable for realistic 3D microstructures.
AI.method: Proposed data-driven surrogate framework: 1) Reconstruct microstructures from micro-CT data, 2) Discretize into Voronoi cells representing fiber-interaction neighborhoods, 3) Homogenize cells via nonlinear FE simulations with matrix damage, 4) Train hybrid Graph Neural Network-LSTM architecture encoding microstructural topology and history-dependent evolution.
AI.result: Surrogate accurately predicts stiffness and stress-strain behavior of unseen microstructures with R²≈0.98 relative to high-fidelity FE simulations, achieving over two orders-of-magnitude reduction in computational cost. Framework identifies that fiber orientation, clustering, and porosity collectively govern local effective stiffness.
AI.conclusion: The approach provides a physics-informed, data-efficient pathway to identify mechanically weak microstructural cells and accelerate digital-twin development for short-fiber thermoplastic components.

title: LLM-Guided Planning for Multi-hop Reasoning over Multimodal Nuclear Regulatory Documents
comment: Accepted at the Second Workshop on Agents in the Wild: Safety, Security, and Beyond @ ICML 2026. 8 pages (main), 3 figures, 1 algorithm
summary: Reviewing nuclear regulatory documents requires multi-hop reasoning across tens of thousands of pages, where judgments depend on evidence assembled across multiple chapters. We frame this task as planning: an LLM-based agent observes the evidence collected so far, picks the next document fragment to inspect, and stops when the evidence is sufficient. The agent operates over a vectorless document tree using browse, read, and search tools, and maintains a dynamic knowledge graph (KG) as state. On a 200-question benchmark over NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report (FSAR) documents, the system reaches 81.5% accuracy with a RAGAS Faithfulness of 0.93. The dominant performance factor is planning: against PageIndex, which uses the same document tree without state-conditioned action selection, the gap is +38.0pp (43.5% to 81.5%, p<0.001). The system also outperforms LightRAG (73.0%, p<0.05), HippoRAG (70.5%, p<0.01), and GraphRAG (49.5%, p<0.001), and matches RAPTOR (75.5%, p=0.11) without offline indexing. Edge inference adds 2.8x cost without raising accuracy; we retain it as a traceability module. Of 7,391 inferred edges, 3 Violates edges (0.04%) flag scope boundaries (Q058) and partial conformance (Q176) as typed annotations that a human reviewer can audit.

AI.tldr: An LLM-based agent system for nuclear regulatory document review uses planning and dynamic knowledge graphs to achieve 81.5% accuracy on a 200-question benchmark, significantly outperforming baseline methods through intelligent state-conditioned action selection.
AI.motivation: Reviewing nuclear regulatory documents requires multi-hop reasoning across tens of thousands of pages where judgments depend on evidence assembled across multiple chapters, presenting a complex information retrieval and reasoning challenge.
AI.method: Frames the task as planning: an LLM-based agent observes collected evidence, picks the next document fragment to inspect, and stops when evidence is sufficient. Uses browse, read, and search tools over a vectorless document tree while maintaining a dynamic knowledge graph as state.
AI.result: Achieves 81.5% accuracy with RAGAS Faithfulness of 0.93 on a 200-question benchmark over NuScale FSAR documents. Outperforms PageIndex (+38.0pp), LightRAG, HippoRAG, and GraphRAG, and matches RAPTOR without offline indexing. Planning is identified as the dominant performance factor.
AI.conclusion: The planning-based approach with dynamic knowledge graphs enables effective multi-hop reasoning for nuclear regulatory document review, with planning being the key performance factor. Edge inference serves as a traceability module for human auditing, though it adds significant cost without improving accuracy.

title: How Far Can Sharpness and Complexity Jointly Explain Generalization?
summary: Sharpness and complexity are two central factors in the generalization analysis of deep neural networks. Existing quantitative evaluations of generalization measures have largely focused on individual scalar measures, leaving the joint explanatory power of sharpness and complexity largely unexplored. This work studies how far sharpness and complexity can jointly explain generalization. We use linear regression and introduce a Pareto-based analysis to quantitatively evaluate the joint explanatory power of these two factors. Beyond the existing parameter-level definitions, we further propose realizations of sharpness and complexity that are closer to function space and less dependent on raw parameter representations. We find that function-oriented definitions of these two quantities expand the explanatory scope of the two-factor view beyond what is achieved by existing parameter-level metrics. Overall, our results support the sharpness-complexity perspective as an informative lens for understanding generalization across diverse settings. At the same time, the remaining failures indicate that whether this two-factor view can serve as a complete theory of generalization remains open.

AI.tldr: Sharpness and complexity jointly explain generalization better than individual measures, with function-oriented definitions outperforming parameter-level metrics.
AI.motivation: Existing generalization analysis focuses on individual scalar measures, leaving the joint explanatory power of sharpness and complexity largely unexplored, despite both being central factors in deep neural network generalization.
AI.method: Use linear regression and introduce Pareto-based analysis to quantitatively evaluate joint explanatory power; propose function-oriented definitions of sharpness and complexity (closer to function space, less dependent on parameter representations) beyond existing parameter-level definitions.
AI.result: Function-oriented definitions expand explanatory scope beyond parameter-level metrics; joint analysis supports sharpness-complexity as an informative lens for understanding generalization across diverse settings; remaining failures indicate limitations of the two-factor view as a complete theory.
AI.conclusion: The sharpness-complexity perspective is informative for generalization analysis but may not be a complete theory; function-oriented definitions provide better explanatory power than parameter-level metrics.

title: MOSAIC: Orchestrating Collaborative Knowledge Tracing with Hierarchical Semantic Alignment
summary: Knowledge Tracing (KT) is important for personalized education but traditionally suffers from two key limitations: a reliance on shallow ID-based representations that neglect semantic depth and a restriction to single-granularity mastery estimation that overlooks hierarchical knowledge dependencies. To address these challenges, we propose MOSAIC (Multi-granularity Online Semantic AI for Collaborative Knowledge), a novel framework that orchestrates LLM-driven semantic alignment with sequential modeling. Unlike methods that use LLMs solely as predictors, MOSAIC leverages a frozen LLM to generate dynamic, context-aware embeddings and hierarchical prediction prompts, explicitly capturing collaborative signals and peer interactions. Furthermore, we introduce a cross-granularity consistency objective that jointly regularizes mastery estimation across concept, topic-cluster, and global proficiency levels. Extensive experiments on ASSISTments, EdNet, and a newly collected large-scale MOOC dataset demonstrate that MOSAIC establishes new state-of-the-art results. Specifically, our method achieves AUC improvements of up to 3.4\% and Accuracy gains of up to 2.5 \% across all benchmarks. Notably, MOSAIC exhibits superior robustness in collaboration-rich environments and long-sequence scenarios (AUC 0.862 on MOOC), offering both high predictive precision and semantically grounded interpretability.

AI.tldr: MOSAIC is a novel knowledge tracing framework that combines LLM-driven semantic alignment with multi-granularity mastery estimation, achieving state-of-the-art performance by addressing limitations of traditional ID-based representations and single-level mastery modeling.
AI.motivation: Traditional Knowledge Tracing suffers from two key limitations: reliance on shallow ID-based representations that lack semantic depth, and restriction to single-granularity mastery estimation that overlooks hierarchical knowledge dependencies. These limitations hinder accurate personalized education systems.
AI.method: MOSAIC orchestrates LLM-driven semantic alignment with sequential modeling. It uses a frozen LLM to generate dynamic, context-aware embeddings and hierarchical prediction prompts, explicitly capturing collaborative signals and peer interactions. Introduces a cross-granularity consistency objective that jointly regularizes mastery estimation across concept, topic-cluster, and global proficiency levels.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on ASSISTments, EdNet, and a newly collected large-scale MOOC dataset show MOSAIC establishes new state-of-the-art results. Achieves AUC improvements up to 3.4% and Accuracy gains up to 2.5% across all benchmarks. Shows superior robustness in collaboration-rich environments and long-sequence scenarios (AUC 0.862 on MOOC), offering high predictive precision and semantically grounded interpretability.
AI.conclusion: MOSAIC effectively addresses traditional KT limitations by integrating semantic understanding through LLMs with multi-granularity modeling. The framework demonstrates significant performance improvements while providing interpretable, semantically rich representations suitable for real-world educational applications in collaborative and large-scale environments.

title: FADE: Mitigating Hallucinations by Reducing Language-Prior Dominance in Large Vision-Language Models
summary: Despite the impressive capabilities of Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs), they remain susceptible to hallucination, generating content inconsistent with the input image. Recent studies attribute this to the dominance of language priors over visual inputs and employ contrastive decoding methods to mitigate this dominance, but the mechanistic origin remains unexplored. We investigate the information flow through each transformer layer and find that attention modules consistently aggregate visual evidence, while FFN modules at critical layers act as the source of language priors. These priors can override visual evidence, causing correct predictions in intermediate layers to drift toward incorrect outputs. Based on this insight, we propose FADE (FFN Attenuation for DEcoding), a training-free method that attenuates FFN outputs to reduce language-prior dominance. Evaluations on POPE, CHAIR, and MME benchmarks across LLaVA-1.5, mPLUG-Owl2, and InstructBLIP show that FADE effectively mitigates hallucinations while preserving inference efficiency.

AI.tldr: FADE is a training-free method that mitigates hallucinations in Large Vision-Language Models by attenuating FFN outputs to reduce language-prior dominance, preserving visual evidence in attention modules.
AI.motivation: Large Vision-Language Models suffer from hallucination problems where they generate content inconsistent with input images, likely due to language priors dominating over visual inputs. While contrastive decoding methods have been used, the mechanistic origin of this issue remains unexplored.
AI.method: Investigated information flow through transformer layers, found attention modules aggregate visual evidence while FFN modules at critical layers act as sources of language priors. Proposed FADE (FFN Attenuation for DEcoding) - a training-free method that attenuates FFN outputs to reduce language-prior dominance without affecting inference efficiency.
AI.result: Evaluations on POPE, CHAIR, and MME benchmarks across LLaVA-1.5, mPLUG-Owl2, and InstructBLIP show FADE effectively mitigates hallucinations while preserving inference efficiency.
AI.conclusion: Language priors originate from FFN modules and can override visual evidence, causing correct intermediate predictions to drift toward incorrect outputs. Attenuating FFN outputs (FADE) is an effective, training-free solution to reduce hallucination in LVLMs.

title: A Kernel Fisher Discriminant Analysis-Based Tree Ensemble Classifier: KFDA Forest
comment: 11 pages, 4 figures, 6 tables; author-created manuscript version of the published article
summary: In general, an ensemble classifier is more accurate than a single classifier. In this study, we propose an ensemble classifier called the kernel Fisher discriminant analysis forest (KFDA Forest), which is a tree-based ensemble method that applies KFDA. To promote diversity, bootstrap is used, and variable sets are randomly divided into K subsets. KFDA is performed on each subset to increase classification accuracy. KFDA maximizes the distance between classes while minimizing the distance within classes. KFDA can also be applied to classification problems in a nonlinear data structure using the kernel trick because it can transform the input space into a kernel feature space, commonly named a rotation, rather than performing a dimensionality reduction. Because new feature axes and KFDA projections are parallel, decision trees are used as a base classifier. To compare the proposed method with existing ensemble methods, we apply these to real datasets from the UCI and KEEL repositories.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes KFDA Forest, a tree-based ensemble classifier that applies Kernel Fisher Discriminant Analysis with bootstrapping and random feature subset splitting to improve classification accuracy and diversity.
AI.motivation: The motivation is that ensemble classifiers generally outperform single classifiers, and the authors aim to develop a novel ensemble method that combines the strengths of tree-based ensembles with Kernel Fisher Discriminant Analysis for better classification performance, particularly in nonlinear data structures.
AI.method: The method involves: 1) Using bootstrapping to create diverse training subsets, 2) Randomly dividing variable sets into K subsets, 3) Applying Kernel Fisher Discriminant Analysis (KFDA) on each subset, which maximizes between-class distance while minimizing within-class distance using kernel trick for nonlinear data, 4) Using decision trees as base classifiers because new feature axes and KFDA projections are parallel.
AI.result: The proposed KFDA Forest method was evaluated on real datasets from UCI and KEEL repositories and compared with existing ensemble methods, though specific numerical results are not provided in the abstract.
AI.conclusion: KFDA Forest represents a novel ensemble approach that effectively combines kernel methods with ensemble learning, particularly suitable for nonlinear classification problems by leveraging the kernel trick while maintaining diversity through bootstrapping and random feature splitting.

title: How Much Due Diligence Before You Bid? Learning in Intractable Takeover Auctions
comment: 21 pages, 13 figures, 2 tables. Code and data: https://github.com/zainnab-sparq/imperfect-information-deal-games
summary: When two companies bid to buy the same target, no one knows exactly what the target is worth. Each bidder pays for due diligence: costly, imperfect homework that sharpens its own private estimate before it bids. How much of that homework is worth buying? We build a simple computer model of the bidding contest and let it teach itself to bid well by playing against itself, the way a game engine learns chess. The economic question, how much diligence pays for itself, and the computational question, when the contest becomes too complex to solve exactly, are both controlled by a single thing: how many pieces of private information a bidder carries. Our main finding is that the right amount of diligence is modest and finite. It falls as diligence gets more expensive, and it falls further when both sides are doing their homework, because competition erodes the value of knowing more. We also test a recent claim from AI research: that simple, general self-play methods can rival the specialized, expensive algorithms usually built for games like these. Running on an ordinary laptop with no costly frontier AI, we find the simple methods are the best of the self-learning approaches, though purpose-built exact methods still win whenever the game is small enough to solve outright. The simple methods earn their keep only once the game grows too large to solve exactly, which is the regime real deals live in, and there we show they still find strong bidding strategies. The contribution is threefold: a cheap, reproducible way to study deal-making under uncertainty; a concrete, model-based answer to how much due diligence is worth buying; and evidence about when lightweight, general-purpose AI is good enough to replace specialized methods. We release all the games, code, and experiments.

AI.tldr: A computational study using self-play AI methods to determine optimal due diligence spending in corporate bidding contests, finding that modest, finite diligence is optimal and competition reduces its value.
AI.motivation: When companies bid for acquisitions, they conduct costly due diligence to refine their value estimates, but there's uncertainty about how much diligence is worth buying. The research aims to understand the optimal amount of due diligence in competitive bidding situations under uncertainty.
AI.method: Built a computer model of bidding contests and used self-play methods (similar to how game engines learn chess) where the AI plays against itself to learn optimal bidding strategies. Compared simple, general self-play methods against specialized exact algorithms, testing on an ordinary laptop without expensive frontier AI.
AI.result:
1) Optimal due diligence is modest and finite, decreasing as diligence costs rise and competition intensifies (competition erodes value of superior information).
2) Simple self-play methods perform best among self-learning approaches when games are too large for exact solutions (real-world scenario).
3) Exact methods win for small solvable games, but simple methods are effective in complex, real-world-sized games.
AI.conclusion: The paper provides three contributions: a reproducible computational approach for studying deal-making under uncertainty, a model-based answer showing optimal diligence is modest and decreases with competition, and evidence that lightweight, general-purpose AI can replace specialized methods in complex real-world scenarios.

title: When Can Conformal Risk Control Certify LLM Outputs? Bounds, Impossibility, and Adaptation for Structured Generation
summary: Large language models (LLMs) deployed for structured generation (NER, JSON extraction, QA, and classification) lack formal reliability guarantees, and standard heuristic abstention policies miss user-specified risk targets by 7.5--12.5%. We characterize when conformal risk control (CRC) can certify structured LLM outputs and when it provably cannot. First, we prove an impossibility result: when the base risk (μ> α), any distribution-free method must abstain on at least ((μ-α)/(1-α)) examples, yielding a closed-form feasibility test: one can check whether CRC will work before running it. Second, we analyze a certification hierarchy across Hoeffding, empirical Bernstein, and a betting-based e-CRC bound, with strict gains in low-variance/large-sample regimes: the Hoeffding-to-Bernstein step delivers the largest gain (+37% certified configurations), while e-CRC adds value when calibration data is scarce (10% certification at 20% data versus 0% for Hoeffding). Third, we validate adaptive conformal inference (ACI) under cross-dataset shift, reducing risk-target violations from 71% to 21%, with residual failures concentrated exactly where the impossibility bound predicts. Across six open-weight models (3B--72B parameters), eight datasets, four tasks, and six nonconformity scores, hard NER/QA/CLS configurations are uncertifiable at (α= 0.10); relaxing to (α= 0.30--0.40) unlocks practical certification (47% NER, 40% QA, 60% CLS). The framework gives a three-step deployment recipe: check feasibility, select the bound and score, then mitigate shift.

AI.tldr: The paper provides a formal reliability framework for structured LLM outputs, showing when conformal risk control can work, when it provably cannot, and offering practical deployment guidance with validated performance improvements.
AI.motivation: Current LLM deployments for structured generation tasks (NER, JSON extraction, QA, classification) lack formal reliability guarantees, and standard heuristic abstention policies often miss user-specified risk targets by significant margins (7.5-12.5%). There's a need for provable reliability certification methods.
AI.method: 1) Proves an impossibility theorem showing when distribution-free conformal risk control (CRC) cannot work; 2) Analyzes a certification hierarchy with Hoeffding, empirical Bernstein, and betting-based e-CRC bounds; 3) Validates adaptive conformal inference under dataset shift; 4) Evaluates across six models (3B-72B parameters), eight datasets, four tasks, six nonconformity scores.
AI.result: Hard configurations (α=0.10) are uncertifiable for NER/QA/CLS tasks, but relaxing to α=0.30-0.40 unlocks practical certification (47% NER, 40% QA, 60% CLS). e-CRC provides value with scarce calibration data (10% certification at 20% data vs 0% for Hoeffding). ACI reduces risk-target violations from 71% to 21% under dataset shift.
AI.conclusion: The framework offers a three-step deployment recipe: check feasibility via the impossibility test, select appropriate bounds and nonconformity scores based on data characteristics, and mitigate dataset shift. Formal guarantees are possible but depend on achievable risk levels relative to user targets.

title: Agent-Computer Observation Interfaces Enable Dynamic Computer Use
summary:
SWE-agent established the action interface as an underexplored design axis for software-engineering agents; we make the analogous case for the observation interface in computer-use (CU) agents. Current CU agents, closed and open-source alike, tie observation to action--one screenshot every 3-5 s, no audio--leaving them blind and deaf between screenshots to video, animations, transient UI events, meetings, and spoken instructions. We introduce the Agent-Computer Observation Interface (AOI), a model-agnostic perception layer that decouples continuous, adaptive observation from discrete actions through three gated components: inter-step keyframe capture, volume-gated audio transcription, and CU-model-generated visual narration that persists as text. Each produces almost nothing on static, silent content, reducing to the standard loop without degrading it.
  On DynaCU-Bench (100 dynamic browser tasks plus a 50-task static control), CU models from 7B to frontier scale gain +17 to +48 pp over their screenshot baselines with zero retraining, turning tasks that are near-impossible from periodic screenshots into largely solved ones. The gap is starkest on audio: on a spoken-content subset AOI agents solve every task, whereas streaming voice models hear accurately but cannot act on what they hear without the scaffold. The decomposition is as informative as the headline gain: keyframe selection turns out not to matter--the value comes from narrating captured frames into persistent text--and the interface is not a fixed bundle, since on a newer model (Gemini 3 Flash) the keyframe stream actively regresses through image-token dilution, so its components must be selected per model rather than shipped as one configuration.

AI.tldr: AOI (Agent-Computer Observation Interface) decouples observation from action in computer-use agents, using keyframe capture, audio transcription, and visual narration to solve dynamic tasks that are impossible with periodic screenshots.
AI.motivation: Current computer-use agents have poor observation interfaces tied to action timing (one screenshot every 3-5 seconds, no audio), making them blind to dynamic content like video, animations, transient UI events, meetings, and spoken instructions.
AI.method: AOI introduces three gated components: 1) inter-step keyframe capture, 2) volume-gated audio transcription, and 3) CU-model-generated visual narration that persists as text. The system reduces to standard screenshot loop on static, silent content.
AI.result: On DynaCU-Bench (150 tasks), CU models from 7B to frontier scale gained +17 to +48 percentage points over screenshot baselines. Audio performance was particularly strong - AOI agents solved every spoken-content task, while voice models alone couldn't act on what they heard.
AI.conclusion: AOI demonstrates that observation interface is a critical design axis for computer-use agents, with components needing per-model selection rather than fixed bundling. The key insight is narrating captured frames into persistent text matters more than keyframe selection itself.

title: Statistically Indistinguishable, Operationally Distinct: A Formal Barrier for Tabular Foundation Models
comment: Accepted at the 2nd ICML Workshop on Foundation Models for Structured Data, 2026
summary: Tabular foundation models cannot reason about data produced by running systems without access to the rules that govern them. We make this statement falsifiable. The \emph{Operational Turing Test} (OTT) constructs pairs of legal and rule-violating database states whose $1$- and $2$-way column-value marginals match to a total variation of $<0.02$; Le~Cam's lemma then bounds any values-only classifier at $\geq0.49$ Bayes error. Three values-only baselines (XGBoost, TabICL, TabPFN) hit the bound exactly (accuracy $0.50$, pre-registered two one-sided tests (TOST) $p<0.002$), raw row-level access does not help, exposing relational value consistency closes most of the gap, and only a classifier fed by seven executable rule-derived audits reaches $1.00$ classification accuracy. In three matched $100$-state frontier large-language-model (LLM) runs, models given the schema, trigger source, rule tables, and state files classify at most $2/50$ legal states as LEGAL; GPT-5.5 accepts $0/50$ legal states even with higher reasoning effort and a Structured Query Language (SQL) executor. The access-ladder pattern also appears on a second schema with structurally distinct rule families (banking ledger: cross-row balance, cumulative aggregate). The barrier is identifiability, not capacity: scale, data, and richer features cannot cross it without operational grounding.

AI.tldr: Tabular AI models fail to distinguish legal vs rule-violating database states that match statistical properties, proving they lack operational understanding of underlying business rules.
AI.motivation: Current tabular foundation models cannot reason about data from operational systems because they lack access to the underlying business rules that govern data relationships and constraints. The paper aims to make this limitation empirically falsifiable.
AI.method: The Operational Turing Test (OTT) constructs pairs of legal and rule-violating database states with matching 1- and 2-way column-value marginals (<0.02 total variation). Uses Le Cam's lemma to bound Bayes error. Tests values-only classifiers (XGBoost, TabICL, TabPFN), raw row-level access, relational value consistency, and rule-derived audits. Also tests frontier LLMs with schema, rules, and state files.
AI.result: Values-only classifiers hit the theoretical bound (0.50 accuracy). Raw row-level access doesn't help. Relational value consistency closes most gaps. Only rule-derived audits achieve 1.00 accuracy. LLMs fail dramatically - GPT-5.5 accepts 0/50 legal states even with SQL executor. Pattern replicates on banking ledger schema.
AI.conclusion: The fundamental barrier is identifiability, not capacity: scale, data, and richer features cannot overcome the lack of operational grounding. Models need access to executable business rules to reason about operational data validity.

title: Faults in Our Formal Benchmarking: Dataset Defects and Evaluation Failures in Lean Theorem Proving
summary: Benchmarks for LLM-assisted theorem proving in Lean are often treated as intrinsically reliable because every solved instance comes with a machine-checked proof. However, the kernel only checks that a proof establishes a \emph{formal} statement; it does not verify that the statement faithfully encodes the intended informal problem, nor that evaluation harnesses are robust to trivial or adversarial solutions. We audit five widely used Lean theorem-proving benchmarks and their forks, using corpus-scale static checkers to surface 4,833 findings, including 398 mechanically certified issues such as counterexamples, vacuous theorems, and unsound axioms. We also document semantic defects such as missing hypotheses, problem simplification, incomplete or incorrect translations, and Lean-specific specification hazards. Beyond dataset construction, we survey evaluation-time failure modes and show, on corrected subsets, that defects can both inflate and deflate reported prover scores. We propose a fault taxonomy, a suite of automated checkers and recall-oriented semantic audit prompts, and release standards to guide the creation of formal math datasets and to make evaluation more reproducible and trustworthy. Our checkers, audit prompts, and corrected dataset snapshots are available at https://github.com/Shashi456/atp-checkers.

AI.tldr: The paper audits Lean theorem-proving benchmarks, finding thousands of defects including certified issues like counterexamples and vacuous theorems, showing these can distort prover performance evaluations.
AI.motivation: While Lean theorem-proving benchmarks are considered reliable due to machine-checked proofs, the authors identify that formal correctness doesn't guarantee the statement faithfully encodes the intended informal problem or that evaluation harnesses are robust to trivial/adversarial solutions. They aim to assess the trustworthiness of these widely used benchmarks.
AI.method: The authors audit five widely used Lean theorem-proving benchmarks and their forks using corpus-scale static checkers to identify defects. They develop automated checkers, recall-oriented semantic audit prompts, and propose standards for formal math datasets.
AI.result: They surface 4,833 findings including 398 mechanically certified issues (counterexamples, vacuous theorems, unsound axioms) and semantic defects like missing hypotheses, problem simplification, incorrect translations, and Lean-specific specification hazards. On corrected subsets, defects are shown to both inflate and deflate reported prover scores.
AI.conclusion: Formal math benchmarks have significant reliability issues beyond formal verification. The authors propose a fault taxonomy, automated checkers, audit prompts, and release standards to guide creation of formal math datasets and make evaluation more reproducible and trustworthy.

title: Priced Motion Through Optimal Faces: A Normal-Fan Geometry for Non-Stationary Adversarial MDPs
summary: In a changing decision problem, standard dynamic-regret analyses have often equated the cost of non-stationarity to how far loss moves. However, it is simultaneously possible for a loss sequence to travel far and retain the same optimal policy, or for a small movement in loss to force the optimal policy to change completely. Thus, the size of the movement through loss variation, transition variation, or comparator path length describe the adversary's motion, but not the cost of that motion to the control problem. For a more faithful analytic interpretation, this paper develops a normal-fan geometry for finite-horizon adversarial MDPs with fixed transitions. Occupancy measures form a polytope, and each loss vector exposes an optimal face of that polytope. Non-stationarity in rewards is therefore a path through the normal fan, where motion inside one cone leaves the optimal face unchanged, while crossing a wall may carry regret. We pose the notion of a face-crossing price, which is the minimum regret incurred by remaining on the previous optimal face under the new loss. For any learner that tracks the previous face, dynamic regret decomposes exactly into intrinsic priced face motion plus within-face selection error. The resulting theory separates consequential from harmless non-stationarity, where loss variation can be arbitrarily large at zero price, and identical one-coordinate variation can hide horizon-scale differences in regret.

AI.tldr: The paper develops a geometric theory for adversarial MDPs where non-stationarity cost is measured by face-crossings in the normal fan of occupancy polytopes, not just loss variation.
AI.motivation: Standard dynamic-regret analyses measure non-stationarity cost through loss variation, but this doesn't capture the actual impact on optimal policies - loss can change dramatically without affecting optimal policies, or small changes can force complete policy shifts.
AI.method: Develops a normal-fan geometry for finite-horizon adversarial MDPs with fixed transitions: occupancy measures form a polytope, each loss vector exposes an optimal face, and non-stationarity becomes a path through the normal fan.
AI.result: Proposes "face-crossing price" concept - minimum regret incurred by staying on previous optimal face under new loss. Shows dynamic regret decomposes exactly into priced face motion plus within-face selection error.
AI.conclusion: The theory separates consequential from harmless non-stationarity, showing loss variation can be arbitrarily large at zero price, while identical coordinate variation can hide horizon-scale regret differences.

title: Cognitive World Models for Process-Level Social Influence Evaluation
summary: Social influence dialogue changes user behavior by altering internal cognitive states. The central evaluation question is whether the user's beliefs, desires, intentions, and emotions measurably change over the course of conversation, a process-oriented criterion that neither surface-level text metrics (BLEU/ROUGE) nor single-score LLM judgments can capture. We propose the \textbf{Cog}nitive \textbf{W}orld \textbf{M}odel \textbf{(CogWM)}, an LLM-based user model that reframes multi-turn dialogue evaluation from ``what did the user say'' to ``how did the user's internal cognitive state evolves.'' CogWM jointly predicts BDI/E cognitive states and user utterances and serves as both a user simulator and an evaluation platform, using a three-tier evaluation framework that covers turn-level fidelity, trajectory-level state dynamics, and task-level composite scoring. Trained via our \textbf{S}ummarize-\textbf{a}nd-\textbf{A}llocate \textbf{(SaA)} annotation pipeline on 150,454 user-turn samples across four social influence scenarios, CogWM achieves 77.6\% emotion accuracy (2.1$\times$ over GPT-5.5). In 3600 multi-agent discrimination trials, it distinguishes six commercial agents by their cognitive influence, with Llama-4-Scout ranking first (CTS +0.233). CogWM moves social influence dialogue evaluation from terminal judgment to process tracking. We have released our code\footnote{\scriptsize Code: https://github.com/lucianma05-create/CogWM} and models\footnote{Model: https://www.modelscope.cn/models/LucianMa/CogWM-14B}.

AI.tldr: CogWM is an LLM-based user model that evaluates social influence dialogues by tracking how users' internal cognitive states (beliefs, desires, intentions, emotions) evolve during conversations, rather than just analyzing surface-level text metrics.
AI.motivation: Existing dialogue evaluation methods (BLEU/ROUGE, single-score LLM judgments) fail to capture process-oriented changes in users' internal cognitive states during social influence dialogues, which is crucial for measuring actual behavior change.
AI.method: Developed CogWM, an LLM-based user model trained on 150,454 user-turn samples using the Summarize-and-Allocate annotation pipeline. It jointly predicts BDI/E cognitive states and user utterances, serving as both user simulator and evaluation platform with a three-tier framework covering turn-level fidelity, trajectory-level state dynamics, and task-level composite scoring.
AI.result: CogWM achieves 77.6% emotion accuracy (2.1× over GPT-5.5) and distinguishes six commercial agents by their cognitive influence in 3600 multi-agent discrimination trials, with Llama-4-Scout ranking first (CTS +0.233).
AI.conclusion: CogWM shifts social influence dialogue evaluation from terminal judgment to process tracking, providing a comprehensive framework for measuring how conversations actually change users' internal cognitive states and ultimately influence behavior.

title: DiLaServe: High SLO Attainment Serving for Diffusion Language Models
summary: Diffusion language models (DLMs) have recently emerged as a promising alternative to conventional autoregressive language models. By generating multiple tokens in parallel during each denoising step, they offer higher inference throughput while maintaining competitive quality. However, realizing these throughput gains while meeting latency SLOs in a serving system requires addressing challenges introduced by DLMs' unique characteristics. These include navigating the speed-quality tradeoff created by confidence-based denoising, choosing appropriate parallelization levels across model instances under fluctuating load, and coordinating approximate KV caching mechanisms that introduce non-uniform per-step costs. To address these challenges, we present DiLaServe, a cluster-level serving system for DLMs. DiLaServe enables deadline-aware scheduling and adaptive load control through confidence-threshold adjustment, and dynamically reconfigures the cluster by solving a quality-aware optimization problem, while explicitly modeling the step-level heterogeneity introduced by approximate KV caching. Across multiple benchmarks and real-world traces, DiLaServe improves SLO attainment by up to 56.6 percentage points and reduces end-to-end request latency by up to 46\% while incurring less than 1\% accuracy drop.

AI.tldr: DiLaServe: A cluster-level serving system for diffusion language models that optimizes throughput while meeting latency SLOs through confidence-threshold adjustment, adaptive load control, and modeling step-level heterogeneity from approximate KV caching.
AI.motivation: Diffusion language models offer higher inference throughput than autoregressive models by generating multiple tokens in parallel, but realizing these throughput gains while meeting latency SLOs in serving systems is challenging due to speed-quality tradeoffs from confidence-based denoising, fluctuating load requiring appropriate parallelization levels, and non-uniform per-step costs from approximate KV caching mechanisms.
AI.method: DiLaServe introduces a cluster-level serving system with deadline-aware scheduling and adaptive load control through confidence-threshold adjustment, dynamically reconfigures clusters by solving quality-aware optimization problems, and explicitly models step-level heterogeneity introduced by approximate KV caching.
AI.result: Across multiple benchmarks and real-world traces, DiLaServe improves SLO attainment by up to 56.6 percentage points, reduces end-to-end request latency by up to 46%, while incurring less than 1% accuracy drop.
AI.conclusion: DiLaServe successfully addresses the unique serving challenges of diffusion language models by providing an efficient cluster-level serving system that balances throughput, latency, and quality requirements, enabling practical deployment of DLMs in production environments.

title: UCOB: Learning to Utilize and Evolve Agentic Skills via Credit-Aware On-Policy Bidirectional Self-Distillation
summary: Skill memories can improve agentic reinforcement learning by reusing past experience as textual guidance, but retrieved skills are not oracular: they may help in one state while misleading the same policy in another. This makes the common privileged-teacher assumption fragile, namely that a skill-conditioned prompt can be treated as a fixed teacher for the no-skill prompt. We introduce UCOB, a framework for learning to utilize and evolve agentic skills via credit-aware on-policy bidirectional self-distillation. UCOB treats skill-conditioned and no-skill prompts as two on-policy context views of the same model, compares their return-to-go within the same task and anchor state, and uses the higher-return view as the local teacher. This local credit signal internalizes useful skill-conditioned behavior, corrects misleading skill usage, and guides task/state skill memory updates, utility-aware retrieval, and reflection self-training. Experiments on agentic tasks, including ALFWorld, WebShop, and Search-QA, show that UCOB outperforms skill-free RL, skill-memory baselines, and self-distillation methods across model scales, with up to 23.5 and 18.0 point gains over SOTA baselines on ALFWorld and WebShop. Ablations and analyses further validate its core mechanisms and efficiency.

AI.tldr: UCOB improves reinforcement learning by learning when to use retrieved skills through bidirectional self-distillation, using higher-return policy views as local teachers instead of assuming skills always help.
AI.motivation: Skill memories can improve RL but retrieved skills are not always helpful - they may help in one state while misleading in another. The common assumption that skill-conditioned prompts can be treated as fixed teachers is fragile, as skills aren't universally beneficial.
AI.method: UCOB uses credit-aware on-policy bidirectional self-distillation. It treats skill-conditioned and no-skill prompts as two on-policy context views of the same model, compares their return-to-go within the same task and anchor state, and uses the higher-return view as the local teacher. This internalizes useful skill behaviors, corrects misleading skill usage, and guides skill memory updates, retrieval, and reflection training.
AI.result: UCOB outperforms skill-free RL, skill-memory baselines, and self-distillation methods across model scales on agentic tasks (ALFWorld, WebShop, Search-QA), with up to 23.5 and 18.0 point gains over SOTA baselines on ALFWorld and WebShop.
AI.conclusion: UCOB's credit-aware bidirectional self-distillation effectively addresses the fragility of privileged-teacher assumptions by dynamically learning when skills help, enabling more robust skill utilization and evolution in agentic reinforcement learning.

title: Few-Step Boltzmann Generators via Scalable Likelihood Flow Maps
summary: Recent progress in flow-based generative modeling has led to models that output high-quality samples while using only a small number of function evaluations. However, at present, there is a lack of similar advances in estimating the model likelihood. In particular, most existing methods either rely on restrictive architectures that enable exact calculations, or use stochastic approximations such as Hutchinson's trace estimator that introduce substantial variance. In this work, we introduce SCAlable LikeLihood distillation of flOw maPs (SCALLOP). SCALLOP builds on the recently proposed F2D2, a likelihood flow map model that can generate samples and their densities in a small number of function evaluations. While F2D2 uses Hutchinson's estimator during training, we introduce an alternative and more scalable likelihood distillation objective that is Hutchinson-free and admits a vectorized formulation. Empirically, we demonstrate the effectiveness of SCALLOP as a Boltzmann generator in molecular science, and further validate its benefit on image datasets. SCALLOP significantly reduces both training variance and training time while consistently improving performance compared to F2D2, and is competitive with the state-of-the-art while achieving up to 10x inference speedup over the fastest baseline.

AI.tldr: SCALLOP introduces a Hutchinson-free likelihood distillation objective for flow-based generative models, reducing training variance/time while improving performance and achieving 10x inference speedup over baselines.
AI.motivation: Current flow-based generative models excel at fast sampling but struggle with efficient likelihood estimation. Most methods either use restrictive architectures for exact calculations or rely on Hutchinson's trace estimator which introduces high variance.
AI.method: SCALLOP builds on F2D2 (a likelihood flow map model) but replaces Hutchinson's estimator with a novel Hutchinson-free likelihood distillation objective that admits vectorized formulation for better scalability.
AI.result: SCALLOP significantly reduces training variance and training time while consistently outperforming F2D2. It's competitive with state-of-the-art methods while achieving up to 10x inference speedup over the fastest baseline.
AI.conclusion: SCALLOP provides an effective Hutchinson-free alternative for likelihood distillation in flow-based models, offering improved scalability, reduced variance, and faster inference while maintaining competitive performance across domains including molecular science and image datasets.

title: OSWorld2.0: Benchmarking Computer Use Agents on Long-Horizon Real-World Tasks
comment: 68 pages, 42 figures. Equal contribution: Mengqi Yuan, Zilong Zhou, and Xinzhuang Xiong
summary: Existing computer-use benchmarks fail to capture the realism, complexity, and long-horizon demands of real-world computer use, limiting their ability to reveal the limitations of frontier agents. We introduce OSWorld 2.0, a benchmark of 108 long-horizon computer-use workflows across everyday and professional tasks, designed to capture complex and challenging real-world phenomena. Each task represents a realistic end-to-end workflow that takes human users a median of about 1.6 hours to complete and requires an average of 318 tool calls with Claude Opus 4.7 using maximum thinking, compared with about 30 in OSWorld 1.0. OSWorld 2.0 targets challenge phenomena that are common in real workflows yet underrepresented in prior benchmarks, spanning interaction-design challenges such as streaming interaction and dynamic environments, as well as agent-pattern challenges such as cross-source reasoning, implicit-state inference, and visual-spatial precision. Tasks are grounded in authentic input artifacts and cross-referenced against realistic stateful user profile data, and include separate safety reports auditing safety-sensitive execution. Under our primary binary-completion metric at 500 steps, Claude Opus 4.8 with maximum thinking and batched tool calls scores best but still completes only 20.6% of tasks at a 54.8% partial score; GPT-5.5 is far more token-efficient yet plateaus near 13%. These results show that current agents are still far from professional-level computer use: rather than stumbling on basic GUI control or coding, they lose track of constraints, miss information that arrives mid-task, guess rather than ask the user, and skip verification, struggling most when a task hinges on hidden state they must recover.

AI.tldr: OSWorld 2.0 is a benchmark for evaluating AI agents on complex, long-horizon real-world computer-use workflows, revealing that current frontier agents perform poorly on realistic tasks requiring sustained reasoning and hidden state management.
AI.motivation: Existing computer-use benchmarks fail to capture the realism, complexity, and long-horizon demands of real-world computer use, limiting their ability to reveal the limitations of frontier AI agents.
AI.method: Introduces OSWorld 2.0 benchmark with 108 long-horizon computer-use workflows across everyday and professional tasks, featuring realistic end-to-end workflows, authentic input artifacts, cross-referenced user profiles, and safety reports. Tasks target underrepresented challenge phenomena like streaming interaction, dynamic environments, cross-source reasoning, implicit-state inference, and visual-spatial precision.
AI.result: Claude Opus 4.8 with maximum thinking scores best but completes only 20.6% of tasks (54.8% partial score), while GPT-5.5 plateaus near 13% completion. Current agents struggle with constraint tracking, mid-task information processing, decision-making between guessing vs asking, and verification - particularly with hidden state recovery.
AI.conclusion: Current AI agents are far from professional-level computer use, not because of basic GUI or coding issues, but due to fundamental limitations in handling complex workflows that require sustained reasoning, constraint management, and hidden state inference over long time horizons.

title: A Novel Latent-Class Attack and its Detection by Class Subspace Orthogonalization
summary: Deep learning, which in general relies on voluminous amounts of training data, is vulnerable to data poisoning attacks, including error-generic attacks and backdoors (Trojans). In this work, we propose a new data poisoning attack we dub a latent class attack. Here, all poisoned examples are from a class that is novel (unknown) for the given classification domain and are mislabeled to one of the known classes (the target class) of the domain, so that the model learns to recognize the novel class as a sub-class of the target class. Such attacks could be used e.g. to defeat AI-based access control systems, or could cause a "foe" to be classified as a "friend". We also propose a post-training defense to detect this attack, without any access to the training set. This detection approach builds on "class subspace orthogonalization" (CSO), a plug-and-play paradigm demonstrated to improve existing backdoor detectors. Here, CSO is used to seek an input (a putative unknown class instance) whose internal representation is not aligned with any of the known classes, and yet which is classified with confidence to one of these classes. Finally, specific to image classification domains, we propose a method for visualizing the estimated unknown class instance, providing explainability to our latent class detections.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a new data poisoning attack called "latent class attack" where poisoned examples from a novel class are mislabeled to a target class, making the model learn the novel class as a sub-class of the target. It also proposes a defense using class subspace orthogonalization to detect such attacks without training set access, with explainable visualization for image domains.
AI.motivation: Deep learning is vulnerable to data poisoning attacks including error-generic attacks and backdoors. The authors identify a need to address a specific type of attack where novel class data is mislabeled to known classes, which could be used to defeat AI-based access control systems or cause misclassification of adversaries as allies.
AI.method: The paper proposes: 1) A latent class attack where all poisoned examples are from a novel class unknown to the domain and mislabeled to a target class. 2) A defense using class subspace orthogonalization (CSO) paradigm to detect attacks by seeking inputs whose internal representations are not aligned with any known classes but are confidently classified to one. 3) For image domains, a method for visualizing estimated unknown class instances for explainable detection.
AI.result: The paper presents a novel attack method (latent class attack) and a corresponding defense mechanism using CSO that can detect such attacks post-training without access to the training set. The defense includes explainable visualization capabilities for image classification domains.
AI.conclusion: The work successfully demonstrates both a new type of data poisoning attack (latent class attack) and an effective defense against it using class subspace orthogonalization, contributing to both offensive and defensive security research in deep learning with practical applications for system security and explainable detection.

title: Learned Coordination Conventions in Cooperative MARL: Measuring the Translation Gap Between Theory-Informed Roles and Learned Routing
comment: 8 pages, 1 figures. Poster Accepted at NExT-Game 2026: New Frontiers in Game-Theoretic Learning, ICML 2026 Workshop
summary:
Role-semantic assignments provide priors over how heterogeneous agents may coordinate, but cooperative MARL systems instead settle on conventions through decentralized, non-stationary learning, with no guarantee that the resulting structure matches those priors. We study this translation gap between theory-informed role expectations and learned coordination structure through a diagnostic combining a role-routing matrix, formation sensitivity ($Δ_{\max}$), and gradient/occlusion attribution across three-role MiniGrid and SMACv2 (Terran) environments.
  We show that label-conditioned attention produces substantially more concentrated and role-specific routing than flat MLP baselines, remains stable under 3v3--9v9 scaling, transfers zero-shot across team sizes, and is invariant to ally-slot padding. A 5-seed re-evaluation shows partial alignment between learned conventions and designer-specified priors while revealing where small-n noise can manufacture apparent strategic divergence. We present these results as an empirical framework for measuring coordination structure in cooperative MARL rather than as a new equilibrium concept or causal explanation.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates the gap between theoretical role expectations and learned coordination in cooperative multi-agent reinforcement learning, developing diagnostic tools to analyze how agents' learned conventions align with designer-specified role priors.
AI.motivation: The paper addresses the "translation gap" between theoretical role-semantic assignments (priors over how agents should coordinate) and what actually emerges from decentralized, non-stationary learning in cooperative MARL. Current systems develop conventions through learning, but there's no guarantee these match designer expectations.
AI.method: Researchers develop a diagnostic framework combining: 1) role-routing matrix, 2) formation sensitivity (Δ_max), and 3) gradient/occlusion attribution. They test this in three-role MiniGrid and SMACv2 (Terran) environments. They compare label-conditioned attention models with flat MLP baselines across team sizes from 3v3 to 9v9.
AI.result: Label-conditioned attention produces more concentrated and role-specific routing than MLP baselines, remains stable across team sizes (3v3-9v9), transfers zero-shot across team sizes, and is invariant to ally-slot padding. A 5-seed evaluation shows partial alignment between learned conventions and designer-specified priors, revealing that small-n noise can create apparent strategic divergence.
AI.conclusion: The paper presents an empirical framework for measuring coordination structure in cooperative MARL, rather than proposing a new equilibrium concept or causal explanation. It demonstrates the value of diagnostic tools for understanding the gap between theoretical role expectations and emergent coordination patterns.

title: How Token Influence Decays with Distance: A Green-Function View of Trained Language Models
summary: We study how the next-token prediction of an autoregressive Transformer language model changes under small perturbations of earlier input token embeddings. Motivated by operator learning and iterative solvers for differential equations, we investigate how the influence of one token on another decays with distance in a trained model. In multilevel methods for differential equations, such as domain decomposition, multigrid, and multilevel preconditioning, one often exploits a separation between strong local interactions and weaker but essential global interactions. The latter correspond to the long tail of the Green's function and are typically handled by a coarse-level operator. Inspired by this perspective, we compute an empirical, distance-resolved gradient profile of token dependencies using autograd. Experiments on trained Pythia models and Qwen2.5-0.5B show that, over the measured distance range, the median Jacobian sensitivity is much better described by a power-law-type decay than by an exponential alternative: the diagonal-normalized profile is well described by $$\overline G(r) \approx γ+β(r+1)^{-p}$$ with exponents $p \approx 0.7$--$0.9$ (typically $0.8$--$0.9$). This behavior appears on coherent text from Gutenberg and WikiText-103. Token-shuffling experiments show that the power-law profile persists even when syntax and prediction quality collapse, whereas randomly initialized models do not exhibit it. The slowly decaying long-range sensitivity thus appears to be a learned property of trained autoregressive Transformer operators. These findings suggest that hierarchical or coarse-level mechanisms in language models may be able to exploit the long-tailed sensitivity profiles.

AI.tldr: Autoregressive Transformer language models exhibit power-law decay (not exponential) in token influence over distance, with exponent ~0.8-0.9, suggesting learned long-range dependencies that could inform hierarchical mechanisms.
AI.motivation: To understand how next-token prediction changes under perturbations of earlier token embeddings, inspired by operator learning and multilevel methods for differential equations which separate strong local interactions from weaker global ones.
AI.method: Computing empirical distance-resolved gradient profiles of token dependencies using autograd on trained Pythia and Qwen2.5-0.5B models, analyzing behavior on coherent text (Gutenberg, WikiText-103), and conducting token-shuffling experiments to isolate effects.
AI.result: Median Jacobian sensitivity decays as a power law: Ĝ(r) ≈ γ+β(r+1)^-p with p ≈ 0.7-0.9 (typically 0.8-0.9). This power-law profile persists even when syntax collapses via token-shuffling, but doesn't appear in randomly initialized models.
AI.conclusion: Slowly decaying long-range sensitivity is a learned property of trained autoregressive Transformers, suggesting potential for hierarchical or coarse-level mechanisms to exploit these long-tailed dependency profiles.

title: SCARCE: Scalable Cascade Analysis for Rare-event Characterisation via Embeddings
summary: Rare events govern the safety profile of modern AI systems, yet their probabilities are extremely difficult to estimate: direct Monte Carlo requires prohibitive sample budgets. Subset Simulation (SS) addresses this by decomposing a rare-event probability into moderate conditional probabilities over nested intermediate events. However, classical SS requires a handcrafted scalar performance function whose sublevel sets define those events, demanding detailed knowledge of the failure geometry and limiting transfer to new domains. We propose SCARCE (Scalable Cascade Analysis for Rare-event Characterisation via Embeddings), which replaces the performance function with learned latent representations and geometric rulers that score proximity to failure regions. Adaptive thresholding constructs nested intermediate events directly from data. We formalise SCARCE through a non-negative supermartingale, yielding a high-probability upper envelope that remains valid under early stopping. On MNIST misclassification, where dense Monte Carlo provides ground truth, SCARCE achieves approximately 400--500 times lower mean absolute error than grid-searched traditional SS while eliminating systematic over-counting. We then study PAIR-style LLM jailbreaks under a fleet-level threat model with adversarial fraction $η$. On Llama-Guard-3-8B hidden states, a PCA-based ruler attains 2.6% mean relative error for $η\geq 10^{-3}$ against finite-sample references whose average bootstrap relative half-width is 27.9%, and transfers to a GCG-style corpus with 2.93% relative error after recalibration. A directional criterion $\mathrm{KL}(p_{\mathrm{good}}\,\|\,p_{\mathrm{bad}})$ ranks rulers consistently with estimation error (Spearman $ρ=0.83$).

AI.tldr: SCARCE (Scalable Cascade Analysis for Rare-event Characterisation via Embeddings) replaces traditional Subset Simulation's handcrafted performance function with learned latent representations and geometric rulers to estimate rare-event probabilities more efficiently, achieving 400-500× lower error on MNIST and transferring effectively to LLM jailbreak detection.
AI.motivation: Rare events are critical for AI safety but extremely difficult to estimate probabilities for; traditional Subset Simulation requires handcrafted scalar performance functions with detailed knowledge of failure geometry, limiting transfer to new domains.
AI.method: Proposes SCARCE which replaces handcrafted performance functions with learned latent representations and geometric rulers scoring proximity to failure regions; uses adaptive thresholding to construct nested intermediate events directly from data; formalizes through non-negative supermartingale for high-probability upper envelope.
AI.result: On MNIST misclassification: 400-500× lower mean absolute error than grid-searched traditional SS while eliminating systematic over-counting; on LLM jailbreaks: PCA-based ruler attains 2.6% mean relative error for adversarial fractions ≥10⁻³, transfers to GCG-style corpus with 2.93% error; directional KL criterion ranks rulers consistently (Spearman ρ=0.83).
AI.conclusion: SCARCE provides a scalable, data-driven alternative to traditional Subset Simulation that eliminates need for handcrafted performance functions, achieves superior accuracy, and effectively transfers across domains for rare-event probability estimation in AI safety applications.

title: On the Nonlinearity of Learning Rate Scaling for LLM Training
summary: Learning-rate transfer can reduce the cost of training large language models: instead of sweeping learning rates at target scale, practitioners extrapolate from smaller runs. Existing approaches often assume that the optimal learning rate follows a log-linear scaling law in data scale and model size. We carefully examine and evaluate this scaling law. In our empirical study of GPT-2--style models from 22M to 707M parameters trained on 5B to 100B tokens, the optimal learning rate develops upward curvature at larger scales, leading to inaccurate extrapolation. We find that this curvature largely disappears when learning rates are replaced by effective learning rate (the step size in normalized weight space), and when data $D$ extrapolation is used instead of model size $N$ extrapolation. Next, we explain nonlinearity in scaling: weight-norm converges to equilibrium slower when optimal learning is small, requiring a larger step size to reduce the transient phase. Experiments with AdamH, which directly controls the effective learning rate, further support this explanation.

AI.tldr: Optimal learning rates for large language models don't follow simple log-linear scaling laws; they develop upward curvature at larger scales, but this can be fixed by using effective learning rates and data extrapolation instead of model size extrapolation.
AI.motivation: Learning-rate transfer aims to reduce the cost of training large language models by extrapolating optimal learning rates from smaller runs, but existing approaches assume incorrect scaling laws that lead to inaccurate extrapolation at larger scales.
AI.method: Empirical study of GPT-2-style models (22M to 707M parameters) trained on 5B to 100B tokens, examining learning rate scaling, replacing learning rates with effective learning rates (step size in normalized weight space), comparing data vs. model size extrapolation, and using AdamH optimizer to directly control effective learning rate.
AI.result: Optimal learning rates show upward curvature at larger scales, breaking the assumed log-linear scaling law. This curvature largely disappears when using effective learning rates and data extrapolation. Weight-norm convergence explains the nonlinearity: slower convergence when optimal learning is small requires larger step sizes to reduce transient phases.
AI.conclusion: Learning-rate transfer should use effective learning rates rather than raw learning rates, and data extrapolation is more reliable than model size extrapolation. The nonlinear scaling behavior can be explained by weight-norm dynamics and transient phase reduction needs.

title: SFBench: The SciFy Scientific Feasibility Benchmark
summary: We present SFBench, a benchmark dataset for evaluating systems that assess the feasibility of scientific claims. SFBench includes 197 claims in materials science, each annotated with a ground-truth feasibility score on a five-point scale along with an explanation of that assessment. The collection differs from previous collections in several important ways: 1) it defines a complex task that requires reasoning over claims of varying scientific feasibility; 2) its claims are not extracted from existing scientific publications but are created de novo, greatly reducing the chances that LLMs have trained on them; 3) claims and ground truth are established by subject matter experts, not by artificial intelligence; and 4) unlike many benchmarks that ask about question/answer pairs, provide multiple choice answers, or ask questions requiring short, fixed answers, SFBench explanations are completely open-ended. We describe the benchmark design, data creation process, and evaluation metrics, and we report baseline results using recent GPT models.

AI.tldr: SFBench is a new benchmark dataset with 197 materials science claims created de novo by experts, each annotated with feasibility scores and open-ended explanations, designed to evaluate systems that assess scientific claim feasibility.
AI.motivation: Existing benchmarks for evaluating scientific claim assessment systems have limitations: they often use claims extracted from publications (which LLMs may have seen during training), rely on AI-generated ground truth, use constrained response formats, and don't adequately test complex feasibility reasoning. There's a need for a benchmark that addresses these issues to better evaluate the capabilities of systems in assessing scientific claim feasibility.
AI.method: Created SFBench dataset with 197 materials science claims designed de novo (not extracted from existing publications). Subject matter experts established ground truth by providing: 1) feasibility scores on a five-point scale, and 2) completely open-ended explanations for each assessment. The benchmark includes evaluation metrics and baseline results were reported using recent GPT models.
AI.result: SFBench represents a benchmark that differs from previous collections in four key ways: 1) focuses on complex feasibility reasoning over varying scientific claims, 2) uses de novo created claims to minimize LLM training exposure, 3) relies on expert-annotated ground truth rather than AI-generated labels, and 4) employs completely open-ended explanations rather than constrained response formats. Baseline results using GPT models are provided.
AI.conclusion: SFBench provides a valuable benchmark for evaluating scientific feasibility assessment systems that addresses limitations of existing benchmarks by using expert-created, de novo claims with open-ended explanations, making it more robust for testing model capabilities in complex scientific reasoning tasks where training data contamination and simplistic evaluation formats are concerns.

title: GLACIER: Rethinking Mass Spectrum Prediction as an Object Detection Problem
summary: Predicting tandem mass spectra (MS/MS) from molecular structures represents a central task in analytical chemistry with direct relevance to clinical metabolomics, systems biology, and adjacent disciplines. In this work, we revisit the problem through the lens of object detection on molecular graphs. Molecular fragmentation, a central step in MS/MS prediction, can be approximated as detecting a set of subgraphs (i.e., fragments) and their associated spectral contributions. Existing fragment-based models follow a two-stage paradigm -- first generating candidate fragments and then scoring them -- analogous to two-stage R-CNNs in computer vision. Towards higher accuracy and faster inference, we introduce GLACIER, a single-stage transformer-based fragment detection neural network for molecular graphs. This unified formulation eliminates the need for candidate enumeration, enabling scalable and globally consistent modeling of molecular fragmentation. GLACIER is faster and more accurate than existing state-of-the-art by a significant margin, achieving 70.0% and 69.7% Top-1 retrieval accuracy with and without contrastive finetuning on the MassSpecGym dataset (from the previous SOTA of 64.0%) and 52.5% and 38.5% respectively on the NIST'20 dataset (from 33.2%). Furthermore, GLACIER provides nearly 8-fold inference speedup over our prior two-stage model. Code is available at https://github.com/coleygroup/ms-pred

AI.tldr: GLACIER is a single-stage transformer-based neural network for predicting tandem mass spectra (MS/MS) from molecular structures using object detection on molecular graphs, eliminating candidate fragment enumeration and achieving state-of-the-art accuracy with significant speed improvements.
AI.motivation: MS/MS prediction from molecular structures is crucial for clinical metabolomics and systems biology. Existing fragment-based models use a two-stage approach (like R-CNNs) that first generates candidate fragments then scores them, which is inefficient and may miss optimal fragments.
AI.method: GLACIER reformulates molecular fragmentation as object detection on molecular graphs, using a single-stage transformer-based neural network that directly detects subgraphs (fragments) and their spectral contributions without candidate enumeration. This enables scalable, globally consistent modeling.
AI.result: GLACIER achieves 70.0% and 69.7% Top-1 retrieval accuracy (with/without contrastive finetuning) on MassSpecGym (previous SOTA: 64.0%), and 52.5%/38.5% on NIST'20 dataset (from 33.2%). It provides nearly 8-fold inference speedup over prior two-stage models.
AI.conclusion: GLACIER demonstrates that single-stage fragment detection via transformers offers significant advantages over two-stage approaches in MS/MS prediction, achieving superior accuracy and dramatically faster inference while eliminating the need for candidate fragment enumeration.

title: Invariant Reasoning Directions in Latent Trajectories of Language Models
comment: 9 main text pages and 6 appendix pages
summary: Latent reasoning models perform multi-step inference directly in hidden-state space, yet the structure of these latent reasoning trajectories remains poorly understood. We show that contrastive refinement signals between stronger and weaker reasoning trajectories exhibit a highly concentrated low-rank structure, while unconstrained latent updates remain sensitive to paraphrases, checkpoint choice, and trajectory perturbations. These observations suggest that latent reasoning trajectories contain stable invariant directions mixed with unstable instance-specific variation. We introduce \textbf{Trajectory-Invariant Latent Refinement (TILR)}, a training-free intervention framework for identifying and manipulating stable reasoning directions in latent space. TILR first learns a low-rank invariant subspace from contrastive trajectory differences across inputs, then constrains latent interventions to this subspace while suppressing poorly aligned updates through an adaptive alignment gate. Across six reasoning benchmarks, we find that a small number of latent directions explain most variation between strong and weak reasoning trajectories. Interventions on these directions causally improve reasoning consistency and reduce trajectory instability under paraphrases and perturbations. TILR improves answer consistency under paraphrase by ~10% and reduces latent trajectory variance by up to $50\%$ while preserving reasoning accuracy. These results support a geometric view of latent reasoning in which transferable reasoning behavior emerges from stable low-dimensional structure within hidden-state trajectories.

AI.tldr: Trajectory-Invariant Latent Refinement (TILR) identifies stable reasoning directions in latent space to improve reasoning consistency and reduce trajectory instability without training.
AI.motivation: Latent reasoning models perform inference in hidden-state space, but their reasoning trajectories are poorly understood and unstable - sensitive to paraphrases, checkpoint choices, and trajectory perturbations.
AI.method: TILR learns a low-rank invariant subspace from contrastive trajectory differences across inputs, then constrains latent interventions to this subspace while suppressing poorly aligned updates through an adaptive alignment gate.
AI.result: TILR improves answer consistency under paraphrases by ~10%, reduces latent trajectory variance by up to 50%, while preserving reasoning accuracy. A small number of latent directions explain most variation between strong/weak reasoning trajectories.
AI.conclusion: Transferable reasoning behavior emerges from stable low-dimensional structure within hidden-state trajectories, supporting a geometric view of latent reasoning.

title: Safety from Honesty in a Disinterested AI Predictor
summary: As AI systems become more capable, training procedures that optimize for downstream outcomes risk introducing implicit agency: goal-directed behavior that designers never specified. We present a formal safety argument for the Scientist AI (SAI) Predictor, trained to approximate the Bayesian posterior conditioned on a dataset of "epistemically contextualized" natural-language statements. We argue that such a Predictor can honestly predict agents, actions, and their consequences without itself being an agent that selects outputs to achieve goals. This rests on data representation and on the training procedure. Epistemic contextualization of text distinguishes latent factual claims from communication acts, so expressions of goals are treated as evidence to be explained rather than drives the model adopts. With a posterior-seeking training objective, this is intended to drive the Predictor toward calibrated, cautious predictions. Training proceeds so downstream effects of deploying a prediction never serve as a reward signal; any agency the system needs is supplied by explicit scaffolding constrained by guardrails. We prove that, under assumptions on the training dynamics and on the argued sparsity of dangerous Predictors, the probability that training produces a Predictor whose guarded deployment carries residual harm above a specified threshold is small: a dangerous Predictor would have to underestimate harm in a coordinated way across many queries while such coordinated patterns are rare under the initialization distribution and receive no direct training signal. Safety and accuracy are jointly supported in this framework, since the constraints that secure accuracy are the same ones that make coordinated deception costly. These guarantees against misalignment and agency arising from within the Predictor itself do not preclude the use of the Predictor as part of an agentic system.

AI.tldr: A safety framework for AI predictors that prevents implicit agency through epistemic contextualization and posterior-seeking training, providing formal guarantees against coordinated deception while maintaining accuracy.
AI.motivation: As AI systems become more capable, training procedures that optimize for downstream outcomes risk introducing implicit agency - goal-directed behavior that designers never specified. There's a need for AI systems that can predict agents and actions without themselves becoming agents with unaligned goals.
AI.method: The Scientist AI (SAI) Predictor uses epistemic contextualization of natural-language statements to distinguish latent factual claims from communication acts. It's trained to approximate the Bayesian posterior conditioned on datasets, with a posterior-seeking objective that drives toward calibrated predictions. The training procedure ensures downstream effects never serve as reward signals, and any needed agency is supplied by explicit scaffolding with guardrails.
AI.result: The paper proves that under assumptions about training dynamics and sparsity of dangerous predictors, the probability of producing a predictor with residual harm above a specified threshold is small. Dangerous predictors would need to coordinate underestimation of harm across many queries, which is rare under initialization and receives no training signal.
AI.conclusion: The framework provides safety guarantees against misalignment and agency arising from within the predictor itself, while supporting accuracy. Safety and accuracy are jointly supported because constraints securing accuracy also make coordinated deception costly. The predictor can still be used as part of agentic systems when needed.

title: Symbolic Mechanistic Data Attribution: Tracing Training Influence to Learned Behavioral Policies
summary: While existing data attribution methods can identify which training examples build specific mechanistic circuits, they cannot explain how training data shapes the high-level behavioral decisions a model learns to make. To bridge this gap, we introduce Symbolic Mechanistic Data Attribution (SMDA), a framework that attributes training pairs to the interpretable symbolic policies governing model behavior. SMDA fits a closed-form Ridge regression over sparse autoencoder (SAE) features to model a target behavior, then analytically decomposes how each supervised fine-tuning example shifts that policy through feature-activation Delta_X and output-probability Delta_Y pathways. We distill a symbolic policy for refusal behavior in Llama-3.2-3B-Instruct and analyze 200 SFT training pairs. Our analysis reveals that (1) the symbolic policy's coefficients expose systematic gaps in the base model's safety behavior for categories like religious stereotyping; (2) per-feature Delta_X/Delta_Y decomposition can mechanistically explain why harmful and harmless pairs exert qualitatively different influences on certain features; and (3) individual training pairs routinely exhibit cross-feature interference, allowing SMDA to identify training pairs whose dominant effect falls on unintended features. These results demonstrate that combining mechanistic interpretability with data attribution yields a diagnostic tool that is both more fine-grained than black-box influence functions and more scalable than manual circuit analysis.
