We additionally define a benchmark protocol of ten metrics across six application categories that would establish, beyond the case study, whether each design choice contributes positively.

AI.tldr: Rhetor is a multi-agent system that automates live software product demonstrations by generating rehearsed presentations with synchronized narration and real-time Q&A, addressing the limitations of current browser automation and static demo videos.
AI.motivation: Live product demos are costly, requiring human presenters to select features, perform interactions, narrate coherently, and answer questions in real time. Existing automation is insufficient - browser agents only do task completion, and demo videos produce fixed artifacts that can't be questioned and break with interface changes.
AI.method: Rhetor uses a multi-agent system that takes a running web app and its source code as input. Key contributions include: cross-modal feature representation merging UI exploration with source code analysis; a grounded scripter constrained to observed UI elements; a pre-presentation rehearsal loop with convergence and graceful degradation; and runtime synchronization tying browser actions to audio narration.
AI.result: Tested on 6 pipeline sessions across 4 deployed apps including Excalidraw. The rehearser's locator-firing rate (sigma-bar) ranged 0.31-1.00 over 147 scripted actions. On substantial workloads (53 actions, full tier differentiation), sigma-bar was ~0.92. On the public-domain reference, convergence reached sigma-bar = 1.00 at iteration 2.
AI.conclusion: Rhetor provides a comprehensive solution for automated live demos that overcomes limitations of current approaches. The authors also propose a benchmark protocol to systematically evaluate design contributions across various application categories.

title: The Mirage of Optimizing Training Policies: Monotonic Inference Policies as the Real Objective for LLM Reinforcement Learning
summary: Reinforcement learning (RL) has gained growing attention in large language model (LLM) post-training, yet RL training remains fragile and can suffer from instability or collapse. One vital cause is training-inference mismatch: LLM adopts separate inference and training engines for generation efficiency and training precision, which in practice exhibits inconsistent probabilities for the same trajectories on training and inference sides, even with synchronized model parameters. This naturally induces a special type of off-policyness ever existing and poisoning the training. Prior works have made various efforts in addressing the off-policyness to stabilize the training policies under the mismatch. In this paper, we point out the objective misalignment neglected by existing works that an effective update to the policy in the training engine not necessarily ensures the improvement of the inference policy, i.e., the one used in deployment. To this end, we propose a new policy optimization objective for LLM RL, named Monotonic Inference Policy Improvement (MIPI). Following this principle, we introduce Monotonic Inference Policy Update (MIPU), a two-step LLM RL framework that constructs sampler-referenced candidate updates and selectively accepts synchronized candidates using an inference-side gap proxy. Experiments conducted on two model scales under high mismatch show that MIPU improves average reasoning performance and training stability.

AI.tldr: MIPU is a new RL framework for LLMs that addresses training-inference mismatch by ensuring monotonic improvement of inference policies through selective update acceptance based on inference-side evaluation.
AI.motivation: RL training for LLMs suffers from instability due to training-inference mismatch where separate engines for training precision and generation efficiency produce inconsistent probabilities for the same trajectories, causing persistent off-policyness that poisons training. Existing works neglect that improvements in training policy don't guarantee improvements in inference policy (the one actually deployed).
AI.method: Proposes Monotonic Inference Policy Update (MIPU), a two-step framework: 1) constructs sampler-referenced candidate updates in the training engine, 2) selectively accepts synchronized candidates using an inference-side gap proxy to ensure monotonic improvement of the inference policy following the Monotonic Inference Policy Improvement (MIPI) principle.
AI.result: Experiments on two model scales under high mismatch conditions show that MIPU improves average reasoning performance and enhances training stability compared to previous approaches.
AI.conclusion: The training-inference mismatch problem in LLM RL requires ensuring monotonic improvement of inference policies, not just training policies. MIPU provides an effective framework to address this objective misalignment and stabilize RL training for LLMs.

title: ManimAgent: Self-Evolving Multimodal Agents for Visual Education
summary: Multi-round reflection lets agents built on large language models recover from failures within a single task, but each task remains an isolated episode: lessons learned across many reflection rounds on one task are discarded before the next begins. We study this gap on a code-generation task: from a scientific paper section, the agent writes Python in the open-source Manim library to render a mathematical animation. We present ManimAgent, a self-evolving multimodal agent that carries reflection experience across tasks through a dual-channel Episodic Memory Bank grown entirely from its own task stream, with no weight updates and no human seeds. After each animation converges, a vision-language model scores the rendered keyframes; the resulting signals populate a positive channel M+ that stores success rationales as soft Reference Examples, and a negative channel M- that stores validated failure patterns as hard Known Pitfalls. On a fixed-probe evaluation against no-memory, matched-budget retrieval-augmented generation, and shuffled-memory baselines, blind human Pass@1 rises and reflection rounds fall as memory size grows. We will release the code, frozen memory snapshots, and the task stream.

AI.tldr: ManimAgent is a self-evolving multimodal agent that uses dual-channel episodic memory to carry reflection experience across code-generation tasks, improving animation quality while reducing reflection rounds.
AI.motivation: Current multi-round reflection approaches for LLM-based agents are isolated within single tasks - lessons learned across reflection rounds on one task are discarded before the next task begins, creating inefficiency and preventing cumulative learning.
AI.method: ManimAgent maintains a dual-channel Episodic Memory Bank grown from its own task stream without weight updates or human seeds. After each animation converges, a vision-language model scores rendered keyframes to populate: (1) M+ channel storing success rationales as soft Reference Examples, and (2) M- channel storing validated failure patterns as hard Known Pitfalls.
AI.result: On fixed-probe evaluation against no-memory, matched-budget retrieval-augmented generation, and shuffled-memory baselines, blind human Pass@1 scores rise and reflection rounds decrease as memory size grows, demonstrating effective cross-task learning.
AI.conclusion: The dual-channel episodic memory approach enables LLM-based agents to accumulate and reuse reflection experience across tasks, improving performance and efficiency in code-generation for mathematical animations without requiring weight updates or human supervision.

title: VISTA-DZ: Visual Semantic Trajectory Adaptation for Personalized Dilemma Zone Prediction
comment: This manuscript is currently under review
summary: Driver decision making in the dilemma zone at signalized intersections is safety critical, as vehicles approaching a yellow signal must decide whether to stop or proceed within limited time and distance margins. Accurate prediction of both stop-go decisions and decision timing is important for adaptive signal control, advanced driver assistance systems, and human-centered intelligent transportation applications. However, dilemma zone behavior is strongly driver dependent. Similar approach trajectories may lead to different decisions across drivers because of differences in risk preference, braking habit, and decision threshold. Existing personalized models often rely on handcrafted scalar descriptors, which provide useful but limited summaries of individual behavior. This paper proposes VISTA-DZ, a semantic-profile-conditioned framework for personalized stop-go and decision-time prediction. Historical trajectories are converted into visual representations, interpreted by a vision-language model to generate behavioral profiles, and encoded as semantic embeddings to condition a dual-output prediction network. The final model combines a bidirectional GRU encoder, driver-conditioned multi-head cross-attention, and Feature-wise Linear Modulation for temporal evidence selection and feature adaptation. Experiments on the SDZ dataset and a newly collected FDZ dataset show that VISTA-DZ outperforms trajectory-only and handcrafted personalization baselines, achieving 93.26% in-domain simulation accuracy and 90.22% mean accuracy across 20 held-out simulation drivers. Cross-domain results further show feasible zero-shot simulation-to-real transfer and better real-world generalization when simulation data are combined with limited field data.

AI.tldr: VISTA-DZ is a personalized prediction framework using visual representations and semantic embeddings to predict driver stop-go decisions and timing at signalized intersections.
AI.motivation: Driver decision-making in dilemma zones is safety-critical but strongly driver-dependent. Existing personalized models rely on limited handcrafted scalar descriptors, failing to capture nuanced behavioral patterns.
AI.method: Converts historical trajectories into visual representations, uses vision-language models to generate behavioral profiles as semantic embeddings, and conditions a dual-output prediction network with bidirectional GRU encoder, driver-conditioned cross-attention, and Feature-wise Linear Modulation.
AI.result: Outperforms trajectory-only and handcrafted personalization baselines, achieving 93.26% in-domain simulation accuracy and 90.22% mean accuracy across 20 held-out drivers. Shows feasible zero-shot simulation-to-real transfer and better real-world generalization.
AI.conclusion: VISTA-DZ provides effective semantic-profile-based personalization for dilemma zone prediction, enabling better safety applications through improved generalization and transfer capabilities.

title: BayesEvolve: Explicit Belief States for Autonomous Scientific Discovery
summary: Autonomous scientific discovery systems increasingly use large language models (LLMs) to propose new hypotheses, but many such systems condition primarily on experimental memory: archives of high-scoring candidates or heuristic summaries of recent trials. We argue that discovery agents should instead maintain explicit, uncertainty-aware beliefs about hypothesis quality. We introduce BayesEvolve, a belief-guided discovery framework that converts experimental evidence into a predictive belief state and uses this belief to guide future experimentation. As a controlled testbed for belief-guided discovery, we evaluate BayesEvolve on shifted BBOB-style black-box optimization tasks, leaving program and laboratory discovery domains to future work. BayesEvolve improves sample efficiency over memory- and archive-guided LLM baselines under a fixed evaluation budget. We further show that the belief state is predictive on held-out candidate pools, that controlled decision-rule ablations favor belief-guided selection with an annealed uncertainty bonus, and that BayesEvolve exhibits productive late-stage concentration rather than unfocused exploration.

AI.tldr: BayesEvolve is a belief-guided discovery framework that maintains explicit, uncertainty-aware beliefs about hypothesis quality to improve sample efficiency in autonomous scientific discovery, outperforming memory-based LLM systems on black-box optimization tasks.
AI.motivation: Current autonomous scientific discovery systems primarily use LLMs that condition on experimental memory (archives of high-scoring candidates or heuristic summaries), which lacks explicit uncertainty-aware beliefs about hypothesis quality. The authors argue that discovery agents should instead maintain explicit beliefs to guide future experimentation more effectively.
AI.method: Introduces BayesEvolve, a belief-guided discovery framework that converts experimental evidence into a predictive belief state about hypothesis quality. The framework uses this belief state to guide future experimentation decisions. The method is evaluated on shifted BBOB-style black-box optimization tasks as a controlled testbed.
AI.result: BayesEvolve improves sample efficiency over memory- and archive-guided LLM baselines under fixed evaluation budgets. The belief state is shown to be predictive on held-out candidate pools. Controlled decision-rule ablations favor belief-guided selection with an annealed uncertainty bonus. BayesEvolve exhibits productive late-stage concentration rather than unfocused exploration.
AI.conclusion: Belief-guided discovery with explicit, uncertainty-aware beliefs about hypothesis quality provides superior performance compared to memory-based LLM approaches. The BayesEvolve framework demonstrates that maintaining predictive belief states leads to more efficient exploration and productive late-stage concentration in autonomous scientific discovery.

title: Optimizer Memory Makes Shuffle Order a First-Order Source of Fine-Tuning Noise
summary: Shuffle order can be a larger source of fine-tuning noise than a memoryless analysis predicts: fixed-clock optimizer memory makes local equal-multiset contrasts first order in the learning rate rather than second order, and the resulting order channel can be large enough for a single seed to flip a close A/B comparison. We isolate this mechanism and derive a fit-free way to size the noise it produces. For a memoryless optimizer, reordering an equal multiset has no first-order endpoint term; the leading local contrast is the $O(η^2)$ gradient bracket. Fixed-clock optimizers such as AdamW are different. Their moment buffers, preconditioner state, and de-biasing counters advance with the step index rather than with the learning-rate-scaled time $τ=ηk$, so the same gradient can receive a position-dependent endpoint weight. For any fixed finite measurement window, a lifted-state expansion gives an $O(η)$ equal-multiset contrast whenever the first-order replay coefficient is nonzero, while regular and clock-matched controls remain $O(η^2)$; a bare fixed-$β$ momentum buffer is already enough. A bitwise-deterministic replay from one warmed optimizer state isolates the mechanism, giving order-variance slopes 1.83 for AdamW, 2.00 for fixed-$β$ momentum, and 4.00 for SGD; matching the memory clock to $τ$ restores the regular exponent. For AdamW with a frozen preconditioner, the same impulse-weight kernel gives a closed-form asymptotic order-variance floor after the local potentials are measured, with no fitted coefficients. The result is local to the measurement window (independent reshuffling can average the channel across windows), but it yields order-noise error bars, positional attribution weights, and a seed-budget criterion for fine-tuning comparisons.

AI.tldr: Shuffle order matters more than expected in fine-tuning: fixed-clock optimizers (AdamW) have first-order noise from order variance due to memory state progression, unlike memoryless SGD which has only second-order effects.
AI.motivation: Standard analysis underestimates shuffle order noise in fine-tuning. Memoryless optimizer analysis predicts only second-order effects from equal-multiset reordering, but real optimizers like AdamW with fixed-clock memory (moment buffers, preconditioners) show first-order effects that can flip close A/B comparisons.
AI.method: Use lifted-state expansion analysis to show how fixed-clock optimizers create first-order equal-multiset contrast. Compare different optimizers: AdamW, fixed-β momentum, and SGD. Use bitwise-deterministic replay experiments to isolate the mechanism and measure order-variance slopes. Derive closed-form asymptotic order-variance floor for AdamW with frozen preconditioner.
AI.result: Fixed-clock optimizers show O(η) equal-multiset contrast vs O(η²) for memoryless/clock-matched controls. Order-variance slopes: AdamW (1.83), fixed-β momentum (2.00), SGD (4.00). Matching memory clock to τ restores regular exponent. Provides method to compute order-noise error bars and seed-budget criterion.
AI.conclusion: Shuffle order noise is significant in fine-tuning comparisons using fixed-clock optimizers like AdamW, requiring proper noise estimation for reliable A/B testing. The paper provides analytical tools to quantify this noise and determine necessary seed budgets.

title: Sequential Fairness Auditing with Limited Output Access
summary: External evaluations are becoming increasingly central to the governance of AI systems. In practice, however, independent auditors often have limited access to deployed models and must rely on query-based interactions. Most existing fairness evaluation methods assume static datasets and fixed-sample statistical tests, making them poorly suited to real-world auditing scenarios in which evidence must be collected sequentially under query constraints. In this work, we formulate fairness auditing as a tolerance-aware sequential hypothesis-testing problem under limited model output access. We develop a sequential generalized likelihood-ratio framework that allows auditors to accumulate evidence from a finite audit pool and stop once sufficient support for compliance or violation has been obtained. The framework is instantiated for decision-based Statistical Parity and Equal Opportunity audits, and extended to score- and logit-based proxy audits when richer observables are available. Our results show that both the fairness metric and the level of model access significantly affect audit efficiency, and that the benefits of richer output information are not uniform across auditing settings. In particular, richer outputs can substantially reduce the number of queries required for some fairness metrics and operating regimes, while offering limited gains in near-threshold cases. This work provides a practical statistical framework for sequential fairness auditing under realistic deployment constraints.

AI.tldr: Sequential fairness auditing framework for AI systems under limited query access using hypothesis testing to accumulate evidence until sufficient support for compliance/violation.
AI.motivation: Current fairness evaluation methods assume static datasets and fixed-sample statistical tests, which are poorly suited for real-world auditing scenarios where auditors have limited access to deployed models and must collect evidence sequentially under query constraints.
AI.method: Develops a sequential generalized likelihood-ratio framework for fairness auditing as a tolerance-aware sequential hypothesis-testing problem. Instantiates the framework for decision-based Statistical Parity and Equal Opportunity audits, and extends to score- and logit-based proxy audits when richer observables are available.
AI.result: Shows that both fairness metric and level of model access significantly affect audit efficiency. Richer outputs can substantially reduce query requirements for some fairness metrics and operating regimes, but offer limited gains in near-threshold cases.
AI.conclusion: Provides a practical statistical framework for sequential fairness auditing under realistic deployment constraints, enabling auditors to efficiently gather evidence with limited access to AI systems.

title: Using Large Language Models as Low-Cost Statistical Estimators for Human-Response Data
summary: Quantitative research across the social and behavioral sciences depends on human subject experiments that are expensive, slow, and subject to sampling bias. Here we show that pretrained large language models induce risk-equivalent estimators of conditional expectations under squared loss, establishing restricted functional risk equivalence: under squared loss, the LLM induces an estimator whose risk matches the Bayes optimal risk for squared-loss prediction of conditional expectations for any inference that depends on the data only through the conditional mean. We formalize the LLM as a misspecified functional estimator $T(\hat{P}_n)$ trained on i.i.d.\ data, decompose the estimation error into representation bias $ε_{\mathrm{rep}}$ and optimization error, and prove that under mild regularity conditions the LLM's expected error converges to the irreducible population variance plus the squared representation bias, with the representation bias bounded by the Pinsker inequality. The identifiability error $δ$ propagates into the effective bias, inflating the asymptotic risk floor. We establish restricted functional risk equivalence via a bidirectional Le Cam deficiency analysis: the forward deficiency vanishes asymptotically while the reverse deficiency is exactly zero. We provide finite-sample concentration bounds and a calibration protocol with explicit decision rules. The result is a precise, provable statement: a well-calibrated LLM achieves the Bayes-optimal risk for conditional-mean-dependent inference, bounded by explicit scope conditions. In practical applications, this means that under satisfied conditions and well-calibrated models, large language models can be used in many prediction and decision-making tasks that originally relied on human experiments, approximating near-optimal statistical inference at lower cost.

AI.tldr: Large language models can achieve Bayes-optimal risk for conditional-mean-dependent inference when properly calibrated, potentially replacing expensive human experiments.
AI.motivation: Human subject experiments in social/behavioral sciences are expensive, slow, and subject to sampling bias. There's a need for more efficient, scalable alternatives for statistical inference.
AI.method: Theoretical framework treating LLMs as misspecified functional estimators T(hat{P}_n) trained on i.i.d. data. Decompose error into representation bias ε_rep and optimization error. Use Pinsker inequality to bound representation bias. Establish restricted functional risk equivalence via bidirectional Le Cam deficiency analysis.
AI.result: LLMs induce risk-equivalent estimators of conditional expectations under squared loss. When well-calibrated, they achieve Bayes-optimal risk for conditional-mean-dependent inference, with risk bounded by irreducible population variance plus squared representation bias (bounded by Pinsker inequality).
AI.conclusion: Under certain conditions and proper calibration, LLMs can replace human experiments for many prediction/decision-making tasks, providing near-optimal statistical inference at lower cost and without sampling bias.

title: Speculative Pre-Positioning: Decoding Stateful Sessions to the Next Decision Point Off the Critical Path
summary: A stateless inference server (vLLM, SGLang, TensorRT-LLM) idles between requests while the accelerator waits; a stateful session reclaims that idle time. Speculative pre-positioning decodes the session forward to its next decision point with the target model's own forward pass and no draft model, moving the cross-request prefill and entry-decode off the critical path: the next request resumes from a pre-paid entry on its delta, or, when a confidence gate fires, is answered from a cached distribution in one near-constant vocabulary scan with no decode, at a cost only of energy and a rare, bounded false accept. The payoff is conditional on capability: a capable model fires the gate at near-full coverage and about 87% precision (a smaller one never clears it), returning the first token in about 1.0 ms versus the 39 ms decode a prefix cache still pays.

AI.tldr: Speculative pre-positioning enables stateful sessions to pre-decode to next decision points using target model's forward pass, moving prefill off critical path and enabling near-instant (1.0 ms) token generation with cached distributions when confidence gate fires.
AI.motivation: Stateless inference servers idle between requests while accelerators wait, wasting computational resources. Stateful sessions can reclaim this idle time but still face latency from cross-request prefill and initial decoding.
AI.method: Speculative pre-positioning decodes sessions forward to next decision points using only the target model's forward pass (no draft model). When a confidence gate fires, answers come from cached distributions via single vocabulary scan instead of full decode. System moves prefill off critical path so next requests resume from pre-paid entry.
AI.result: Capable models fire confidence gate with near-full coverage and ~87% precision, returning first token in ~1.0 ms vs 39 ms for prefix cache decoding. Smaller models never clear the gate. System has bounded false accept rate and only energy cost.
AI.conclusion: Speculative pre-positioning enables efficient stateful session management with dramatic latency reduction for first token generation, trading energy for speed when model confidence is high enough to trigger the confidence gate.

title: Whose Side Is Your Agent On? Multi-Party Principal Loyalty in LLM Agents
summary: A rapidly growing class of LLM agents is multi-party: the agent acts for a principal (who briefs it, sends follow-ups, and receives results) while also conversing in a separate channel with a counterparty whose interests may diverge (negotiating with a vendor, screening inbound requests, or mediating between employees). Here "help whoever you are talking to" is the wrong objective. The agent must stay loyal to the principal it represents without over-refusing the principal's own cooperative asks. We study this multi-party loyalty problem and contribute a measurement instrument, two mechanisms, and a structural lesson. PrincipalBench is a 75-item multi-turn benchmark with leak probes, dual judges, and an integrity-audit gate. Across 13 frontier subjects it exposes a sharp split (<=20% vs. 53.6-75.3% harm) invisible to single-turn safety evaluations: a selective cluster that declines adversarial probes while still following the principal's legitimate requests, and an over-refusing cluster that refuses broadly. (M1) A prompt-time loyalty scaffold (a fixed system prompt of seven prioritized rules, open-coded from 50+ failure trajectories) holds Claude-Sonnet to 19.4% harm and all nine selective subjects to <=20%. (M2) A per-token-KL distillation recipe transfers a prompted Qwen3-32B teacher into 8B Qwen3 and Llama-3.1 students, the strongest open-weight recipe we measure. (Lesson) Both mechanisms only move along a common leak/over-refusal trade-off rather than crossing it: improving one axis costs the other, and the jointly favorable outcome stays out of reach.

AI.tldr: A study of multi-party LLM agent loyalty problems with a benchmark showing agents split into selective vs over-refusing clusters, two mechanisms that improve loyalty but reveal an inherent trade-off between information leaks and over-refusal.
AI.motivation: Multi-party LLM agents must represent principals while interacting with counterparties whose interests may diverge, creating loyalty problems where "help whoever you're talking to" fails. Agents need to stay loyal to principals without over-refusing legitimate cooperative requests.
AI.method: 1) PrincipalBench: 75-item multi-turn benchmark with leak probes, dual judges, and integrity-audit gate to measure loyalty. 2) Prompt-time loyalty scaffold: Fixed system prompt of seven prioritized rules derived from failure trajectories. 3) Per-token-KL distillation: Transfers prompted teacher models into smaller student models.
AI.result: Benchmark reveals sharp split in frontier models (≤20% vs. 53.6-75.3% harm) invisible to single-turn evaluations. Selective cluster declines adversarial probes but follows legitimate requests; over-refusing cluster refuses broadly. Loyalty scaffold holds Claude-Sonnet to 19.4% harm and selective subjects to ≤20%. Distillation creates strongest open-weight models. Both mechanisms reveal fundamental leak/over-refusal trade-off.
AI.conclusion: Multi-party loyalty requires new measurement and mechanisms beyond single-turn safety evaluations. While improvements are possible through prompting and distillation, a fundamental trade-off exists between preventing information leaks and avoiding over-refusal, with jointly optimal outcomes remaining elusive.

title: The Joint Effect of Quantization and Sampling Temperature on LLM Safety Alignment: A Factorial Analysis
summary: Modern LLM deployments routinely compress models and raise sampling temperature to reduce cost, latency, or repetition, yet safety evaluations usually treat these choices as fixed implementation details. This leaves a practical uncertainty: does a model that is safe at FP16 and greedy decoding remain safe after it is quantized and sampled stochastically, or do the two deployment knobs amplify one another? We study this question with a factorial evaluation of 9 instruction-tuned models from six families, 3 precisions (FP16, GPTQ INT8, AWQ INT4), and 6 temperatures ($T{=}0$ to $1.0$), yielding 161 configurations and $\approx$322k responses judged by a six-model safety ensemble. Contrary to the concern that low-bit deployment broadly erodes alignment, standard non-adversarial quantization is usually safety-neutral: INT4 keeps or lowers attack success for 7 of 9 models, with clear degradation concentrated in the weakest baseline model, SmolLM3-3B ($18.5\%{\to}36.0\%$). The larger risk comes from sampling: higher temperature sharply increases decision instability for vulnerable models, with DFR reaching 53.0\% at $T{=}1.0$, even when average ASR changes modestly. Finally, the interaction is not a ``double penalty'': our Compound Degradation Index remains largely sub-additive ($-0.195$ to $+0.045$), indicating that quantization and temperature do not systematically compound. These results suggest a deployment rule of thumb: standard INT4/INT8 quantization can be reasonable for strongly aligned models, but safety claims at elevated temperature should report multi-sample stability, not only average attack success.

AI.tldr: Standard non-adversarial quantization (INT4/INT8) is generally safety-neutral for well-aligned models, while higher temperature sampling poses greater safety risks by increasing decision instability, especially for vulnerable models.
AI.motivation: Modern LLM deployments often compress models (quantization) and use stochastic sampling (higher temperature) for cost/latency reduction, but safety evaluations typically treat these as fixed implementation details. There's uncertainty about whether these deployment choices amplify each other's safety risks or if quantization broadly erodes model alignment.
AI.method: Conducted a factorial evaluation of 9 instruction-tuned models from 6 families, testing 3 precisions (FP16, GPTQ INT8, AWQ INT4), and 6 temperatures (0 to 1.0), yielding 161 configurations. Generated ≈322k responses judged by a six-model safety ensemble to measure attack success rate (ASR) and decision instability.
AI.result: INT4 quantization kept or lowered attack success for 7 of 9 models, with clear degradation only in the weakest baseline model. Higher temperature sharply increases decision instability for vulnerable models, with Decision Fluctuation Rate (DFR) reaching 53.0% at T=1.0. Quantization and temperature do not systematically compound risks (Compound Degradation Index was largely sub-additive).
AI.conclusion: Standard INT4/INT8 quantization is reasonable for strongly aligned models, but safety evaluations at elevated temperatures should report multi-sample decision stability, not just average attack success rates. The primary safety risk comes from sampling temperature rather than quantization itself.

title: ENC-ODE: Event-level Neurodegenerative Modeling in Continuous Time with Neural ODEs
summary: Accurately predicting the temporal evolution of clinical biomarkers is crucial for the early diagnosis and management of neurodegenerative diseases such as Alzheimer's disease. However, this relies on longitudinal data to capture biomarker changes over time, which is often sparse and irregular due to the high cost, labor-intensive nature, and patient burden. To address these challenges, we propose ENC-ODE, an Event-level Neurodegenerative modeling in Continuous time with neural Ordinary Differential Equations. ENC-ODE predicts future biomarker evolution by modeling clinical events through diagnosis-conditioned continuous dynamics. A target-conditioned attention mechanism weights and aggregates event-level predictions for the target time and modality without history compression. Extensive experiments on Alzheimer's Disease Neuroimaging Initiative (ADNI) dataset demonstrate that ENC-ODE outperforms representative sequence models while offering a scalable and neuroscientifically grounded solution for clinical support. The code is available at https://github.com/JardinDelSol/enc-ode.

AI.tldr: ENC-ODE: A neural ODE-based model for predicting Alzheimer's disease biomarker evolution using continuous-time modeling of clinical events without history compression
AI.motivation: Predicting clinical biomarker evolution is crucial for early diagnosis of neurodegenerative diseases like Alzheimer's, but longitudinal data is often sparse, irregular, costly, and burdensome to collect.
AI.method: ENC-ODE uses neural Ordinary Differential Equations to model clinical events through diagnosis-conditioned continuous dynamics, with a target-conditioned attention mechanism that weights and aggregates event-level predictions without history compression.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on ADNI dataset show ENC-ODE outperforms representative sequence models while offering scalable and neuroscientifically grounded clinical support.
AI.conclusion: ENC-ODE provides an effective solution for predicting biomarker evolution in Alzheimer's disease using continuous-time modeling that handles sparse, irregular data better than existing approaches.

title: Bilevel Optimization for Neural Architecture Search
summary: Bilevel optimization has become an influential and widely adopted framework for addressing hierarchical optimization problems in machine learning, providing an effective approach to modeling the interaction between two levels of optimization, with applications such as hyperparameter tuning, meta-learning, adversarial training, and data poisoning. Neural Architecture Search (NAS), a subfield of hyperparameter optimization, is a prime example of a bilevel optimization problem, with architecture parameters optimized at the outer-level and network weights optimized at the inner level. This paper presents a structured overview of NAS through the lens of bilevel optimization. We categorize existing NAS approaches into two main classes: sampling-based methods, which search optimal architectures using different architecture samplers, and bilevel theory-based methods, which solve the architecture search problem using bilevel optimization principles. We further highlight our current research direction, wherein the bilevel NAS formulation is addressed through an auxiliary mathematical programming framework. This framework enables the systematic integration of second-order information from the model's training loss function and ensures the optimality of the model parameters while modifying architecture parameters. By simultaneously updating the architecture and model parameters along their respective optimal descent directions derived from the auxiliary mathematical program, these methods achieve more principled and theoretically consistent results. The same auxiliary program can also be used for simultaneous hyperparameter and model fine-tuning. A comparative analysis shows that bilevel theory-based approaches generally outperform sampling-based methods, both in accuracy and efficiency.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a structured overview of Neural Architecture Search (NAS) framed as bilevel optimization, categorizing approaches into sampling-based and bilevel theory-based methods, with the latter generally outperforming the former in accuracy and efficiency.
AI.motivation: Bilevel optimization has become influential for hierarchical optimization problems in ML, with NAS being a prime example where architecture parameters are optimized at the outer level and network weights at the inner level. There's a need for a structured framework to understand and improve NAS approaches through bilevel optimization principles.
AI.method: Categorizes NAS approaches into: 1) Sampling-based methods that search architectures using different samplers, and 2) Bilevel theory-based methods that solve architecture search using bilevel optimization principles. Proposes an auxiliary mathematical programming framework to systematically integrate second-order information and ensure optimality while updating both architecture and model parameters simultaneously.
AI.result: Comparative analysis shows that bilevel theory-based approaches generally outperform sampling-based methods in both accuracy and efficiency. The auxiliary mathematical programming framework enables more principled and theoretically consistent results.
AI.conclusion: Viewing NAS through bilevel optimization provides a powerful framework for understanding and improving architecture search. The auxiliary mathematical programming approach offers systematic integration of optimization principles and can also be extended to simultaneous hyperparameter and model fine-tuning, making bilevel theory-based methods superior to sampling-based approaches.

title: The FIL Hypothesis: Inductive Biases Help with Kernel Engineering
summary: The Bitter Lesson, which posits that general-purpose methods that scale with computation and data ultimately outperform those with built-in human knowledge, has become a dominant paradigm in the era of Large Language Models. We revisit this principle by observing a new and critical scaling dimension: the duration of the Feedback Information Loop (FIL), the time required for a system to receive a verification signal after generating a prediction. Most historic successes in Artificial Intelligence (AI) have benefited from near instantaneous feedback (e.g., games or classification tasks), but we argue that future AI applications in science and the physical world will inherently involve FILs ranging from hours to weeks. This trend poses a fundamental scaling limit, as obtaining enough verification steps required by purely data-driven methods becomes practically impossible. Additionally, we propose a method that is orthogonal to purely data-driven approaches, based on human-inspired expert knowledge. The method relies on inductive biases and constraining the solution space. We provide an initial validation of the hypothesis and the method, by studying the real-world GPU programming task, a domain with non-trivial FIL, and demonstrate that incorporating inductive biases yields superior performance over data-driven approaches. The code is released under: https://github.com/ai-nikolai/robust_kernelbench

AI.tldr: The paper challenges the "Bitter Lesson" paradigm by identifying a new scaling limitation - the duration of Feedback Information Loops (FIL) in real-world applications, and proposes using expert knowledge and inductive biases instead of purely data-driven approaches for domains with long feedback cycles.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to address a fundamental limitation in current AI paradigms: most successful AI systems rely on near-instant feedback, but future applications in science and the physical world will involve much longer feedback loops (hours to weeks), making purely data-driven methods impractical due to insufficient verification steps.
AI.method: The method proposes incorporating human-inspired expert knowledge through inductive biases and constraining the solution space, which is orthogonal to purely data-driven approaches. This is validated using GPU programming as a test domain with non-trivial feedback loops.
AI.result: The paper demonstrates that incorporating inductive biases yields superior performance over data-driven approaches in the real-world GPU programming task, providing initial validation for their hypothesis about the limitations of the "Bitter Lesson" paradigm in domains with long feedback cycles.
AI.conclusion: The "Bitter Lesson" paradigm has scaling limitations in domains with long feedback information loops, and incorporating expert knowledge through inductive biases offers a viable alternative approach for such applications where purely data-driven methods are impractical.

title: STEMGym: Benchmarking Sequential Decision-Making under Dose Budgets in Autonomous Electron Microscopy
summary: A central premise of autonomous scientific imaging is that smarter navigation, whether Bayesian, RL-based, or otherwise adaptive, is the principal lever for sample-efficient acquisition. We present evidence to the contrary in scanning transmission electron microscopy (STEM), an atomic-resolution imaging modality whose every measurement deposits damaging electron dose. We introduce STEMGym, an open-source Gymnasium benchmark of 15 physics-simulated STEM worlds spanning five materials, three difficulty levels, and four characterisation tasks, scored by the Dose-Efficiency Curve area (DEC-AUC), a single scalar capturing the information-vs-dose Pareto frontier. Across 33 agent configurations under realistic dose budgets, the dominant determinant of dose efficiency is the analyst (perception) pipeline, not the navigator: pairing a trained CNN analyst with naïve raster scanning raises DEC-AUC by 5.5x over a CNN-free raster baseline (0.287 vs.\ 0.052), while substituting Bayesian or adaptive finite-state-machine navigation for raster yields no statistically significant further gain. Production-tier vision-language models further underperform task-specific CNNs by {\sim}13x on crystallographic defect analysis. By decoupling perception, navigation, and planning under a unified dose budget, STEMGym reframes where ML effort should be invested in autonomous electron microscopy and provides the measurement infrastructure to test it.

AI.tldr: In autonomous electron microscopy, smarter navigation strategies don't significantly improve sample efficiency - perception/analysis pipelines are 5.5x more important than navigation algorithms for dose-efficient atomic imaging.
AI.motivation: The paper challenges the central premise in autonomous scientific imaging that smarter navigation algorithms (Bayesian, RL-based, adaptive) are the key to sample-efficient acquisition. In scanning transmission electron microscopy (STEM), where every measurement causes electron damage, there's a need to understand what truly drives dose efficiency.
AI.method: The authors introduce STEMGym, an open-source Gymnasium benchmark with 15 physics-simulated STEM worlds spanning five materials, three difficulty levels, and four characterization tasks. They use Dose-Efficiency Curve area (DEC-AUC) as a single scalar metric capturing the information-vs-dose Pareto frontier. They evaluate 33 agent configurations under realistic dose budgets, comparing perception pipelines vs navigation strategies.
AI.result: The dominant determinant of dose efficiency is the analyst (perception) pipeline, not the navigator. Pairing a trained CNN analyst with naïve raster scanning raises DEC-AUC by 5.5x over CNN-free raster baseline (0.287 vs. 0.052). Substituting Bayesian or adaptive finite-state-machine navigation for raster yields no statistically significant further gain. Production-tier vision-language models underperform task-specific CNNs by ~13x on crystallographic defect analysis.
AI.conclusion: STEMGym reframes where ML effort should be invested in autonomous electron microscopy - perception pipelines are far more important than navigation algorithms for dose efficiency. The benchmark provides measurement infrastructure to test this and guide future research investments.

title: Entity Binding Failures in Tool-Augmented Agents
summary: Tool-augmented language-model agents are often evaluated by whether they select the correct tool, produce valid API arguments, and complete the requested task. However, an agent may choose the right tool and still act on the wrong external entity. For example, a request to "email Alex about the launch" may lead the agent to contact the wrong Alex, attach the wrong launch document, reply in the wrong thread, or update the wrong customer account. We call these errors entity binding failures. This paper studies entity binding failures as a distinct reliability and safety problem in tool-augmented agents. We formalize the separation between tool correctness and entity correctness, introduce a taxonomy of wrong-entity failures in enterprise workflows, and evaluate entity-aware execution mechanisms including entity-resolution preconditions, confidence-gated binding, clarification under ambiguity, and provenance tracking. In a controlled diagnostic evaluation across 60 tasks, five model backends, and six tool-use methods, all methods achieved 0.0 percent wrong-tool error, yet action-oriented baselines still produced wrong-entity actions in 24.0-26.0 percent of runs. Entity-aware methods eliminated wrong-entity actions and risk-weighted wrong-entity exposure in this setting, but reduced direct task completion by deferring under ambiguity. These findings show that safe tool use requires not only selecting the correct tool, but also reliably binding natural-language references to the correct real-world entity before action.

AI.tldr: The paper identifies "entity binding failures" as a critical reliability problem in tool-augmented language model agents - when agents select the right tool but act on the wrong external entity (e.g., wrong person, wrong document, wrong account).
AI.motivation: Current evaluations focus on whether agents select correct tools and produce valid API arguments, but ignore the critical problem of correctly binding natural language references to real-world entities before taking action. Agents may choose the right tool but still fail by acting on the wrong entity, posing serious reliability and safety risks.
AI.method: The paper formalizes separation between tool correctness and entity correctness, introduces a taxonomy of wrong-entity failures in enterprise workflows, and evaluates entity-aware execution mechanisms including: entity-resolution preconditions, confidence-gated binding, clarification under ambiguity, and provenance tracking. Evaluated across 60 tasks, five model backends, and six tool-use methods in controlled diagnostic evaluation.
AI.result: All methods achieved 0.0% wrong-tool error, but action-oriented baselines still produced wrong-entity actions in 24.0-26.0% of runs. Entity-aware methods eliminated wrong-entity actions and risk-weighted wrong-entity exposure, but reduced direct task completion by deferring under ambiguity.
AI.conclusion: Safe tool use requires not only selecting the correct tool, but also reliably binding natural-language references to the correct real-world entity before action. Entity binding failures represent a distinct reliability and safety problem that needs to be addressed separately from tool selection accuracy.

title: How AI settled the complexity of the oldest SGD algorithm
summary: In 1937, Stefan Kaczmarz proposed a simple algorithm for solving systems of linear equations. This algorithm turned out to be the earliest known example of stochastic gradient descent, a ubiquitous computing paradigm that drives the training of modern AI models such as ChatGPT and Gemini. Now, those AI models have joined forces to discover the worst-case complexity of the Kaczmarz algorithm. This paper tells the story of how it happened.

AI.tldr: The paper explores the historical connection between the 1937 Kaczmarz algorithm (an early form of stochastic gradient descent) and modern AI models, and how these AI models were used to analyze the algorithm's worst-case complexity.
AI.motivation: To examine the historical significance of the Kaczmarz algorithm as an early example of stochastic gradient descent, which now powers modern AI systems, and to demonstrate how these same AI models can be used to analyze the algorithm's computational properties.
AI.method: The paper appears to use modern AI models (like those behind ChatGPT and Gemini) to analyze and discover the worst-case complexity of the Kaczmarz algorithm, creating a circular connection where the algorithm's descendants are used to study it.
AI.result: The AI models successfully discovered the worst-case complexity of the Kaczmarz algorithm, demonstrating that these modern AI systems can be applied to analyze fundamental mathematical algorithms.
AI.conclusion: The paper highlights the historical continuity in algorithmic development - from the simple 1937 Kaczmarz algorithm to modern AI systems, and shows how these AI systems can now contribute back to understanding their mathematical foundations through computational analysis.

title: Latent Actions from Factorized Transition Effects under Agent Ambiguity
comment: Accepted to ICML 2026 Workshop on Compositional Learning. Project Page: https://hazel-heejeong-nam.github.io/LAM/
summary: Latent Action Models (LAMs) learn action-like proxies from observation transitions. However, in multi-object or distractor-rich scenes, these visual effects mix agent motion with distractors, camera dynamics, and background changes, making the underlying action source ambiguous without supervision. Structuring this mixture as reusable transition effects provides an intermediate representation from which action-like latents can be more robustly formed. We introduce Observed Transition Factorization (OTF), which decomposes each transition into a sparse set of observed transition primitives. Using these primitives as the transition interface, we propose OTF-LAM, which abstracts motion primitives into action-like latents within the standard inverse-forward dynamics framework, and OTF-LAM-Dino, a decoder-free variant that predicts future states in a frozen DINOv2 representation space. Empirically, OTF primitives transfer zeroshot across controlled carrier and morphology shifts, showing reusability. Furthermore, downstream policy learning results match or outperform baselines under complex transition ambiguity.

AI.tldr: OTF-LAM learns robust action-like latents by decomposing visual transitions into reusable primitives, handling distractors and camera motion without supervision, and shows strong zero-shot transfer and policy learning performance.
AI.motivation: Existing Latent Action Models (LAMs) struggle in multi-object or distractor-rich scenes where visual transitions mix agent motion with distractors, camera dynamics, and background changes, creating ambiguous action sources without supervision.
AI.method: Introduces Observed Transition Factorization (OTF) which decomposes each transition into a sparse set of observed transition primitives. Uses these primitives as the transition interface, with two variants: OTF-LAM (abstracts motion primitives into action-like latents within inverse-forward dynamics framework) and OTF-LAM-Dino (decoder-free variant predicting future states in frozen DINOv2 representation space).
AI.result: OTF primitives demonstrate zero-shot transfer across controlled carrier and morphology shifts, showing reusability. Downstream policy learning results match or outperform baselines under complex transition ambiguity.
AI.conclusion: Structuring visual transitions as reusable primitives provides an effective intermediate representation that enables robust learning of action-like latents in complex, ambiguous environments without supervision.

title: Mechanistically Eliciting Latent Behaviors in Language Models
summary: We aim to discover diverse, generalizable perturbations of LLM internals that can surface hidden behavioral modes. Such perturbations could help reshape model behavior and systematically evaluate potential risks. We introduce Causal Perturbative Elicitation (CPE), an unsupervised method for discovering interpretable low-rank adapters (LoRAs) that can elicit these latent behaviors. CPE decomposes the computations of a deep transformer slice using a heuristic tensor-decomposition-based algorithm. CPE exhibits remarkable data efficiency, learning a large number of interpretable LoRAs from a single example. Even though CPE is unsupervised, we find that in some cases it can be competitive with supervised elicitation methods via brute-force enumerative search over weight space. For instance, CPE performs similarly to matched-wall-clock-time GRPO on the Countdown task for Qwen3-8B (85% vs 87%), demonstrating that CPE can efficiently elicit complex multi-token behaviors. Since CPE is unsupervised, it can also surface hidden failure modes, such as sandbagging, restoring 85% of locked BigCodeBench performance on a password-locked version of Llama3-70B introduced by Taylor et al. (2025). Additionally, since CPE explores behaviors in weight-space rather than token-space it can potentially ameliorate exploration hacking, a misalignment failure which may arise in sufficiently self-aware AI models (Ngo, 2022). In fact, we find that CPE virtually eliminates alignment-faking (Greenblatt et al., 2024) behavior in a Llama3-70B-based model organism developed by Hughes et al. (2025). Finally, we find that CPE can be used to initialize GPT-OSS-20B in an aligned basin when running GRPO on an environment prone to reward-hacking. By providing a data-efficient method to systematically explore the space of latent model behaviors, CPE yields a powerful tool for aligning AI systems and evaluating their safety.

AI.tldr: CPE is an unsupervised method that discovers interpretable low-rank adapters (LoRAs) through tensor decomposition to elicit latent behaviors and hidden failure modes in LLMs, offering a data-efficient tool for AI safety and alignment.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to discover diverse, generalizable perturbations of LLM internals to surface hidden behavioral modes, which could help reshape model behavior and systematically evaluate potential risks in AI systems.
AI.method: CPE (Causal Perturbative Elicitation) is an unsupervised method that uses a heuristic tensor-decomposition-based algorithm to decompose the computations of a deep transformer slice, discovering interpretable low-rank adapters (LoRAs) from minimal data.
AI.result: CPE shows remarkable data efficiency (learning from single examples), competitive performance with supervised methods (85% vs 87% on Countdown task), restores locked performance (85% on password-locked models), eliminates alignment-faking behavior, and helps initialize models in aligned basins for reward-hacking-prone environments.
AI.conclusion: CPE provides a powerful, data-efficient method to systematically explore latent model behaviors in weight-space rather than token-space, offering valuable tools for AI alignment and safety evaluation while potentially ameliorating exploration hacking issues.

title: Does Role Specialization Matter for Explanation Faithfulness in Mixture-of-Experts?
summary: Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architectures have recently been extended with role-based mechanisms for interpretability. This is typically done by assigning semantic roles to individual expert components, for example roles like synergy, redundancy, and uniqueness in multimodal settings. However, whether such structural role decomposition preserves explanation faithfulness of the overall architecture remains largely underexplored. We hypothesize that inter-expert representation overlap weakens effective role separation and degrades attribution-based faithfulness, even when semantic roles are explicitly defined. To address this limitation, we introduce representation-level decorrelation regularization to explicitly reduce inter-expert similarity in latent space. Using representation decorrelation objectives, we encourage clearer specialization among experts by minimizing representation overlap. Our experiments show that across multiple multimodal benchmarks, this separation consistently improves explanation faithfulness, as measured by comprehensiveness, sufficiency, and their Area Over the Perturbation Curve (AOPC) summaries, while preserving task performance. We further show that these improvements are not limited to role-based architectures such as Interpretable Multimodal Interaction-aware MoE (I2MoE). Similar trends are observed in a standard sparse MoE baseline, suggesting that representation-level separation may provide a more general mechanism for enhancing explanation faithfulness in MoE systems. Overall, our findings suggest that structural role decomposition alone may be insufficient to guarantee faithful explanations and that representation-level separation helps improve explanation faithfulness. To support reproducibility, the source code and supplementary material are publicly available at https://github.com/dut0817/FL-I2MoE_Decor.

AI.tldr: Adding representation decorrelation regularization to Mixture-of-Experts architectures improves explanation faithfulness by reducing inter-expert similarity, even when semantic roles are explicitly defined.
AI.motivation: Current role-based MoE architectures assign semantic roles to experts (e.g., synergy, redundancy in multimodal settings), but it's unclear whether this structural decomposition actually preserves explanation faithfulness. The authors hypothesize that inter-expert representation overlap weakens role separation and degrades attribution-based faithfulness.
AI.method: Introduces representation-level decorrelation regularization to explicitly reduce inter-expert similarity in latent space. Uses representation decorrelation objectives to minimize representation overlap and encourage clearer specialization among experts.
AI.result: Experiments across multiple multimodal benchmarks show that representation separation consistently improves explanation faithfulness (measured by comprehensiveness, sufficiency, and AOPC summaries) while preserving task performance. Improvements are observed in both role-based architectures (I2MoE) and standard sparse MoE baselines.
AI.conclusion: Structural role decomposition alone is insufficient to guarantee faithful explanations in MoE systems. Representation-level separation provides a more general mechanism for enhancing explanation faithfulness, as decorrelation regularization helps improve both interpretability and faithfulness while maintaining performance.

title: The Human Creativity Benchmark
summary: Modern AI evaluation frameworks treat evaluator disagreement as noise to be resolved. In creative domains, professional disagreement reflects genuine differences in taste, not measurement error. We argue that evaluating creative AI requires preserving two distinct signals: convergence, where professionals align around shared best practices, and divergence, where individual taste legitimately varies. We present the Human Creativity Benchmark (HCB), a benchmark that operationalizes this separation by collecting pairwise preferences, scalar ratings on prompt adherence, usability, and visual appeal, and qualitative rationale from domain professionals. Across 15,000 professional judgments spanning five creative domains and three workflow phases (ideation, mockup, refinement), we find that convergence concentrates on verifiable dimensions like technical correctness and visual hierarchy, while divergence concentrates on taste-driven dimensions like aesthetic direction and conceptual risk. No model excels uniformly across all phases. Collapsing these signals into a single quality metric discards the most actionable information: where models must be correct versus where they should remain steerable.

AI.tldr: Creative AI evaluation frameworks should separate convergence (shared best practices) and divergence (individual taste) signals rather than treating professional disagreement as noise.
AI.motivation: Modern AI evaluation frameworks treat evaluator disagreement as measurement error, but in creative domains, professional disagreement reflects genuine differences in taste, not noise.
AI.method: The authors present the Human Creativity Benchmark (HCB), which operationalizes the convergence-divergence separation by collecting pairwise preferences, scalar ratings on prompt adherence, usability, visual appeal, and qualitative rationale from domain professionals across 15,000 judgments.
AI.result: Across five creative domains and three workflow phases (ideation, mockup, refinement), convergence concentrates on verifiable dimensions like technical correctness and visual hierarchy, while divergence concentrates on taste-driven dimensions like aesthetic direction and conceptual risk. No model excels uniformly across all phases.
AI.conclusion: Collapsing convergence and divergence signals into a single quality metric discards the most actionable information: understanding where models must be technically correct versus where they should remain steerable to accommodate individual taste.

title: t-STEP: An interpretable model for Total Electron Content predictions and irregularities estimations
comment: 40 pages, 15 figures. Note that the article has been published in the Earth Science Informatics Journal
summary: Earth system infrastructures relying on satellite-based technologies, such as Global Positioning System (GPS) communications, are affected by ionospheric Total Electron Content (TEC) gradients. Modeling these gradients under physical constraints remains challenging due to their dynamic and transient nature. While existing machine learning (ML) models can predict hourly TEC variations, it remains unclear whether their temporal resolution is sufficient to preserve small-scale TEC irregularities within predicted signals. To address this gap, we introduce an interpretable ML-based model, t-STEP, designed to predict TEC at a 30-second resolution and estimate irregularity signatures from the modeled signals. This high cadence enables the derivation of Rate of TEC changes (ROT) and the ROT Index (ROTI) as diagnostic indicators of ionospheric variability. The model is developed using GPS observations from solar cycle 24 at a station located at 5.49°S, 47.49°W. A multi-metric evaluation framework, including dynamic time warping, is used for robustness assessment, while SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations) provides insight into feature contributions. The 30-second TEC predictions achieve 91% accuracy with a mean absolute error (MAE) of 4.38 TECU during high solar activity (2015). Compared with the International Reference Ionosphere (IRI-2020), the hourly model improves accuracy by 35%, reduces absolute errors by 57%, and increases prediction skill by 54%. More importantly, the 30-second model captures TEC irregularity dynamics and morphologies during geomagnetic storms of different intensities, outperforming an attention-based Long Short-Term Memory model under the same experimental conditions. This study demonstrates the potential of a single TEC prediction framework for scalable irregularity monitoring without requiring separate models for individual transient events.

AI.tldr: t-STEP, an interpretable ML model, predicts TEC at 30-second resolution to capture ionospheric irregularities, outperforming existing models and enabling scalable monitoring.
AI.motivation: Earth system infrastructures relying on satellite-based technologies, such as GPS communications, are affected by ionospheric TEC gradients. Modeling these gradients under physical constraints remains challenging due to their dynamic and transient nature. While existing ML models can predict hourly TEC variations, it remains unclear whether their temporal resolution is sufficient to preserve small-scale TEC irregularities within predicted signals.
AI.method: Introduce interpretable ML-based model t-STEP, designed to predict TEC at 30-second resolution and estimate irregularity signatures from modeled signals. High cadence enables derivation of ROT and ROTI as diagnostic indicators. Model developed using GPS observations from solar cycle 24 at a station located at 5.49°S, 47.49°W. Multi-metric evaluation framework including dynamic time warping for robustness assessment, SHAP for feature contributions.
AI.result: 30-second TEC predictions achieve 91% accuracy with a mean absolute error (MAE) of 4.38 TECU during high solar activity (2015). Compared with the International Reference Ionosphere (IRI-2020), the hourly model improves accuracy by 35%, reduces absolute errors by 57%, and increases prediction skill by 54%. More importantly, the 30-second model captures TEC irregularity dynamics and morphologies during geomagnetic storms of different intensities, outperforming an attention-based Long Short-Term Memory model under the same experimental conditions.
AI.conclusion: This study demonstrates the potential of a single TEC prediction framework for scalable irregularity monitoring without requiring separate models for individual transient events.

title: DOPD: Dual On-policy Distillation
summary: On-policy distillation (OPD) offers superior capacity transfer by supervising student-sampled trajectories with dense token-level signals. To furnish high-quality supervision sources and thereby elevate the performance frontier of distillation, an intuitive direction is to infuse privileged information to either teacher or student itself. However, this additional input induces a potential failure mode we dub privilege illusion: a pattern that conflates the transferable capability gap that students are meant to close, and the information asymmetry gap that can only be mimicked but never replicated. This issue is further amplified by the inherent non-uniformity of token-level supervision, where only a small subset of tokens carries pivotal capability-bearing signals. To this end, we propose DOPD, an advantage-aware dual distillation paradigm that dynamically routes token-level supervision between privileged teacher and privileged student policies based on their advantage gap and relative probabilities. Each token receives supervision of different strength, objective, and strategy from either teacher or student itself, which transfers credible capability while simultaneously receiving auxiliary signals, to alleviate privilege illusion. Extensive experiments on both large language model (LLM) and vision-language model (VLM) settings demonstrate that DOPD consistently outperforms Vanilla OPD and other counterparts. Further results on stability, robustness, continual learning, and out-of-distribution tasks validate its superiority.

AI.tldr: DOPD is a dual distillation method that dynamically routes token-level supervision between privileged teacher and student to address privilege illusion and improve knowledge transfer.
AI.motivation: On-policy distillation suffers from privilege illusion where privileged information creates an information asymmetry gap that students can't fully replicate, and token-level supervision is non-uniform with only a few tokens carrying crucial signals.
AI.method: Proposes DOPD, an advantage-aware dual distillation that dynamically routes token-level supervision between privileged teacher and privileged student based on their advantage gap and relative probabilities, with each token receiving supervision of different strength, objective, and strategy.
AI.result: DOPD consistently outperforms Vanilla OPD and other counterparts in LLM and VLM settings, showing superior stability, robustness, continual learning, and out-of-distribution performance.
AI.conclusion: DOPD effectively mitigates privilege illusion in on-policy distillation by dynamically routing supervision between privileged teacher and student, leading to better knowledge transfer and performance.

title: Fuzzing Large Language Models to Elicit Hidden Behaviours
summary: Sleeper agents are the canonical model organism of deception: models trained to behave normally but to emit an unsafe behaviour on a specific trigger. Eliciting that behaviour without knowing the trigger has not been studied systematically. We study fuzzing: injecting Gaussian noise into a model's weights or residual-stream activations and checking whether the perturbed outputs reveal the behaviour. On 6 backdoored models (7B-13B) we compare both forms of fuzzing head-to-head against temperature-sampling baselines. Fuzzing elicits the hidden behaviour more often than temperature sampling on 4 of 6 models (up to ~6x on OpenHermes-13B), and which form wins depends on the task, so both are worth running. Elicitation is uneven across each method's hyperparameter grid: a uniform sweep gives only a few percent on most models, while the best cell is 2-10x higher, so the bottleneck is hyperparameter selection, not the technique. To select hyperparameters without ground-truth access, we use a cheap proxy task (in-context secret elicitation, where a base64-encoded secret is placed in the system prompt for the model to hide) and run Thompson sampling on it to pick candidate cells, which we evaluate on the real backdoor. On the four models that can decode the secret, proxy-selected cells raise activation-fuzzing elicitation ~4x over the uniform-sweep mean (recovering ~70% of the best-cell rate on the best performing model) and weight-fuzzing by 1.3-1.8x. To our knowledge this is the first systematic study of fuzzing on sleeper-agent backdoors and the first to show proxy-task hyperparameter selection transferring to real-task elicitation. We also propose reporting such results as a (uniform-baseline, proxy-selected, oracle) triple, since these are three distinct claims that prior work has often blurred.

AI.tldr: Fuzzing (injecting Gaussian noise into weights or activations) can elicit hidden behaviors in backdoored language models better than temperature sampling, and proxy-task hyperparameter selection via Thompson sampling improves elicitation rates.
AI.motivation: To systematically study how to elicit hidden behaviors in sleeper-agent models (backdoored LLMs) without knowing the trigger, comparing different fuzzing methods against baselines and addressing hyperparameter selection challenges.
AI.method: Compare weight-fuzzing and activation-fuzzing (injecting Gaussian noise) against temperature-sampling baselines on 6 backdoored models (7B-13B). Use a proxy task (in-context secret elicitation with base64-encoded secret) and Thompson sampling to select hyperparameters without ground truth, then evaluate on real backdoors.
AI.result: Fuzzing elicits hidden behavior more often than temperature sampling on 4 of 6 models (up to ~6x on OpenHermes-13B). Proxy-selected cells improve activation-fuzzing elicitation ~4x over uniform-sweep mean and weight-fuzzing by 1.3-1.8x, recovering ~70% of best-cell rate on best model.
AI.conclusion: Fuzzing is effective for eliciting sleeper-agent behaviors, with hyperparameter selection via proxy tasks being crucial. Results should be reported as (uniform-baseline, proxy-selected, oracle) triple to clarify distinct claims.

title: Self-Evolving World Models for LLM Agent Planning
summary: World models offer a principled way to equip long-horizon LLM agents with foresight: predictions of action consequences before execution. However, unreliable foresight can be ignored, misused, or even degrade downstream decision-making. In this paper, we introduce WorldEvolver, a self-evolving world model framework that revises its deployment-time context while keeping the downstream agent and all model parameters frozen. WorldEvolver integrates three modules: (i) Episodic Memory, which exploits real action transitions through retrieval-based simulation; (ii) Semantic Memory, which extracts persistent heuristic rules from prediction-observation mismatches; and (iii) Selective Foresight, which filters low-confidence predictions before integrating them into agent reasoning context. We evaluate WorldEvolver on ALFWorld and ScienceWorld, measuring world model prediction accuracy on Word2World and downstream agent success rate on AgentBoard. Extensive experiments show that WorldEvolver achieves the highest prediction accuracy across three backbones and leads other world model baselines on downstream agent success rate, demonstrating that test-time memory revision enhances both predictive fidelity and planning performance.

AI.tldr: WorldEvolver is a self-evolving world model framework that improves foresight reliability for LLM agents by revising deployment-time context using episodic memory, semantic memory, and selective foresight.
AI.motivation: Unreliable foresight in world models can be ignored, misused, or degrade downstream decision-making for long-horizon LLM agents, highlighting the need for more reliable prediction mechanisms.
AI.method: WorldEvolver integrates three modules: (i) Episodic Memory for retrieval-based simulation of real action transitions; (ii) Semantic Memory to extract persistent heuristic rules from prediction-observation mismatches; (iii) Selective Foresight to filter low-confidence predictions before integrating them into the agent's reasoning context. The framework revises deployment-time context while keeping the downstream agent and all model parameters frozen.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on ALFWorld and ScienceWorld show WorldEvolver achieves the highest prediction accuracy across three backbones and outperforms other world model baselines on downstream agent success rate (measured on AgentBoard).
AI.conclusion: Test-time memory revision enhances both predictive fidelity and planning performance, as demonstrated by WorldEvolver's superior accuracy and downstream success rates.

title: I-BBS: Coordinate-Free Inference of Latent Sub-Manifolds Using Random Distance Matrix Theory
summary: Bogomolny, Bohigas and Schmit (BBS) found that the spectrum of the pairwise distance matrix on N points sampled from a smooth d-dimensional manifold encodes a signature of the underlying geometry. We develop I-BBS (Inference-BBS), a coordinate-free method that identifies a low-dimensional latent sub-manifold embedded in a high-dimensional ambient distance matrix alone, without accessing an ambient high-dimensional vector space. It therefore applies even when that space is only partly observable or undefined. We model the ambient embedding by two classes of generative noise, model-based and model-free. The noise mixes the latent signal with off-manifold components, so the eigenvalues reorganise collectively and the latent geometry cannot be read off eigenvalue by eigenvalue. We recover it instead from two integer-stable signatures that survive the noise: the multiplicity of the top non-Perron multiplet, which fixes $d$, and a parameter-free law for how the multiplet positions shrink as the noise grows. On synthetic spheres $S^1$, $S^2$ and $S^3$ these integer signatures are far more stable under noise than the continuous spectral slope, and a blind test recovers both the manifold and the noise model from a single distance matrix. Applications to neural-network representations and to the dynamic training regime are developed in two companion papers.

AI.tldr: I-BBS is a coordinate-free method that identifies low-dimensional latent manifolds embedded in high-dimensional distance matrices without requiring access to the original vector space, using noise-robust integer signatures for stable dimension estimation.
AI.motivation: The BBS method showed that distance matrices encode geometric signatures, but real-world data often has noisy or incomplete high-dimensional embeddings where the original vector space may be partially observable or undefined. There's a need for methods that can extract latent geometry directly from distance matrices in such cases.
AI.method: I-BBS (Inference-BBS) develops a coordinate-free approach that models ambient embeddings with two classes of generative noise (model-based and model-free) that mix latent signals with off-manifold components. It recovers latent geometry using two integer-stable signatures: 1) multiplicity of the top non-Perron multiplet that fixes dimension d, and 2) a parameter-free law for how multiplet positions shrink as noise grows. These signatures remain stable under noise unlike continuous spectral slopes.
AI.result: On synthetic spheres S¹, S², and S³, the integer signatures are far more stable under noise than continuous spectral slopes. A blind test recovers both the manifold and noise model from a single distance matrix. Applications to neural network representations and dynamic training regimes are developed in companion papers.
AI.conclusion: I-BBS provides a robust, coordinate-free method for extracting low-dimensional latent manifolds from high-dimensional distance matrices even when the original vector space is incomplete or undefined, using noise-resistant integer signatures for dimension estimation and manifold identification.

title: Learning as Observable Matrix Dynamics: Diffusive Relaxations versus Phase Transitions
summary: Observable Matrix Dynamics (OMD) is a diagnostic framework that probes the dynamics of high-dimensional internal representations of inputs by a neural network via a fixed-size $N \times N$ distance matrix $M(t)$ on a held set of $N$ inputs. OMD uses methods of random matrix theory and particle dynamics to explore spectral reorganisations that are missed by scalar loss functions, but are informative of the training process. We read $M(t)$ against a perturbative ambient-versus-latent decomposition extending the Bogomolny--Bohigas--Schmit (BBS) theory of random distance matrices, with per-snapshot diagnostics for the top-of-spectrum band structure and ambient noise, trajectory-level observables linking snapshots, and a 3D MDS embedding (bottom-three eigenvectors) rendering training as a moving particle cloud. Across seven experiments, diffusive regimes lack stable top-of-spectrum band structure, while sharp endogenous or externally driven reorganisations produce stable fingerprints: consistent with smooth or product latent geometries in BBS-adjacent cases, and with finite-cluster or Fourier-soliton structures otherwise. OMD thus reads the geometric regime of a representation rather than reporting a single intrinsic dimension.

AI.tldr: OMD is a diagnostic framework that analyzes neural network training dynamics using distance matrices on input representations, revealing spectral reorganizations missed by scalar metrics through random matrix theory and particle dynamics methods.
AI.motivation: Scalar loss functions miss important spectral reorganizations in neural network internal representations during training. There's a need for diagnostic tools that can probe high-dimensional representation dynamics to understand training processes better.
