AI.motivation: Current OOD detection methods that use loss-landscape curvature incur high computational costs and rely on unverified assumptions about curvature differences between in-distribution and OOD data. There's a need for systematic investigation of these curvature discrepancies and a more efficient detection approach.
AI.method: 1) Systematically investigates curvature discrepancy showing OOD inputs have larger Hessian curvature than ID data. 2) Proposes Fold detector using feature Hessian (instead of parameter-space) and partial feature normalization for better ID-OOD separability. 3) Introduces AutoFold for self-supervised tuning using ID logit masking to synthesize pseudo-OOD samples for automatic calibration without external data.
AI.result: Fold outperforms prior methods on OOD benchmarks, improving average AUROC by 1.63% and reducing FPR95 by 2.30%, while maintaining computational efficiency comparable to standard forward passes. Theoretical analysis and extensive ablations support the findings.
AI.conclusion: Fold provides a principled and practical solution for robust real-world deployment of OOD detection, offering improved performance with computational efficiency by leveraging feature-space curvature analysis and adaptive normalization.

title: DuoMem: Towards Capable On-Device Memory Agents via Dual-Space Distillation
summary: Large Language Model (LLM)-based agents can solve complex procedural tasks by interacting with environments over multiple turns, but this ability typically depends on large models, long contexts, and repeated inference calls. This makes advanced memory-augmented agents difficult to deploy on resource-constrained devices. We introduce DuoMem, a dual-space distillation framework that transfers procedural problem-solving ability from a large teacher model to compact student models. DuoMem distils in two complementary spaces: (1)context-space distillation, which replaces student-generated memories with higher-quality teacher-generated procedural memories prepended to the student's input, and (2)parameter-space distillation, which fine-tunes lightweight LoRA adapters on successful teacher trajectories. Evaluated on ALFWorld, a challenging embodied decision-making benchmark, DuoMem boosts a 4B-parameter model from 4.3% to 77.9% task success rate, closing most of the gap to a 72B teacher model (87.1%), while adding fewer than 10M trainable parameters and only a few megabytes of pre-computed teacher memories. Moreover, the DuoMem-enhanced 4B model completes tasks over 3x faster than the 72B teacher in wall-clock time, making it viable for real-time edge deployment, which would be challenging for the teacher.Extensive ablations across eight models spanning 2B-72B parameters reveal that both distillation axes contribute complementary

AI.tldr: DuoMem is a dual-space distillation framework that transfers procedural problem-solving abilities from large teacher LLMs to compact student models through context-space and parameter-space distillation, achieving near-teacher performance with much smaller models suitable for edge deployment.
AI.motivation: LLM-based agents for complex procedural tasks typically require large models, long contexts, and repeated inference calls, making them difficult to deploy on resource-constrained devices. There's a need to make advanced memory-augmented agents viable for edge deployment.
AI.method: Dual-space distillation framework with two complementary approaches: (1) Context-space distillation - replaces student-generated memories with higher-quality teacher-generated procedural memories prepended to student input; (2) Parameter-space distillation - fine-tunes lightweight LoRA adapters on successful teacher trajectories.
AI.result: On ALFWorld embodied decision-making benchmark, DuoMem boosts a 4B-parameter model from 4.3% to 77.9% task success rate, closing most of the gap to a 72B teacher model (87.1%). The enhanced 4B model completes tasks over 3x faster than the 72B teacher, with fewer than 10M trainable parameters and minimal memory overhead.
AI.conclusion: DuoMem effectively transfers procedural problem-solving abilities from large to compact models through complementary distillation approaches, making advanced memory-augmented agents viable for real-time edge deployment while maintaining high performance.

title: NeuReasoner: Theory-grounded Mapping of Reasoning Elicitation Boundaries
summary: A growing body of work suggests that the reasoning capabilities of large language models are largely latent in their base form, with post-training primarily amplifying rather than introducing them. However, this evidence comes mainly from mathematical and coding benchmarks, leaving the boundary conditions of that claim largely unexplored, namely which cognitive tasks can be recovered through elicitation and where that recovery fails. To investigate this, we introduce NeuReasoner, a theory-grounded elicitation instrument. At each step, an orchestrator pairs a Neuro Lens, inspired by functional specificity, with a Cognitive Lens, drawn from the Erotetic Theory of Reasoning, and integrates their outputs through internal modularization of a single model, without external tools. We evaluate NeuReasoner on CogBench, a suite of behavioral tasks from cognitive psychology, alongside standard mathematical and coding benchmarks, measuring both its improvement over vanilla inference and its ability to match a model's post-trained thinking mode. At sufficient scale, NeuReasoner matches or exceeds thinking-mode baselines on arithmetic reasoning, code generation, Bayesian reasoning, and reward learning; these gains persist against self-consistency and iterative-refinement baselines matched to NeuReasoner's per-decision call budget. Using NeuReasoner allows us to find clear boundaries: risk-taking and decision making under uncertainty remains hard to recover through elicitation alone, and model scale interacts with elicitation in both directions: widening its advantage on some cognitive signatures while erasing it on others. Overall, through NeuReasoner as a modular, interpretable, theory-grounded elicitation instrument, we empirically map where reasoning elicitation succeeds and fails, beyond the mathematical and coding benchmarks where prior claims have rested.

AI.tldr: NeuReasoner is a theory-grounded elicitation instrument that uses modular Neuro and Cognitive Lenses to unlock latent reasoning capabilities in LLMs. It matches or exceeds post-trained thinking modes on arithmetic, coding, Bayesian reasoning, and reward learning at sufficient scale, but fails on risk-taking and decision making under uncertainty.
AI.motivation: Prior evidence that LLMs' reasoning capabilities are latent and amplified by post-training comes mainly from mathematical and coding benchmarks, leaving the boundary conditions unexplored - specifically which cognitive tasks can be recovered through elicitation and where that recovery fails.
AI.method: NeuReasoner is a theory-grounded elicitation instrument where an orchestrator pairs a Neuro Lens (inspired by functional specificity) with a Cognitive Lens (drawn from Erotetic Theory of Reasoning), integrating their outputs through internal modularization of a single model without external tools.
AI.result: At sufficient scale, NeuReasoner matches or exceeds thinking-mode baselines on arithmetic reasoning, code generation, Bayesian reasoning, and reward learning. These gains persist against self-consistency and iterative-refinement baselines. However, risk-taking and decision making under uncertainty remain hard to recover through elicitation alone, and model scale interacts with elicitation in complex ways.
AI.conclusion: NeuReasoner serves as a modular, interpretable, theory-grounded elicitation instrument that empirically maps where reasoning elicitation succeeds and fails, extending beyond mathematical and coding benchmarks to provide clearer boundary conditions for claims about LLM reasoning capabilities.

title: Atompack: A Storage and Distribution Layer for Read-Heavy Atomistic ML Training Datasets
summary: Atomistic machine learning datasets are increasingly used for training: large immutable snapshots are read repeatedly, shuffled across epochs, staged across clusters' storage systems, and republished as reusable scientific artifacts. This workload differs from interactive scientific curation, where mutable records and ad hoc inspection are often more important than random indexed throughput. We present Atompack, an append-oriented storage format and distribution layer designed around a simple workload: training pipelines usually consume complete molecular records, while the order of records is randomized by the learning algorithm. Atompack appends records efficiently during dataset construction, then commits an immutable index and serves records through a memory-mapped read path optimized for training. We compare Atompack with HDF5, LMDB, and ASE baselines representing array stores, key-value records, serialized records, and object-oriented databases. The benchmarks measure sequential reads, shuffled reads, shared-filesystem behavior, write throughput, and artifact size. On a representative 64-atom workload, Atompack is 96x faster than ASE LMDB on shuffled training-style reads while producing artifacts about 79\% smaller. The results indicate that serving complete molecule records, rather than field chunks or reconstructed objects, improves shuffled training throughput while keeping artifacts compact enough for public distribution.

AI.tldr: Atompack is a specialized storage format optimized for atomistic machine learning training workloads, offering significantly faster shuffled reads and smaller artifact sizes compared to existing formats like HDF5 and LMDB.
AI.motivation: Traditional storage formats aren't optimized for machine learning training workloads where large, immutable datasets are read repeatedly with random shuffling. Existing formats (HDF5, LMDB, ASE) are designed for different use cases like interactive scientific curation, field-level access, or mutable records, but atomistic ML training has distinct requirements: complete molecular record consumption and record-order randomization.
AI.method: Developed Atompack, an append-oriented storage format with an immutable index. It efficiently appends records during dataset construction, then uses a memory-mapped read path optimized for training. Key design principles: serving complete molecule records rather than field chunks or reconstructed objects, with append-only writes and immutable indexes for efficient shuffled reads.
AI.result: Comprehensive benchmarks show Atompack outperforms HDF5, LMDB, and ASE baselines across multiple metrics. On 64-atom workloads, Atompack achieves 96x faster shuffled training-style reads than ASE LMDB while producing artifacts ~79% smaller. It excels in sequential reads, shuffled reads, shared-filesystem behavior, write throughput, and artifact compactness.
AI.conclusion: Atompack demonstrates that serving complete molecule records optimized for the specific pattern of ML training workloads (complete record consumption with random shuffling) provides substantial performance benefits in both throughput and storage efficiency, making it suitable for public distribution of reusable scientific datasets.

title: Stabilizing Extrapolation in Looped Transformers via Learned Stochastic Stopping
summary: Looped Transformers, which repeatedly apply a shared transformer block, are an architecturally natural fit for variable-length algorithmic tasks. Although they can exhibit strong length generalization beyond the length of training sequences, this behavior is brittle, yielding high out-of-distribution (OOD) variance, even across well-performing in-distribution solutions. We trace this variance to the spurious correlation in simple algorithmic tasks between sequence length and number of loops. Introducing stochasticity into the number of loops during training sharply reduces OOD variance and stabilizes predictions across inference-time loop counts. To improve upon heuristic randomization schemes, we further analyze RL-Halting as a learned stochastic schedule and find that it generally improves the accuracy-stability trade-off. Across binary addition, Dyck-1, Unique Set, and Copy, learned stochastic stopping often improves this trade-off but can also stabilize a suboptimal computation. Our work suggests that "when to stop" should be treated as a training-time design choice, not merely an inference-time computation-allocation rule.

AI.tldr: Looped Transformers for algorithmic tasks show brittle length generalization with high OOD variance; introducing stochastic training-time loop counts reduces variance and stabilizes predictions, with learned stochastic schedules (RL-Halting) offering better accuracy-stability trade-offs.
AI.motivation: Looped Transformers are architecturally suitable for variable-length algorithmic tasks and can show length generalization, but this generalization is brittle with high out-of-distribution variance even among well-performing in-distribution solutions.
AI.method: The paper addresses the brittleness by: 1) Tracing the variance to spurious correlation between sequence length and number of loops in simple algorithmic tasks, 2) Introducing stochasticity into the number of loops during training to reduce OOD variance, 3) Analyzing RL-Halting as a learned stochastic schedule to improve the accuracy-stability trade-off, and 4) Testing these approaches across multiple algorithmic tasks (binary addition, Dyck-1, Unique Set, Copy).
AI.result: Stochastic training-time loop counts sharply reduce OOD variance and stabilize predictions across inference-time loop counts. RL-Halting generally improves the accuracy-stability trade-off compared to heuristic randomization schemes, though learned stochastic stopping can sometimes stabilize suboptimal computations rather than optimal ones.
AI.conclusion: "When to stop" in looped architectures should be treated as a training-time design choice rather than merely an inference-time computation-allocation rule, as stochastic loop scheduling during training significantly improves generalization stability in algorithmic tasks.

title: T3R: Deeper Test-Time Adaptation for Graph Neural Networks via Gradient Rotation
summary: Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) deployed in real-world systems typically have fixed weights, often leading to degraded performance under distribution shifts. This issue can be mitigated by conventional fine-tuning, but in many real-world cases, collecting labeled data is expensive or infeasible. A potential approach is Test-Time Training (TTT), which adapts models' weights using unlabeled test data, yet it is typically limited to shallow updates that affect only a subset of model parameters. We propose T3R, leveraging multiple Rotograd matrices to improve task affinity between the target and auxiliary tasks, essential for effective test-time training. T3R further introduces a rotation technique that reorients self-supervised signals using these matrices to create surrogate gradients for the target task, allowing deeper adaptation across nearly the entire architecture. Empirically, T3R reduces MAE by 0.172 points over standard inference in regression datasets and achieves at least 9.37% relative improvement on cross-domain OGB classification benchmarks compared to models without adaptation. These results highlight the potential to develop an adaptation pipeline for graph-based systems, particularly in settings where conventional fine-tuning or retraining is infeasible.

AI.tldr: T3R is a test-time training method for Graph Neural Networks that uses multiple Rotograd matrices to improve adaptation to distribution shifts using only unlabeled test data, enabling deeper parameter updates compared to standard TTT approaches.
AI.motivation: GNNs deployed in real-world systems often degrade under distribution shifts, but conventional fine-tuning is impractical due to expensive labeled data collection. Test-Time Training offers adaptation with unlabeled data, but is limited to shallow updates of only a subset of parameters.
AI.method: T3R leverages multiple Rotograd matrices to improve task affinity between target and auxiliary tasks, and introduces a rotation technique that reorients self-supervised signals using these matrices to create surrogate gradients for the target task, enabling deeper adaptation across nearly the entire architecture.
AI.result: Empirically, T3R reduces MAE by 0.172 points over standard inference in regression datasets and achieves at least 9.37% relative improvement on cross-domain OGB classification benchmarks compared to models without adaptation.
AI.conclusion: T3R demonstrates potential for developing adaptation pipelines for graph-based systems, particularly in settings where conventional fine-tuning or retraining is infeasible, by enabling effective test-time training with deeper parameter adaptation.

title: Heads, Not Backbones: Output Heads Dominate Architectures on Fat-Tailed Returns
summary: In a deep forecasting pipeline for fat-tailed financial returns at short horizons, which matters more - the backbone architecture or the output head? We compare four modern backbones (TimesNet, DLinear, N-BEATS, iTransformer) under three output heads: a point head, a single-Gaussian density head, and a Gaussian mixture density head with K=4 components. On S and P 500 monthly log-returns (1871-2023) under anchored walk-forward validation, the three heads form a strict gradient: switching from point to Gaussian improves CRPS by about 1.3 percent; switching from Gaussian to mixture adds a further about 2.4 percent. Switching between backbones, in contrast, changes CRPS by less than 1.5 percent on the point-head row and on the backbone-mean axis; density-head backbone spread is larger (up to 5.1 percent on the h=1 Gaussian row, driven by N-BEATS) but the head gradient (3.7 percentage points) still dominates. The Model Confidence Set on squared errors does not exclude any of the 12 variants at the 5 percent level: the head separates them only on distributional metrics (CRPS, pinball, coverage), not on squared error. The mixture head incremental value over a single Gaussian is largest in the highest-volatility regimes (13.9 percent in 1970s stagflation at h=12), confirming the mixture captures tail risk beyond what a unimodal Gaussian can express. The picture is horizon-dependent: the head dominates at short horizons, but at long horizons (h >= 6) the backbone re-takes the lead - an h-split we document against classical baselines (section 5.1). We conclude that on fat-tailed returns at short horizons, the head dominates the backbone, and the mixture distribution adds genuine value over a single Gaussian during crisis periods when risk-management decisions actually matter.

AI.tldr: For fat-tailed financial returns at short horizons, output head (distributional modeling) matters more than backbone architecture, with Gaussian mixture heads providing significant gains during high-volatility regimes.
AI.motivation: To determine whether backbone architecture or output head is more important in forecasting fat-tailed financial returns at short horizons, and to evaluate the value of distributional heads (Gaussian mixtures) over point forecasts.
AI.method: Compared four modern backbones (TimesNet, DLinear, N-BEATS, iTransformer) under three output heads: point head, single-Gaussian density head, and Gaussian mixture density head (K=4). Used S&P 500 monthly log-returns (1871-2023) with anchored walk-forward validation and Model Confidence Set analysis.
AI.result: Output heads form a strict performance gradient: switching from point to Gaussian improves CRPS by ~1.3%; switching from Gaussian to mixture adds ~2.4%. Backbone differences are smaller (<1.5%). Mixture head adds most value in high-volatility regimes (13.9% in 1970s stagflation). Head dominates at short horizons, but backbone becomes more important at longer horizons (h≥6).
AI.conclusion: For fat-tailed returns at short horizons, output head design dominates backbone architecture, with Gaussian mixture heads providing genuine value for risk management during crisis periods. The choice between backbone and head importance is horizon-dependent.

title: Building Multi-Task Agentic LLMs via Two-Phase Distillation
summary: A key step toward artificial general intelligence is to train models that can perform multiple tasks. In this paper, we study how to build such models by first training separate RL experts for individual tasks and then consolidating them via distillation, as an alternative to directly training a single model on mixed tasks. We show that off-policy distillation degrades in multi-task settings due to the mode-covering nature of forward KL: aggregating data from multiple tasks introduces a large number of behavioral modes that can exceed the student's capacity, forcing it to average across behaviors and leading to degraded performance. In contrast, on-policy distillation is mode-seeking but requires strong initialization. Inspired by these observations, we propose a two-phase approach: off-policy distillation followed by on-policy refinement. Evaluation across conversational agents and text-based games confirms that this two-phase approach matches single-task RL expert performance for each individual task, whereas off-policy or on-policy distillation alone fails to match this performance.

AI.tldr: A two-phase distillation approach combining off-policy and on-policy methods enables multi-task RL models to match single-task expert performance, overcoming limitations of individual distillation methods.
AI.motivation: The paper addresses the challenge of building artificial general intelligence models that can perform multiple tasks. Current approaches either directly train single models on mixed tasks or use distillation methods, but these have limitations: off-policy distillation suffers from mode-covering issues in multi-task settings, while on-policy distillation requires strong initialization.
AI.method: The proposed method involves first training separate RL experts for individual tasks, then using a two-phase distillation approach: (1) off-policy distillation followed by (2) on-policy refinement. This combines the benefits of both methods while mitigating their individual weaknesses.
AI.result: Evaluation across conversational agents and text-based games shows that the two-phase approach successfully matches single-task RL expert performance for each individual task. In contrast, off-policy or on-policy distillation alone fails to achieve this level of performance.
AI.conclusion: The proposed two-phase distillation approach effectively consolidates multiple task-specific RL experts into a single multi-task model that preserves expert-level performance for each task, overcoming the mode-covering limitations of off-policy distillation and the initialization requirements of on-policy distillation.

title: From Failure Taxonomy to Intervention: A Diagnostic Methodology for Industry-Scale AVLM in Video and Live-Streaming Platform Moderation
summary: Industry-scale video and live-streaming moderation imposes requirements that are difficult to satisfy with generic pretrained public models or external APIs, including adaptation to platform-specific data distributions, policy-specific objectives, and product-level safety constraints. As a result, platforms must undertake internal model development, naturally turning to shared public research for guidance. However, existing multimodal foundation-model studies primarily report architectures, training recipes, data scaling strategies, and benchmark results, but provide less systematic guidance on how failures should be localized and translated into targeted model-development interventions. Interventions are essential because deployment failures are rarely self-explanatory. Similar failures can originate from different causes. Without targeted interventions, improvement reduces to heuristic trial-and-error, where benchmark improvements are weakly attributable, and failures are difficult to trace to their underlying causes. To address this gap, we present a diagnostic methodology for industry-scale Audio-Visual-Language Models AVLM development. The methodology maps model failures into a taxonomy of observable failure signatures and links each class of failure to an intervention space. We instantiate this methodology across the development and alignment lifecycle of an AVLM foundation model for a large-scale video and live-streaming platform. The resulting system supports over 100 regions and is designed for noisy, ambiguous, and highly diverse content drawn from global platform traffic.

AI.tldr: Researchers developed a diagnostic methodology for industry-scale Audio-Visual-Language Models that maps failures to observable signatures and links them to targeted interventions, applied to a large-scale video streaming platform.
AI.motivation: Industry-scale video moderation requires adaptation to platform-specific data, policies, and safety constraints, but existing multimodal foundation model research lacks systematic guidance on localizing failures and implementing targeted interventions, leading to heuristic trial-and-error development.
AI.method: A diagnostic methodology that maps model failures into a taxonomy of observable failure signatures and links each class of failure to an intervention space, applied across the development and alignment lifecycle of an AVLM foundation model.
AI.result: The methodology was successfully instantiated for an AVLM foundation model on a large-scale video and live-streaming platform, resulting in a system supporting over 100 regions designed for noisy, ambiguous, and diverse global content.
AI.conclusion: The diagnostic methodology addresses the critical gap in systematic failure analysis and targeted intervention for industry-scale multimodal model development, enabling more efficient and traceable model improvement beyond benchmark-focused approaches.

title: Data-Driven Energy-Based Learning via Gibbs Measures on Hierarchical Structures
summary:
We introduce a data-driven probabilistic framework for learning systems based on Gibbs measures on hierarchical structures. Unlike standard empirical risk minimization, where a dataset is used to identify a single optimal parameter, our approach transforms the empirical loss function into an interaction potential defining an energy-based model. The resulting Gibbs distribution describes a family of equilibrium learning states generated by the data.
  We formulate the consistency conditions of the associated finite-volume distributions and derive nonlinear integral fixed-point equations whose solutions characterize the admissible learning states. These equations provide a rigorous connection between empirical loss landscapes and probabilistic inference on trees. For translation-invariant solutions, the problem reduces to the analysis of positive compact operators induced by data-dependent kernels, allowing us to establish existence and uniqueness conditions in the one-dimensional setting.
  Furthermore, we show that hierarchical learning systems may exhibit phase-transition phenomena: for certain empirical kernels on Cayley trees, multiple Gibbs measures emerge beyond a critical inverse temperature, corresponding to distinct equilibrium prediction regimes. Numerical experiments with non-separable kernels illustrate the appearance of multiple solution branches and demonstrate the coexistence of several data-induced learning states.
  Our results provide a new perspective on energy-based learning, where data do not merely determine an optimal model through minimization but define an entire probabilistic landscape of possible inference states.

AI.tldr: A probabilistic framework for learning systems using Gibbs measures on hierarchical structures, transforming empirical loss into interaction potentials to describe families of equilibrium learning states, with phase transitions enabling multiple prediction regimes.
AI.motivation: To move beyond standard empirical risk minimization (which identifies a single optimal parameter) and provide a richer probabilistic perspective where data define an entire landscape of possible inference states, capturing the complexity of learning as an equilibrium process.
AI.method: Transforms empirical loss functions into interaction potentials defining energy-based models, uses Gibbs measures on hierarchical structures, formulates consistency conditions for finite-volume distributions, derives nonlinear integral fixed-point equations, analyzes translation-invariant solutions via data-dependent kernels, and studies phase transitions on Cayley trees.
AI.result: Establishes existence and uniqueness conditions for learning states in one-dimensional setting, demonstrates hierarchical learning systems exhibit phase-transition phenomena with multiple Gibbs measures emerging beyond critical inverse temperature, and shows coexistence of multiple data-induced learning states through numerical experiments with non-separable kernels.
AI.conclusion: Provides a new perspective on energy-based learning where data don't just determine a single optimal model through minimization but define an entire probabilistic landscape of possible inference states, revealing complex equilibrium behavior in learning systems with phase transitions enabling multiple prediction regimes.

title: Neural Subspace Reallocation: Continual Learning as Retrieval-Based Subspace Memory Management
summary: We introduce Neural Subspace Reallocation (NSR), which reframes continual learning as memory management over parameter subspaces. Instead of treating Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) modules as disposable per-task adapters, NSR manages them as compressible, retrievable memory units on a frozen backbone through a recurring cycle: (1) compress learned LoRAs via SVD, (2) reserve them in a TaskKnowledgeBank, (3) recall related past LoRAs by embedding similarity to warm-start new or returning tasks, and (4) reallocate the active subspace accordingly, with distillation protecting prior tasks. We prove that in cyclic environments any memoryless allocation policy incurs cumulative regret Omega(T(M-1)Delta_switch) relative to a history-aware policy backed by the Bank (Theorem 1). Empirically, on Split-CIFAR-100 the Bank reduces cyclic recovery time by 10x, exactly as predicted, and on the heterogeneous 5-Datasets benchmark NSR achieves the highest accuracy and the least forgetting, about 9x closer to zero backward transfer than the memoryless heuristics. Crucially, we run a controlled study that isolates which component matters: holding the Bank fixed and varying only the allocation rule, we find that a simple similarity-based retrieval rule matches or beats a learned reinforcement-learning controller (recovering recurring tasks in 0 vs 1.8 steps and reaching equal accuracy). Our central, honest finding is therefore that the memory mechanism -- compression and similarity retrieval -- rather than a learned allocation policy, drives continual-learning performance under fixed capacity. A memory-budget analysis confirms the compressed Bank stays small -- 0.29 MB of parameter memory per task -- so a top-K retention cap bounds the total footprint while preserving fast recovery for retained tasks.

AI.tldr: NSR reframes continual learning as memory management over parameter subspaces, using compressed LoRA modules as retrievable memory units that dramatically improve task recovery and reduce forgetting through similarity-based retrieval rather than learned allocation policies.
AI.motivation: Current continual learning approaches treat LoRA modules as disposable per-task adapters, leading to inefficient memory usage and poor task recovery in cyclic environments. There's a need for more efficient memory management that can leverage past knowledge to warm-start new or returning tasks.
AI.method: NSR implements a recurring cycle: (1) compress learned LoRAs via SVD, (2) store them in a TaskKnowledgeBank, (3) recall related past LoRAs by embedding similarity to warm-start tasks, and (4) reallocate active subspace with distillation protecting prior tasks. Uses similarity-based retrieval rather than learned allocation policies.
AI.result: On Split-CIFAR-100: reduces cyclic recovery time by 10x; on 5-Datasets benchmark: achieves highest accuracy and least forgetting (~9x closer to zero backward transfer than memoryless heuristics. Bank requires only 0.29 MB per task. Similarity-based retrieval matches/beats learned RL controllers.
AI.conclusion: The memory mechanism (compression and similarity retrieval) drives continual-learning performance more than learned allocation policies. NSR provides an efficient, scalable approach to continual learning with minimal memory footprint while enabling fast recovery for retained tasks.

title: Predictive Objectives Discard Exogenous Control-Relevant Features: A Controlled Mechanistic Study
comment: 15 pages 3 tables 5 figures for associated github repo see https://github.com/bushesarebetter/jepa_research_project
summary: Joint-embedding predictive (JEPA-style) objectives learn representations by predicting future latents. In doing so they can discard features that are exogenous (uncontrollable by the agent) yet control-relevant, even when those features are trivially encodable. This occurs because the objective optimizes temporal predictability rather than control-relevance. We isolate this failure mode in a controlled 2x2 experimental design that varies feature controllability and relevance independently, using a predictability knob that decouples a feature's temporal predictability from its control-relevance. Comparing six objectives: reconstruction, JEPA, action-conditioned JEPA, controllability-based JEPA, inverse dynamics under a random policy, and reward-grounded JEPA, we observe that all evaluated reward-free predictive objectives leave the exogenous control-relevant feature near chance accuracy, while a reward-grounded variant retains it selectively. The remedy is label-efficient and robust: as little as 2% of reward-labeled transitions recovers the feature, the effect holds across two environments with different surface forms, and it persists across latent dimensions from 16 to 1024. Comparing the learned latent geometry against bisimulation theory's prediction, the JEPA latent realizes only a small fraction of the class separation a supervised reference attains.

AI.tldr: JEPA-style representation learning objectives can fail to capture exogenous yet control-relevant features because they optimize for predictability rather than control-relevance, but reward-grounded variants can selectively recover these features efficiently.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to identify a failure mode in joint-embedding predictive (JEPA-style) objectives where they discard exogenous (uncontrollable by the agent) yet control-relevant features, even when those features are trivially encodable. This happens because JEPA optimizes for temporal predictability rather than control-relevance.
AI.method: The authors use a controlled 2x2 experimental design varying feature controllability and relevance independently, with a "predictability knob" that decouples a feature's temporal predictability from its control-relevance. They compare six objectives: reconstruction, JEPA, action-conditioned JEPA, controllability-based JEPA, inverse dynamics under a random policy, and reward-grounded JEPA. They test across environments with different surface forms and latent dimensions from 16 to 1024.
AI.result: All reward-free predictive objectives leave the exogenous control-relevant feature near chance accuracy, while reward-grounded JEPA retains it selectively. The remedy is label-efficient and robust: as little as 2% of reward-labeled transitions recovers the feature, and the effect holds across environments and persists across latent dimensions. The JEPA latent realizes only a small fraction of the class separation compared to what bisimulation theory predicts and what a supervised reference attains.
AI.conclusion: JEPA-style objectives have a fundamental limitation in capturing exogenous control-relevant features due to their focus on predictability rather than control-relevance. However, incorporating reward information (even sparsely) can remedy this issue efficiently. This suggests that pure predictability objectives may be insufficient for learning representations that capture all control-relevant aspects of the environment.

title: Online Data Selection for Instruction Tuning via Gaussian Processes
summary: With Large Language Model (LLM) pre-training and fine-tuning shifting its focus from data volume to data quality, quality data selection has emerged as a critical research topic. Existing online data selection methods for LLM training are typically "batch-constrained", limiting optimization to local utility within random batches. To overcome this, we propose GAIA (Global Adaptive Instruction tuning via GAussian processes), a framework that formulates data valuation as a global estimation process. GAIA employs Gaussian Process regression to model continuous utility manifolds across the semantic space, utilizing an adaptive strategy fusion mechanism to dynamically prioritize high-utility samples. By casting the strategy-posterior update as an instance of the classical fixed-share Hedge framework for tracking the best expert, we inherit a dynamic-regret guarantee that characterizes GAIA's robustness under non-stationary quality scores during training. Empirical evaluations on three datasets demonstrate that GAIA significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines like \greats, establishing our method as a scalable and robust solution for efficient instruction tuning.

AI.tldr: GAIA is a global data selection framework for LLM instruction tuning that uses Gaussian Process regression to model utility across semantic space, outperforming existing batch-constrained methods.
AI.motivation: With LLM training shifting focus from data volume to data quality, existing online data selection methods are limited by being "batch-constrained" - they only optimize local utility within random batches rather than global optimization across the entire data space.
AI.method: GAIA formulates data valuation as a global estimation process using Gaussian Process regression to model continuous utility manifolds across semantic space. It employs adaptive strategy fusion to dynamically prioritize high-utility samples and uses a fixed-share Hedge framework to update strategy posteriors.
AI.result: GAIA significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines like GREAT on three datasets, establishing itself as a scalable and robust solution for efficient instruction tuning with dynamic-regret guarantees.
AI.conclusion: GAIA provides a novel framework for global data selection in LLM training that overcomes the limitations of batch-constrained methods, offering both theoretical guarantees and empirical effectiveness for quality-focused instruction tuning.

title: Query-Aware Spreading Activation for Multi-Hop Retrieval over Knowledge Graphs
comment: Accepted for publication in Cybernetics and Systems Analysis (Springer). Not yet published
summary: Retrieval-augmented generation built on knowledge graphs (Graph RAG) outperforms flat passage retrieval on multi-hop question answering by leveraging graph structure. In most existing systems, however, the question only sets the seed nodes; the subsequent traversal becomes "query-blind", depending solely on the graph structure. The exception is QAFD-RAG, which implements query-aware traversal via a flow-diffusion solver with combined edge re-weighting. This architecture requires loading the full graph into Python memory and an iterative solver with a variable number of iterations complicating integration with the graph database. We propose a spreading-activation method that achieves the same query-aware traversal with a single per-step semantic gate: the step weight is the cosine similarity between the candidate entity's description and the question, and the number of iterations is fixed. The whole retrieval procedure - seed mapping, propagation, top-K selection and context assembly - is expressed as a single Cypher query executed in one round-trip to Neo4j; the graph never leaves the database. On MuSiQue our method matches QAFD-RAG by exact match (32.80 vs 33.50) and outperforms the strongest purely-structural baseline in our comparison, HippoRAG, by 5.3 EM and 3.4 F1; on 2WikiMultiHopQA HippoRAG and QAFD-RAG retain an advantage due to their phrase-node architectures. An ablation with the gate disabled confirms that the gate is the source of a simultaneous F1 gain of 3.6 to 7.4 points and a retrieval-latency reduction by a factor of 1.5 to 4.9.

AI.tldr: A graph RAG method using spreading activation with query-aware semantic gating that outperforms structural baselines and matches advanced methods while enabling single-query execution in Neo4j.
AI.motivation: Existing graph RAG systems become "query-blind" after seed node selection, with traversal depending only on graph structure. QAFD-RAG addresses this with query-aware traversal but requires full graph loading into Python memory and complex iterative solvers, complicating database integration.
AI.method: Proposes a spreading-activation method with query-aware traversal using a single semantic gate per step: cosine similarity between candidate entity's description and the question. Uses fixed iterations and expresses entire retrieval as a single Cypher query executed in one round-trip to Neo4j.
AI.result: Matches QAFD-RAG performance on MuSiQue (32.80 vs 33.50 EM) and outperforms HippoRAG by 5.3 EM and 3.4 F1. On 2WikiMultiHopQA, HippoRAG and QAFD-RAG retain advantage due to phrase-node architectures. Ablation shows gate contributes 3.6-7.4 F1 gain and 1.5-4.9× latency reduction.
AI.conclusion: The spreading-activation approach achieves effective query-aware traversal with simpler architecture, better database integration, and competitive performance while providing significant efficiency gains through semantic gating.

title: Robust Strategic Classification under Decision-Dependent Cost Uncertainty
comment: 29 pages, 7 figures, accepted for publication at ICML 2026
summary: Humans facing algorithmic decision systems have been found to ``game'' them by altering their input data (at a cost to them) in order to favorably change the algorithmic outcomes they receive (at a cost to the algorithm). The growing literature on strategic classification seeks to develop robust machine learning algorithms that account for, and reduce, unwanted strategic behavior. A limitation of these existing works is that they assume the cost of strategic behavior to be fixed and independent of the classifier's decision. In practice, however, manipulation costs evolve and depend on past algorithmic decisions: today's decisions influence tomorrow's costs. This paper proposes and analyzes a two-stage robust optimization framework with a decision-dependent uncertainty set to capture such dependencies. We highlight that awareness of policy-dependent costs not only reduces uncertainty, but also better curtails gaming of the algorithmic system over time.

AI.tldr: Two-stage robust optimization framework for strategic classification that accounts for policy-dependent manipulation costs, where today's decisions affect tomorrow's gaming costs.
AI.motivation: Existing strategic classification literature assumes fixed, independent costs for manipulation behavior, but in practice, manipulation costs evolve and depend on past algorithmic decisions - today's decisions influence tomorrow's costs.
AI.method: Proposes a two-stage robust optimization framework with a decision-dependent uncertainty set to capture the dependencies between algorithmic decisions and manipulation costs over time.
AI.result: Awareness of policy-dependent costs not only reduces uncertainty but also better curtails gaming of the algorithmic system over time compared to existing approaches.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework addresses a key limitation in strategic classification by modeling the dynamic relationship between algorithmic decisions and manipulation costs, leading to more robust machine learning systems against strategic behavior.

title: Federated Learning with Energy-Based Structured Probabilistic Inference
comment: Accepted to the Structured Probabilistic Inference Generative Modeling workshop at ICML 2026
summary: Federated learning typically aggregates client updates using fixed or heuristic weighting rules, which can be suboptimal when clients have heterogeneous data and varying contributions to the global model. We propose a framework that refines client aggregation weights using Conditional Random Fields (CRFs). Our method defines unary potentials for individual clients and pairwise potentials for all client pairs, allowing the server to model both client-specific reliability and interactions between clients. The resulting CRF inference produces aggregation weights that enable better convergence of the global training objective. Experiments show that, under non-IID heterogeneity, our approach consistently improves performance over well-established federated learning baselines.

AI.tldr: CRF-FL: A federated learning framework that uses Conditional Random Fields to dynamically optimize client aggregation weights based on client reliability and interactions, improving performance in non-IID heterogeneous settings.
AI.motivation: Traditional federated learning uses fixed or heuristic aggregation rules that don't account for client heterogeneity, leading to suboptimal performance when clients have varying data distributions and contributions to the global model.
AI.method: The framework applies Conditional Random Fields (CRFs) to refine client aggregation weights. It defines unary potentials for individual client reliability and pairwise potentials for client-client interactions, then performs CRF inference to compute optimal aggregation weights for the global model update.
AI.result: Experiments demonstrate consistent performance improvements over established federated learning baselines under non-IID data heterogeneity conditions.
AI.conclusion: Using CRFs to model client reliability and interactions provides a principled approach for adaptive client aggregation in federated learning, leading to better convergence and improved model performance in heterogeneous settings.

title: Beyond Drug Discovery: The Nanotechnology Molecular Optimization (NMO) Benchmark
summary: Generative molecular design is shaped by simple proxy benchmarks for drug-like properties and models pretrained on large pharmaceutical datasets. This combination yields strong benchmark metrics but limits transferability to domains structurally distinct from drug discovery. To overcome this limitation and drive discovery toward real, scientifically grounded targets, we introduce the Nanotechnology Molecular Optimization (NMO) Benchmark, which bridges machine learning (ML) and quantum materials science. NMO acts simultaneously as a rigorous testbed for the ML community and a discovery engine for nanotechnology research. The suite replaces proxy oracles with quantum simulations and introduces strict protocols that prioritize scientific utility over leaderboard-oriented overfitting. The physics-based NMO tasks impose hard structural constraints and rugged fitness landscapes, posing fundamentally new requirements on generative models. Notably, advanced molecular optimization methods underperform much simpler approaches on the NMO tasks. We develop a new baseline method identifying the critical components to solve the NMO tasks, including a novel representation for modeling structural constraints and a domain-agnostic pretraining strategy to eliminate pharmaceutical dataset bias. Our results surpass state-of-the-art physical properties and reveal previously unknown structural motifs, offering new insights for the nanotechnology community and demonstrating that ML can drive genuine scientific discovery.

AI.tldr: The NMO Benchmark bridges ML and quantum materials science by replacing drug-like proxy benchmarks with quantum simulations, enabling genuine scientific discovery in nanotechnology while challenging existing generative models that struggle with structural constraints.
AI.motivation: Current generative molecular design relies on drug-like property benchmarks and pharma dataset pretraining, which limits transferability to structurally distinct domains like nanotechnology. There's a need for scientifically-grounded benchmarks that drive real discovery rather than leaderboard optimization.
AI.method: Introduced Nanotechnology Molecular Optimization (NMO) Benchmark using quantum simulations instead of proxy oracles with strict protocols. Developed baseline method with novel representation for structural constraints and domain-agnostic pretraining to eliminate pharmaceutical dataset bias.
AI.result: Advanced molecular optimization methods underperform simpler approaches on NMO tasks due to hard structural constraints and rugged fitness landscapes. The new baseline method surpasses state-of-the-art physical properties and reveals previously unknown structural motifs.
AI.conclusion: NMO successfully bridges ML and materials science, demonstrating ML can drive genuine scientific discovery in nanotechnology while highlighting limitations of current drug-focused approaches. It provides a rigorous testbed that prioritizes scientific utility over benchmark overfitting.

title: Characterizing Optimizer-Dependent Training Dynamics Through Hessian Eigenvector Displacement and Localization
comment: Accepted as a poster at High-dimensional Learning Dynamics (HiLD), ICML 2026. OpenReview: https://openreview.net/forum?id=SabYcw5Nh6
summary: Hessian spectral properties are a standard tool in analysing neural-network training, with eigenvalues linked to sharpness, generalization, and optimization dynamics. Eigenvalues quantify curvature magnitude, while eigenvectors identify which parameters generate that curvature. In this work, we study how the leading Hessian eigenvectors evolve during training and how they affect the learning trajectories. We track the training dynamics of multilayer perceptrons on a classification problem and measure eigenvector dynamics through two complementary statistics: (i) displacement over time, inspired by analyses of glassy systems, and (ii) localization via the inverse participation ratio. The metrics are compared against a random null model of the Hessian induced by the architecture. Our results reveal clear optimizer-dependent behaviour. SGD leads to progressively more stable leading curvature directions, while Adam exhibits substantially stronger reorganization of eigenvectors throughout training. We also observe a localization phenomenon under Adam, where a small subset of parameters contributes disproportionately to the leading curvature directions. These results suggest that Hessian eigenvector dynamics capture key differences in optimizer behaviour and the resulting training trajectories.

AI.tldr: Hessian eigenvector dynamics reveal optimizer-dependent training behaviors: SGD leads to stable curvature directions while Adam shows strong eigenvector reorganization and parameter localization.
AI.motivation: Hessian eigenvalues are standard for analyzing neural network training (sharpness, generalization, optimization), but eigenvectors identify which parameters generate curvature. The paper aims to understand how leading Hessian eigenvectors evolve during training and affect learning trajectories, comparing optimizer behaviors.
AI.method: Track training dynamics of multilayer perceptrons on classification, measure eigenvector dynamics through two statistics: (1) displacement over time (inspired by glassy systems analysis), and (2) localization via inverse participation ratio. Compare metrics against a random null model of Hessian induced by the architecture.
AI.result: Clear optimizer-dependent behavior: SGD leads to progressively more stable leading curvature directions; Adam exhibits substantially stronger reorganization of eigenvectors throughout training. Adam shows localization phenomenon where small subset of parameters contributes disproportionately to leading curvature directions.
AI.conclusion: Hessian eigenvector dynamics capture key differences in optimizer behavior and resulting training trajectories, providing insights beyond eigenvalue analysis alone.

title: B3O: Scalable Boltzmann Batch Bayesian Optimization
summary: Modern engineering workflows increasingly rely on massive parallel simulation, driving the need for scalable, large-batch Bayesian Optimization (BO). Existing batch BO methods, however, incur large computational cost or rely on approximations that erode batch diversity. We propose B3O (Boltzmann Batch Bayesian Optimization), a framework that reframes batch generation as a pure sampling problem: drawing samples directly from the Boltzmann distribution defined by the acquisition function avoids the bottlenecks of existing large-batch methods. Theoretically, we prove that queries sampled from this distribution incur only negligible additional regret. Empirically, B3O outperforms existing batch BO methods on standard synthetic benchmarks and adapts robustly across complex applied tasks, including multi-objective electrode design and mixed-variable race car configuration.

AI.tldr: B3O is a novel Bayesian Optimization framework that generates large batches by sampling directly from the Boltzmann distribution of the acquisition function, enabling scalable parallel optimization without sacrificing batch diversity.
AI.motivation: Modern engineering workflows require massive parallel simulation, creating a need for scalable large-batch Bayesian Optimization. Existing batch BO methods have limitations: they either incur large computational costs or rely on approximations that reduce batch diversity, making them unsuitable for modern parallel computing environments.
AI.method: B3O (Boltzmann Batch Bayesian Optimization) reframes batch generation as a pure sampling problem. Instead of using optimization or approximations, it draws samples directly from the Boltzmann distribution defined by the acquisition function. This approach avoids computational bottlenecks and preserves batch diversity while enabling large-batch generation.
AI.result: Theoretically, the authors prove that queries sampled from the Boltzmann distribution incur only negligible additional regret. Empirically, B3O outperforms existing batch BO methods on standard synthetic benchmarks and demonstrates robust adaptation across complex applied tasks, including multi-objective electrode design and mixed-variable race car configuration.
AI.conclusion: B3O provides an efficient, scalable solution for large-batch Bayesian Optimization that addresses the computational and diversity limitations of existing methods, making it well-suited for modern parallel simulation workflows in engineering applications.

title: Curvature-Guided Sheaf Diffusion for Unsupervised Community Detection on Heterophilic Graphs
summary: Detecting communities in heterophilic graphs -- where connected nodes often belong to different classes -- is hard for unsupervised methods: classical modularity and spectral methods are feature agnostic, while deep graph-clustering methods rely on contrastive or generative machinery that is opaque. We propose Curvature-Guided Sheaf Diffusion (CGSD), a fully unsupervised community-detection algorithm that uses the discrete Forman--Ricci curvature of each edge as its single topological signal, propagated through every stage of an end-to-end pipeline. CGSD makes three concrete contributions: (i)~a curvature-gated sheaf-diffusion encoder that gates edge messages by $σ(κ_e)$ and is trained from three label-free structural losses (modularity, anti-collapse, curvature-weighted reconstruction); (ii)~a curvature-aware spectral clusterer (CSpec) that re-weights the $k$-NN affinity of the embedding by $σ(ακ_{e^*})$ before Ng--Jordan--Weiss; and (iii)~a unified label-free evaluation against nine truly-unsupervised baselines. On five heterophilic benchmarks (Cora, Cornell, Texas, Wisconsin, Chameleon), CGSD wins outright on Wisconsin and Chameleon and is competitive on the remaining three against nine unsupervised baselines. The gain over the strongest baseline is driven by the clusterer, not the encoder: on the same embedding, CSpec improves mean NMI from $0.091$ with $K$-Means to $0.107$ ($+15\%$, paired $t$-test $p=0.008$). The mechanism is interpretable: intra-community and inter-community curvature distributions are visibly separated. Code is open-sourced at https://github.com/woodywff/cgsd.

AI.tldr: CGSD is an unsupervised community detection method for heterophilic graphs that uses edge curvature as topological signal, combining curvature-gated sheaf diffusion encoding with curvature-aware spectral clustering.
AI.motivation: Community detection in heterophilic graphs (where connected nodes often belong to different classes) is challenging for existing unsupervised methods - classical methods ignore features, while deep clustering methods are opaque and complex.
AI.method: Uses discrete Forman-Ricci curvature of edges as topological signal. Combines: (1) curvature-gated sheaf-diffusion encoder with three label-free structural losses, (2) curvature-aware spectral clusterer that re-weights k-NN affinity by curvature before clustering, and (3) unified label-free evaluation framework.
AI.result: Outperforms nine unsupervised baselines on five heterophilic benchmarks, winning outright on Wisconsin and Chameleon datasets. Curvature-aware clusterer improves mean NMI by 15% over K-Means on same embeddings. Shows interpretable separation between intra- and inter-community curvature distributions.
AI.conclusion: CGSD provides an effective, interpretable unsupervised approach for heterophilic community detection using topological curvature signals, with significant improvements over existing methods and interpretable mechanisms.

title: KnowsTFM: Knowledge-Informed Fine-Tuning of Small Tabular Foundation Models
summary: Tabular foundation models have advanced deep learning for tabular data by delivering strong default performance across many small and medium tasks. Yet in niche domains, where data is scarce, high-dimensional, and shifted from the pretraining distribution, they may still fail to outperform carefully designed domain-specific methods. Many such domains also provide curated relational knowledge in the form of knowledge graphs and knowledge banks, but how to use this knowledge to improve and steer \textit{small} specialist tabular foundation models remains unclear. We address this problem through \textbf{Know}ledge-informed fine-tuning of \textbf{s}mall \textbf{T}abular \textbf{F}oundation \textbf{M}odels (\modelname). Specifically, we study nanoscale TabPFN- and TabICL-style variants, pretrained under controlled synthetic prior families and adapted using two complementary mechanisms: structural attention priors derived from knowledge graphs and parameter-efficient low-rank updates. We show that injecting domain-specific structural knowledge during fine-tuning yields meaningful gains over vanilla variants in specialist settings, whereas gains on general-domain tasks are marginal. We further observe that continual fine-tuning of frontier models can trigger collapse of pretrained knowledge and mechanisms.

AI.tldr: Know-TFM introduces a knowledge-informed fine-tuning approach for small tabular foundation models, using structural attention priors from knowledge graphs and parameter-efficient updates to improve performance in niche domains with scarce, shifted data.
AI.motivation: Tabular foundation models often fail to outperform domain-specific methods in niche domains where data is scarce, high-dimensional, and distributionally shifted from pretraining data, despite the availability of curated relational knowledge in knowledge graphs.
AI.method: Uses knowledge-informed fine-tuning of small tabular foundation models (TabPFN- and TabICL-style variants) with two complementary mechanisms: 1) structural attention priors derived from knowledge graphs, and 2) parameter-efficient low-rank updates.
AI.result: Injecting domain-specific structural knowledge during fine-tuning yields meaningful gains over vanilla variants in specialist settings, while gains on general-domain tasks are marginal. Also observes that continual fine-tuning of frontier models can trigger collapse of pretrained knowledge and mechanisms.
AI.conclusion: Knowledge-informed fine-tuning through structural attention priors and parameter-efficient updates can effectively improve small tabular foundation models in specialist domains, addressing the limitations of vanilla models when dealing with scarce, shifted data in niche applications.

title: When Is a Draft Accepted? A Theory of Acceptance in Speculative Decoding
summary: Speculative decoding accelerates language model inference by using a fast drafter to propose candidate tokens that are then verified by a larger target model. Existing theory largely studies the stochastic, distribution-preserving setting, where the goal is to exactly sample from the target distribution. In contrast, many practical systems use greedy decoding, relaxed acceptance rules, or tree-based candidate sets, where success is governed by local ranking and threshold events rather than exact distributional equality. We develop a theory for these regimes. We identify that many common acceptance criteria have rejection regions that can be characterized as lower level sets of the target distribution. For these, we characterize the exact KL divergence required for rejection yielding exact certificates and sharp margin-based bounds for strict greedy decoding, additive and multiplicative relaxed acceptance, top-(m) relaxed criteria, and entropy-thresholded acceptance. We then extend the framework to greedy tree decoding, deriving exact and margin-only certificates for when the target greedy token remains covered by the drafter's top-(m) candidates. Finally, we evaluate the resulting certificates on Qwen3 models, showing that relaxed and tree-based criteria substantially enlarge the region of certified acceptance, especially on decoding steps with low target model distribution margin. These results complement existing distribution-preserving analyses of speculative decoding by characterizing the deterministic local acceptance events common in practical inference systems.

AI.tldr: A theoretical analysis of speculative decoding acceptance criteria, focusing on practical greedy decoding regimes rather than distribution-preserving stochastic sampling.
AI.motivation: Existing theory for speculative decoding focuses on stochastic, distribution-preserving settings, but practical systems often use greedy decoding, relaxed acceptance rules, or tree-based candidate sets where success depends on local ranking/threshold events rather than exact distributional equality.
AI.method: Develop theory for practical regimes by characterizing acceptance criteria rejection regions as lower level sets of target distributions, derive exact KL divergence requirements for rejection, and provide certificates for various criteria (strict greedy, additive/multiplicative relaxed, top-m, entropy-thresholded). Extend to greedy tree decoding with certificates for when target greedy token remains in drafter's top-m candidates.
AI.result: Derived exact and margin-based bounds for various acceptance criteria, showing that relaxed and tree-based criteria substantially enlarge certified acceptance regions, especially on decoding steps with low target model distribution margin. Validated on Qwen3 models.
AI.conclusion: The analysis complements existing distribution-preserving analyses by characterizing deterministic local acceptance events common in practical inference systems, providing theoretical foundations for practical speculative decoding implementations.

title: Towards Continual Motion-Language Agents: LoRA Variants for Incremental Motion Understanding and Generation
comment: 16 pages, 1 figure, Accepted at the Conference on Lifelong Learning Agents (CoLLAs) 2026
summary: Motion-language agents must possess the bidirectional capability to both understand human movement (motion-to-text, M2T) and generate it from natural language (text-to-motion, T2M). While foundational models have achieved strong performance in static settings, autonomous agents operating in dynamic environments must continuously incorporate new motion concepts -- such as novel athletic styles or specialized gestures -- without catastrophic forgetting of previously acquired skills. We investigate the stability-plasticity trade-off in bidirectional motion-language learning under sequential task exposure. Building on a frozen large language model backbone, we introduce low-rank adaptation (LoRA) variants designed to mitigate inter-task interference. We specifically propose mixture-of-experts architectures that utilize an autoencoder-based router to select task-specific experts at inference time, so that no task-label is needed. To evaluate these methods, we establish a reproducible five-task benchmark derived from HumanML3D through semantic clustering of motion descriptions. Our experimental results demonstrate near-zero forgetting across both M2T and T2M directions while maintaining high generation and captioning quality. Furthermore, we show that hard expert selection via routing significantly outperforms soft expert blending in quality metrics, indicating that preserving expert isolation is critical for maintaining performance in our continual learning setting. Finally, we observe that a divergence between token-level accuracy and downstream generation quality may occur, highlighting the need for more comprehensive evaluation protocols in future research on lifelong motion-language agents.

AI.tldr: Continual learning for bidirectional motion-language agents using mixture-of-experts with autoencoder routing achieves near-zero forgetting across sequential tasks.
AI.motivation: Autonomous agents in dynamic environments need to continuously learn new motion concepts without catastrophic forgetting of previously acquired skills, addressing the stability-plasticity trade-off in bidirectional motion-language learning.
AI.method: Building on frozen large language model backbone with LoRA variants, using mixture-of-experts architectures with autoencoder-based router for task-specific expert selection at inference without task labels. Evaluated on five-task benchmark from HumanML3D through semantic clustering.
AI.result: Achieved near-zero forgetting across both motion-to-text and text-to-motion directions while maintaining high generation and captioning quality. Hard expert selection via routing significantly outperforms soft expert blending, and token-level accuracy may diverge from downstream generation quality.
AI.conclusion: Preserving expert isolation is critical for maintaining performance in continual learning settings, and more comprehensive evaluation protocols are needed for lifelong motion-language agents due to potential divergence between token-level and generation quality metrics.

title: DreamForge-World 0.1 Preview: A Low-Compute Real-Time Controllable World Model
summary: We present DreamForge-World 0.1 Preview, a preview foundational world model for real-time interactive world simulation. The system adapts the LongLive 1 autoregressive video stack, itself derived from Wan2.1-T2V-1.3B, with a residual action pathway inspired by the Matrix-Game family. DreamForge-World 0.1 Preview focuses on a complementary axis to frontier-scale world simulators: low-compute adaptation, consumer-GPU runtime, and broad interactive capability coverage. It supports live keyboard and mouse control, multimodal initialization, mid-stream reprompting, dual-view operation, and minute-scale interactive rollouts at native 480p resolution, reaching up to 14 to 15 FPS FPS on a single RTX 4090 with a low memory footprint. By leveraging open video backbones and applying targeted adaptation runs, we build the preview system with high cost-efficiency. DF-World 0.1 Preview is not yet a memory-complete or frontier-quality world simulator, but demonstrates a practical low-compute route toward real-time controllable world-model previews on consumer GPUs.

AI.tldr: DreamForge-World 0.1 Preview is a lightweight foundational world model for real-time interactive world simulation that runs efficiently on consumer GPUs with live keyboard/mouse control and multimodal capabilities.
AI.motivation: To create a complementary approach to frontier-scale world simulators by focusing on low-compute adaptation, consumer-GPU runtime efficiency, and broad interactive capability coverage rather than maximum scale or quality.
AI.method: Adapts the LongLive 1 autoregressive video stack (derived from Wan2.1-T2V-1.3B) with a residual action pathway inspired by the Matrix-Game family, leveraging open video backbones and targeted adaptation runs for cost-efficiency.
AI.result: Achieves real-time interactive simulation with live keyboard/mouse control, multimodal initialization, mid-stream reprompting, dual-view operation, and minute-scale rollouts at native 480p resolution, reaching 14-15 FPS on a single RTX 4090 with low memory footprint.
AI.conclusion: While not a memory-complete or frontier-quality world simulator, DreamForge-World 0.1 Preview demonstrates a practical low-compute route toward real-time controllable world-model previews on consumer GPUs, offering high cost-efficiency for interactive applications.

title: Toward an Energy-Optimized Operation of Data Centers Located in Wind Farms Using Reinforcement Learning
summary: This paper studies Reinforcement Learning as an online controller for curtailment-aware workload shifting in wind-turbine-integrated high-performance computing (HPC) data centers. We introduce a reproducible fixed-day simulation framework with synthetic wind and price signals and delayed completion feedback, designed to be extensible toward more complex scenarios. As a controlled benchmarking basis, we then focus on the minimal case with one wind turbine and one co-located data center. In this setting, pure Reinforcement Learning exhibits a pronounced credit-assignment problem and tends to underuse free wind energy early in the day. We therefore evaluate two complementary countermeasures: optimization-based Imitation Learning and potential-based Reward Shaping. Across multi-seed training and a 200-day test set, Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) and a Soft Actor-Critic (SAC) variant with an additional on-policy update routine achieve strong empirical performance among learned policies, and both Imitation Learning and Reward Shaping provide improvements in relevant configurations. A performance gap to the optimizer remains, which is expected: the optimizer plans offline with full-day foresight, whereas Reinforcement Learning must decide online from current observations without future realizations. The benchmark and ablation results provide a transparent basis for extending the approach toward richer multi-site and continuous-time scenarios.

AI.tldr: RL-based online controller for wind-powered HPC data centers that learns to shift workloads while avoiding curtailment, using imitation learning and reward shaping to improve performance.
AI.motivation: To address the challenge of efficiently using renewable energy in HPC data centers by developing an online controller that can shift computational workloads to match wind energy availability while avoiding curtailment (wasted energy).
AI.method: 1) Created reproducible simulation framework with synthetic wind/price signals and delayed feedback. 2) Focused on minimal case (1 wind turbine + 1 data center). 3) Used RL (PPO and SAC) as online controller. 4) Applied two countermeasures: optimization-based Imitation Learning and potential-based Reward Shaping. 5) Compared against offline optimizer with full foresight.
AI.result: PPO and SAC with on-policy updates performed well. Both Imitation Learning and Reward Shaping improved RL performance in relevant configurations. RL still showed a performance gap compared to the offline optimizer (expected due to online vs offline nature). The approach provides a benchmark for more complex scenarios.
AI.conclusion: Reinforcement Learning can effectively control workload shifting in wind-powered HPC data centers, though performance lags behind offline optimization due to lack of foresight. Imitation Learning and Reward Shaping help mitigate RL's credit assignment problems and improve early wind energy utilization, establishing a foundation for more complex multi-site scenarios.

title: Hybrid Active-Online Learning Framework for Label-Efficient Concept Drift Adaptation in Optical Network Failure Detection
comment: Accepted for oral presentation at the European Conference on Optical Communication (ECOC 2026)
summary: We propose a hybrid active-online learning framework for label-efficient concept drift adaptation in optical network failure detection. Using margin-based selective labeling, our method achieves nearceiling accuracy and AUC scores while querying only 3.4% of streaming samples, with negligible latency overhead compared to static inference.

AI.tldr: Hybrid active-online learning framework achieves label-efficient concept drift adaptation for optical network failure detection with only 3.4% labeling requirement while maintaining high accuracy.
AI.motivation: Need for efficient adaptation to concept drift in optical network failure detection while minimizing labeling costs and maintaining real-time performance.
AI.method: Hybrid active-online learning framework with margin-based selective labeling for querying only informative samples from streaming data.
AI.result: Achieves near-ceiling accuracy and AUC scores while querying only 3.4% of streaming samples, with negligible latency overhead compared to static inference.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework enables label-efficient concept drift adaptation in optical network failure detection, balancing high performance with minimal labeling requirements and computational overhead.

title: FlexTab: A Flexible Encoder-Decoder Architecture for In-Context Learning Across Diverse Tabular Tasks
summary: We introduce FlexTab, a flexible encoder-decoder architecture for in-context learning on tabular data that pairs a single, task-agnostic encoder with a suite of task-specific decoders. Unlike existing tabular in-context learners, which entangle feature representations with a specific prediction target, our design produces \textit{target-agnostic} row embeddings that can be leveraged across a wide range of downstream tasks within a table-native in-context learning setup. We demonstrate this flexibility on six distinct problems: classification, regression, anomaly detection, clustering, entity matching, and entity classification in relational databases. Both the encoder and the task-specific decoders are trained on a large corpus of real-world, unlabeled tables. FlexTab achieves state-of-the-art performance on classification, regression, anomaly detection and entity matching, while remaining competitive with specialized models on entity classification in a relational setting. These results demonstrate that a single shared encoder, paired with task-specific decoders, can serve as an effective general-purpose backbone for diverse tabular prediction problems. The inference code and checkpoints will be made publicly available at https://github.com/SAP-samples/flextab.

AI.tldr: FlexTab is a flexible encoder-decoder architecture for in-context learning on tabular data that uses a single task-agnostic encoder with multiple task-specific decoders, achieving state-of-the-art performance across six different tabular tasks.
AI.motivation: Existing tabular in-context learning methods entangle feature representations with specific prediction targets, limiting their flexibility across different tasks. The authors aim to create a more versatile approach that can handle multiple tabular problems without retraining the encoder for each task.
AI.method: FlexTab uses a single, task-agnostic encoder to produce target-agnostic row embeddings from tabular data. These embeddings can then be used by different task-specific decoders for various downstream tasks. The system is trained on a large corpus of real-world, unlabeled tables.
AI.result: FlexTab achieves state-of-the-art performance on classification, regression, anomaly detection, and entity matching tasks. It remains competitive with specialized models on entity classification in relational databases. The system successfully demonstrates flexibility across six distinct tabular problems.
AI.conclusion: A single shared encoder paired with task-specific decoders can serve as an effective general-purpose backbone for diverse tabular prediction problems. The approach enables greater flexibility and performance across multiple tasks without retraining the core feature extractor for each specific problem.

title: DRIFT: Difficulty Routing Self-DIstillation with Rhythm-Gated Exploration and Success BuFfer Training
summary: Enabling large language models to achieve stable self-improvement without external expert supervision remains a central challenge in complex reasoning tasks. Existing self-distillation and reinforcement learning methods lack explicit mechanisms for tracking problem-level learning progress and adapting optimization strategies accordingly. Consequently, training may over-optimize easy problems, receive weak supervision from hard problems, and fail to sufficiently explore borderline cases. To resolve these issues, we propose DRIFT, an online self-evolution policy optimization framework for large language models. DRIFT regulates the model's self-improvement process through the joint use of Difficulty Routing and Rhythm Gating. The former identifies the model's learning state at the problem level and dynamically allocates self-distillation and reinforcement learning signals, while the latter refines policy updates at the token level, concentrating exploration on critical reasoning positions. By further incorporating a success buffer and a two-stage curriculum learning strategy, DRIFT preserves high-quality historical experience while progressively guiding the model from reliable behavior acquisition toward stable policy evolution. Evaluated across five benchmarks and three model scales, DRIFT surpasses the peak performance of both GRPO and SDPO across all evaluated metrics. On the average score over the five benchmarks, DRIFT achieves 79.5$\%$, outperforming GRPO by 9.5$\%$ and SDPO by 7.5$\%$, establishing a new state-of-the-art result. Notably, on ToolUse, DRIFT reaches an accuracy of 79.2$\%$, improving over GRPO by 13.5$\%$ and SDPO by 10.7$\%$, setting a new state-of-the-art and substantially outperforming all concurrent methods.

AI.tldr: DRIFT is a self-evolution framework for LLMs that uses difficulty routing and rhythm gating to achieve stable self-improvement without external supervision.
